profile.domain: Food Service Technology / Digital Menu Management

project_goal.value: CLI tool for food truck operators and small restaurant owners to push menu item updates, price changes, and 86'd item flags to cloud-connected digital display boards from the command line
project_goal.rationale: Core operator workflow to update screens without a GUI or browser

target_users.value: Food truck operators and small restaurant owners managing 1-10 digital display boards
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in product discovery

project_class.rationale: Command-line interface with no browser UI required by operators

capabilities.rationale: API backend and display board integration; no frontend needed for CLI

complexity_level.rationale: Focused CRUD operations with external display API calls; limited state

risk_level.rationale: Internal operational data only; no financial or personal data exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Menu pricing and item data is business-internal but not regulated

external_exposure.rationale: API endpoints require JWT authentication; not publicly accessible

access_model.value: JWT-based CLI authentication with per-operator credential files stored locally
access_model.rationale: Operators authenticate once and store token; no multi-user RBAC needed at MVP

feature_scope.value: Push menu item availability (86'd/available), update item prices, manage display zones, configure scheduled availability windows, list board status
feature_scope.rationale: Full operator workflow for daily menu board management

mvp_scope.value: 86 and restore menu items, update prices, list boards and current item states
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable commands for daily food truck operations

security_baseline.value: JWT tokens with 24-hour expiry, HTTPS-only API calls, credentials stored in OS keychain or encrypted local config file
security_baseline.rationale: Sufficient for low-risk internal tool with authenticated API access

frontend_stack.value: N/A - CLI only, no frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI tool requires no browser UI

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 with TypeScript, commands implemented via Commander.js
backend_stack.rationale: TypeScript for type safety, Commander.js for CLI arg parsing

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for menu item and board configuration state on DigitalOcean Managed Databases
data_platform.rationale: Relational store for item/board relationships and scheduling rules

hosting_target.value: DigitalOcean App Platform for the API backend; CLI distributed as npm package
hosting_target.rationale: Simple managed hosting for API; npm for operator CLI installation

privacy_retention_policy.value: Menu operation logs retained 90 days for audit; board configuration history retained indefinitely
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Operational logs needed for troubleshooting; config history for rollback

future_scope.value: Web dashboard for operators, multi-location chain support, image upload for menu items
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 expansion after CLI validation

constraints.value: Must work on macOS and Linux terminals; CLI install via npm; API must respond within 500ms for instant operator feedback
constraints.rationale: Operator environment requirements for food truck use

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on the backend API, structured JSON logs, CLI error reporting with request IDs for support
observability_baseline.rationale: Sufficient observability for a simple API backend

execution_preference.value: Iterative development with weekly releases via npm publish CI pipeline
execution_preference.rationale: Rapid iteration cycle for operator feedback

project.goal: CLI tool to manage digital menu board displays from the command line
project.target_users: Food truck operators and small restaurant owners

backend.framework: TypeScript with Commander.js for CLI commands
backend.api: REST API on DigitalOcean App Platform

security.auth: JWT with 24-hour expiry, OS keychain credential storage
security.transport: HTTPS only for all API calls

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with npm publish on tag
devops.hosting: DigitalOcean App Platform for API, npm registry for CLI

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP scope (86 items, restore items, update prices, list boards) maps cleanly to four commands an operator would use before and during a service shift. The scheduled availability window feature is valuable for food trucks with breakfast/lunch menu transitions but can be Phase 2 without blocking daily operations.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Commander.js on Node.js 22 is appropriate for a typed CLI at this complexity level. The API backend on App Platform keeps infrastructure simple. Display board push notifications should use a webhook or long-poll pattern rather than requiring display hardware to poll at short intervals, to avoid unnecessary load at scale.
reasoner_reviews.security: JWT stored in OS keychain via keytar is the right pattern here — never flat-file plaintext credentials. API endpoints should validate the operator's board ownership on every request to prevent cross-operator data access. Rate limiting on the 86 endpoint prevents accidental or malicious bulk status floods.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: DigitalOcean App Platform handles auto-scaling adequately for this traffic profile. The 500ms p99 target is achievable with direct PostgreSQL queries and no joins heavier than board-to-item lookups. npm distribution is standard and sufficient for the target operator demographic.

backend.service_design: Single Express-based REST API deployed on App Platform, exposing endpoints consumed by the CLI. Domain modules: menu-items (CRUD + availability toggle), boards (list, status, zone management), schedules (availability window CRUD), auth (JWT issue and refresh). CLI is a standalone npm package that wraps HTTP calls to this API.
backend.api_patterns: REST with versioned endpoints at /api/v1. CLI commands map 1:1 to API calls: menu:eighty-six -> PATCH /api/v1/items/:id/availability, menu:price -> PATCH /api/v1/items/:id/price, board:list -> GET /api/v1/boards, schedule:set -> POST /api/v1/schedules.
backend.failure_handling: CLI prints human-readable error messages with request IDs for support escalation. API returns structured error JSON with code and message fields. Retry with exponential backoff on 5xx responses (max 3 attempts). Offline detection prints actionable message rather than raw connection error.

security.auth_design: JWT issued by /auth/login endpoint, stored in OS keychain via keytar npm package. Tokens expire after 24 hours. Refresh endpoint extends sessions for operators on long shifts without re-login.
security.authorization_model: Per-operator board ownership enforced at API layer — each board belongs to an operator_id foreign key; all board/item mutations verify ownership before executing.
security.secrets_management: API database credentials and JWT signing secret stored in DigitalOcean App Platform environment variable vault, never in source code or build artifacts.

data.entities: display_boards (id, operator_id FK, name, location_label, status ENUM online/offline/error, last_seen_at). menu_items (id, board_id FK, name, price_cents, category, availability ENUM available/eighty_six, updated_at). display_zones (id, board_id FK, zone_name, zone_type ENUM main/specials/drinks). availability_schedules (id, item_id FK, zone_id FK, start_time TIME, end_time TIME, days_of_week INT[]).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on DigitalOcean Managed Database with connection pooling via pgBouncer. item_updates table as append-only audit log for all price and availability changes.
data.retention_and_deletion: item_updates audit log purged after 90 days via scheduled pg_cron job. Board and item records retained indefinitely unless operator explicitly deletes.

devops.deployment_topology: DigitalOcean App Platform running the Node.js API as a single service with 2 replicas. Managed PostgreSQL database on same DO datacenter. CLI published to npm registry.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on push to main, run tsc typecheck + Jest unit tests, build Docker image for API, deploy to App Platform via doctl. On git tag matching v*, publish CLI npm package via npm publish.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM agent on App Platform. Structured JSON logs with operator_id and board_id on every request. Custom metric: item_availability_changes_per_hour by board.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a simple CLI tool for food truck operators with a companion REST API backend. Key decisions: Commander.js for typed command definitions with auto-generated help, JWT in OS keychain for secure credential storage without asking operators to manage files, PostgreSQL for board/item state with an append-only item_updates table for audit history. Keeping the API surface minimal — four core commands map to four API endpoints — so the MVP can ship in 8 weeks and be validated with operators before adding scheduling and web UI.
target_output.title: Digital Menu Board CLI Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Node.js 22 TypeScript CLI tool (menuboard-cli) for food truck operators and small restaurant owners to manage cloud-connected digital display boards from the command line. Operators run commands to 86 items, update prices, manage display zones, and configure scheduled availability windows. A companion REST API on DigitalOcean App Platform persists board state in PostgreSQL and pushes changes to display hardware. The CLI is distributed via npm and works on macOS and Linux terminals with JWT-based authentication stored securely in the OS keychain.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-component architecture: a Node.js 22 CLI package (menuboard-cli on npm) and a companion REST API backend on DigitalOcean App Platform. The CLI wraps authenticated HTTPS calls to the API; no logic executes locally beyond argument parsing, output formatting, and credential retrieval from the OS keychain. The API backend manages all persistent state in a PostgreSQL 16 managed database — menu items, board configurations, display zones, and availability schedules. Display boards poll the API on a configurable TTL (default 10 seconds) to refresh their displayed content, ensuring 86 status changes are visible on screens within one poll cycle. Board online/offline status is tracked via last_seen_at heartbeat timestamps.
target_output.technology_stack: CLI: Node.js 22, TypeScript 5.4, Commander.js for command/flag parsing, keytar for OS keychain credential storage, chalk for terminal output formatting, ora for progress spinners. API: Node.js 22, TypeScript 5.4, Express 4, pg driver with pgBouncer connection pooling. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on DigitalOcean Managed Databases. Infrastructure: DigitalOcean App Platform (2 replicas), GitHub Actions CI/CD. Observability: Datadog APM, structured JSON logging. Distribution: npm package registry.
target_output.functional_feature_map: menuboard item:eighty-six <item-id|name> — marks item as sold out, pushes to all configured boards immediately. menuboard item:restore <item-id|name> — marks item as available again. menuboard item:price <item-id> --price <cents> — updates displayed price in cents to avoid floating-point issues. menuboard board:list — shows all operator boards with online/offline status and item counts. menuboard board:status <board-id> — displays current zone layout and all item availability states. menuboard schedule:set <item-id> --start 07:00 --end 11:00 --days mon,tue,wed,thu,fri — configures automatic show/hide windows. menuboard auth:login --email — initiates authentication and stores JWT in OS keychain.
target_output.system_components: menuboard-cli npm package: Commander.js command tree, HTTP client with retry logic, keytar credential vault integration, chalk-formatted tabular output for board status. REST API: Express router with versioned /api/v1 prefix, JWT middleware for auth, operator ownership validation middleware, menu-items module, boards module, schedules module. PostgreSQL 16: display_boards, menu_items, display_zones, availability_schedules, item_updates (audit log). DigitalOcean App Platform: 2 API replicas with health check on /health, App Platform Managed DB connection, Datadog APM agent sidecar.
target_output.workflows: Item 86 Workflow: Operator runs 'menuboard item:eighty-six tacos'. CLI retrieves JWT from keychain, calls PATCH /api/v1/items/tacos/availability with body {availability: 'eighty_six'}. API validates operator owns the board containing the item, updates menu_items.availability, inserts record to item_updates audit log, returns updated item. CLI prints 'Tacos marked as 86d on Truck Board 1'. Board polls API within 10 seconds and updates display. Price Update Workflow: Operator runs 'menuboard item:price breakfast-burrito --price 1295'. CLI calls PATCH /api/v1/items/breakfast-burrito/price. API validates ownership, updates price_cents field, logs change to item_updates with previous_price_cents for rollback reference. CLI confirms with formatted output showing old and new price.
target_output.data_model: display_boards (id UUID PK, operator_id UUID FK, name VARCHAR, location_label VARCHAR, status ENUM online/offline/error, last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). menu_items (id UUID PK, board_id UUID FK, zone_id UUID FK nullable, name VARCHAR, price_cents INT, category VARCHAR, availability ENUM available/eighty_six, display_order INT, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). display_zones (id UUID PK, board_id UUID FK, zone_name VARCHAR, zone_type ENUM main/specials/drinks, position INT). availability_schedules (id UUID PK, item_id UUID FK, start_time TIME, end_time TIME, days_of_week SMALLINT, active BOOLEAN). item_updates (id UUID PK, item_id UUID FK, operator_id UUID FK, change_type ENUM price/availability/schedule, previous_value JSONB, new_value JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: REST API at /api/v1. Authentication: POST /auth/login (issue JWT), POST /auth/refresh. Items: PATCH /api/v1/items/:id/availability {availability: 'eighty_six'|'available'}, PATCH /api/v1/items/:id/price {price_cents: INT}, GET /api/v1/items?board_id=:id. Boards: GET /api/v1/boards (list operator boards with status), GET /api/v1/boards/:id/status (full zone and item state). Schedules: POST /api/v1/schedules {item_id, start_time, end_time, days_of_week}, DELETE /api/v1/schedules/:id. All responses: {data, meta} envelope. Errors: {error: {code, message, request_id}}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Authentication: JWT HS256 signed with 512-bit secret, 24-hour expiry. CLI stores token via keytar in OS keychain (macOS Keychain, Linux Secret Service, Windows Credential Manager) — never written to disk as plaintext. Operator ownership enforcement: every board/item mutation runs SELECT board_id FROM display_boards WHERE operator_id = $jwt_sub before executing; returns 403 if mismatch. Transport: HTTPS only; HTTP connections refused. Rate limiting: 60 requests/minute per JWT on all mutation endpoints via express-rate-limit. Input validation: Joi schemas on all request bodies to prevent injection. API deployed with Helmet.js security headers.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: DigitalOcean App Platform: 2 replicas of the Node.js API, auto-deploy on push to main via GitHub integration. Managed PostgreSQL 16 on same DO datacenter (NYC3) with automatic daily backups and point-in-time recovery. pgBouncer connection pooling at 20 connections per replica. Health check endpoint GET /health returns DB ping status. GitHub Actions pipeline: on push to main — tsc --noEmit type check, Jest unit tests (70% coverage gate), Docker build, App Platform deploy via doctl CLI. On git tag v* — run full test suite, npm publish --access public.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM on API with trace sampling at 100% for write operations (item availability/price changes). Structured JSON logs on every request: operator_id, board_id, command, duration_ms, status_code, request_id. Custom Datadog metrics: menuboard.item_eighty_six_count (counter by board), menuboard.price_updates_per_hour, menuboard.board_offline_duration_minutes. Alert: board offline > 30 minutes triggers Datadog monitor notification to operator email.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: DigitalOcean App Platform Basic plan (~$24/month for 2 replicas) and Managed PostgreSQL Basic plan (~$15/month) totals approximately $40/month for the backend. npm package hosting is free. At 1000 daily CLI commands across all operators, PostgreSQL handles load comfortably on the smallest instance class. Scaling path: upgrade to DO App Platform Professional tier and add read replicas when operator count exceeds 500.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): Core four commands (item:eighty-six, item:restore, item:price, board:list), JWT auth with keychain storage, PostgreSQL schema with item_updates audit log, REST API on App Platform, GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline, npm package published. Phase 2 (3 weeks): menuboard board:status with zone breakdown, schedule:set for availability windows, board heartbeat and online/offline tracking, Datadog APM integration. Phase 3 (future): Web dashboard UI for operators preferring GUI, multi-location chain support with board grouping, image upload for menu items, webhook push to boards replacing polling.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode throughout CLI and API. Commander.js command files colocated with their HTTP client calls — one file per command group (items.ts, boards.ts, schedules.ts). API route handlers thin — business logic in service modules, not Express handlers. Jest unit tests for all service module functions with PostgreSQL queries mocked via pg-mock. Integration tests against a real Postgres test container in CI. Semantic versioning for CLI; breaking flag changes require major version bump. CLI --help output tested as part of CI to prevent accidental interface regressions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Board polling interval creates up to 10-second delay between CLI 86 command and screen update. Mitigation: document this to operators; Phase 2 can add WebSocket push for instant updates if delay is unacceptable. Risk: keytar dependency requires native OS keychain access, which can fail in some CI or container environments. Mitigation: fallback to encrypted local config file with user warning. Tradeoff: Commander.js over Ink/React-based TUI — simpler implementation and faster startup time at the cost of no interactive prompts in MVP. Tradeoff: PostgreSQL over Redis for item state — durability and relational queries justify the cost over a cache-only approach.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Commander.js over yargs for better TypeScript ergonomics and built-in help generation. Chose npm distribution over Homebrew tap for faster release iteration. Selected OS keychain via keytar over environment variable credentials to avoid operators accidentally committing secrets to dotfiles.

metadata.notes: Greenfield CLI tool for food truck digital menu board management; clean two-component architecture with npm CLI and DO App Platform API.

project_goal.value: Real-time POS transaction ingestion pipeline normalizing sales data from 50+ restaurant locations across multiple POS vendors, feeding live ops dashboards and inventory depletion alerts with sub-minute latency
project_goal.rationale: Core operational need for multi-location visibility

target_users.value: Operations teams and district managers at fast-casual restaurant chains needing live revenue and waste visibility across 50+ locations
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers of pipeline output dashboards

project_class.rationale: Pure data ingestion, normalization, and streaming architecture with no user-facing UI

capabilities.rationale: Requires real-time stream processing, batch reconciliation, POS vendor integrations, and analytics output

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-vendor normalization with stateful stream processing and sub-minute SLA at 50+ location scale

risk_level.rationale: Handles financial transaction metadata but no card PAN data; internal pipeline only

data_sensitivity.rationale: POS transaction data includes revenue figures and card transaction metadata subject to PCI-DSS scope assessment

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline is internal infrastructure; dashboards served to authenticated internal users only

access_model.value: Custom JWT for internal dashboard API; service-to-service mTLS between pipeline components
access_model.rationale: No external consumers; internal auth sufficient with service mesh security

feature_scope.value: Kafka-based POS event ingestion, multi-vendor schema normalization, ClickHouse aggregation, inventory depletion alert engine, live ops dashboard feeds, nightly reconciliation batch jobs
feature_scope.rationale: Full scope for v1 operational pipeline

mvp_scope.value: Single POS vendor integration (Toast), real-time revenue aggregation per location, inventory alert for top 20 menu items, Grafana dashboard for ops team
mvp_scope.rationale: Fastest path to demonstrable value with one vendor before expanding integrations

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 on all Kafka connections, mTLS between Flink and ClickHouse, secrets in AWS Secrets Manager, no raw card data stored
security_baseline.rationale: PCI-DSS scope reduction by excluding PAN; transport security for financial data

frontend_stack.value: None — pipeline only; dashboards served via Grafana
frontend_stack.rationale: No custom frontend required; Grafana covers ops dashboard needs

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Flink (PyFlink) for stream processing; Go 1.22 for vendor adapter microservices
backend_stack.rationale: PyFlink for stateful aggregation; Go for low-latency vendor HTTP polling adapters

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for OLAP aggregations; Kafka MSK for event streaming; Redis 7 for alert state and deduplication
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse sub-second query performance for ops dashboards; Kafka for durable multi-consumer event distribution

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate for Flink and adapter containers; MSK for Kafka; ElastiCache Redis; self-managed ClickHouse on EC2 r6i instances
hosting_target.rationale: Managed Kafka reduces ops burden; ClickHouse requires EC2 for performance tuning

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw POS events retained 90 days in Kafka and S3; ClickHouse aggregates retained 3 years; PII fields (card last-4) masked within 24 hours of ingestion
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial reporting requires multi-year aggregates; raw event TTL limits PCI scope

future_scope.value: Additional POS vendor integrations (Square, NCR Aloha, Lightspeed); predictive demand forecasting ML models consuming pipeline output; mobile alert app for district managers
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 expansion after Toast integration is proven

constraints.value: Sub-minute end-to-end latency SLA; budget under $8,000/month AWS spend; no raw card PAN data may be stored or transmitted; pipeline must handle 10,000 transactions/minute peak
constraints.rationale: Operational SLA and PCI scope reduction requirements

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for pipeline metrics; custom dashboards for lag, throughput, error rates; PagerDuty for alert engine failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing ops monitoring stack; Grafana native to ClickHouse datasource

execution_preference.value: Trunk-based development with feature flags for vendor rollout; 2-week sprint cycles
execution_preference.rationale: Allows incremental vendor integration without branching complexity

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS SAQ-A scoping: exclude PAN storage, mask card last-4 within 24 hours, quarterly ASV scans on network perimeter, internal data governance policies for financial reporting integrity
compliance_context.rationale: Card transaction metadata in pipeline requires PCI-DSS scope assessment and controls

project.goal: Ingest and normalize POS transactions from 50+ locations in real-time with sub-minute latency
project.target_users: Operations teams and district managers
project.timeline: Q2 MVP with single vendor, Q4 multi-vendor

backend.framework: PyFlink for stream processing, custom Go adapters for POS vendor APIs
backend.api: Internal Kafka topic producers; REST API for alert config

security.auth: Custom JWT for dashboard API, mTLS for service mesh
security.transport: TLS 1.3 on Kafka, mTLS between Flink and ClickHouse
security.compliance: PCI-DSS scope reduction via PAN exclusion

data.db: ClickHouse on EC2 r6i for OLAP

devops.hosting: ECS Fargate for Flink jobs and adapters
devops.environments: dev, staging, production

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP scoping to Toast-only with top-20 inventory items is strategically sound — it limits integration surface while delivering the key ops visibility use case. The alert engine should be configurable per-location since par thresholds differ by store volume.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: PyFlink on Fargate is viable but stateful checkpointing to S3 requires careful tuning of checkpoint intervals against the sub-minute SLA. Recommend testing Flink savepoint overhead under peak 10K tx/min load before committing to the topology. Consider separating normalization jobs from aggregation jobs for independent scaling.
reasoner_reviews.security: Card last-4 masking must happen in the normalization layer before events reach ClickHouse — do not rely on post-storage masking. The pipeline is effectively out-of-scope for PCI-DSS SAQ-D by excluding PAN, but the masking SLA of 24 hours should be tightened to at-ingestion to avoid any cardholder data window.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The $8,000/month budget is achievable but tight. ClickHouse on r6i.2xlarge runs ~$1,200/month. MSK 3-broker cluster adds ~$900/month. Fargate for Flink adds variable cost depending on parallelism settings. Budget headroom is thin for production incidents requiring vertical scaling.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The previous plan (Round 1) did not specify a dead-letter queue strategy for malformed POS events from non-standard vendor payloads. Without DLQ handling, a single malformed event batch could stall a Kafka partition and cause location-level data gaps in dashboards.

backend.service_design: Three ECS Fargate services: (1) VendorAdapterService (Go) — one container per POS vendor, polls vendor webhooks or APIs and publishes raw events to vendor-specific Kafka topics; (2) NormalizationJob (PyFlink) — consumes raw vendor topics, applies vendor-specific schema mappers, emits canonical pos_transactions events to normalized topic; (3) AggregationJob (PyFlink) — stateful tumbling-window jobs computing revenue_snapshots and triggering inventory_depletion alerts
backend.api_patterns: REST API (FastAPI) for alert configuration: POST /alerts/thresholds, GET /alerts/thresholds/{location_id}, GET /alerts/history. Kafka topics: raw.pos.toast, raw.pos.square (future), normalized.pos.transactions, aggregated.revenue.snapshots, alerts.inventory.depletion
backend.failure_handling: Dead-letter topics (dlq.pos.toast, dlq.normalization) for malformed events; Flink exactly-once semantics via Kafka transactions; Redis idempotency keys on VendorAdapter for duplicate event detection; circuit breaker on vendor API polling with 30-second backoff
backend.background_jobs: Nightly reconciliation job (Python): compares ClickHouse daily aggregates against vendor settlement reports and writes variance records to reconciliation_runs table; S3 archival job for raw event compaction after 7-day window
backend.scaling_notes: Flink NormalizationJob scales by Kafka partition count (64 partitions = 64 task slots max); AggregationJob uses keyed streams by location_id enabling linear horizontal scaling; VendorAdapter scales by vendor count, not load

security.auth_design: Custom JWT (RS256) issued by internal auth service for dashboard API consumers; 1-hour expiry with refresh token rotation; mTLS certificates via AWS ACM Private CA for service-to-service Flink-ClickHouse and Flink-Kafka connections
security.secrets_management: AWS Secrets Manager for vendor API credentials (Toast API key, webhook secrets); automatic 90-day rotation; ECS task roles with least-privilege IAM policies for MSK and S3 access
security.privacy_controls: Card last-4 digits masked to 'XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-####' at VendorAdapter ingestion point before Kafka publish; masking applied in NormalizationMapper, not deferred; ClickHouse stores masked values only; S3 raw archives encrypted with SSE-KMS
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail for all AWS API actions; structured JSON logs from all Flink jobs including record counts, error rates, and checkpoint durations; alert engine logs all threshold evaluations with location_id, item_id, and trigger condition

data.entities: pos_transactions (event_id, location_id, register_id, transaction_ts, items_json, subtotal_cents, tax_cents, tender_type ENUM cash/card/mobile_pay, card_last4_masked, vendor ENUM toast/square/ncr_aloha), location_registers (register_id, location_id, vendor, last_seen_at, firmware_version), menu_items (item_id, sku, name, category, current_price_cents, is_tracked_inventory BOOL), inventory_depletions (alert_id, location_id, item_id, shift_period_id, units_sold, par_threshold, triggered_at), revenue_snapshots (snapshot_id, location_id, window_start, window_end, gross_revenue_cents, transaction_count, avg_ticket_cents), shift_periods (period_id, location_id, shift_type ENUM breakfast/lunch/dinner/late, start_ts, end_ts)
data.storage_design: ClickHouse: MergeTree engine on pos_transactions partitioned by toYYYYMM(transaction_ts), ORDER BY (location_id, transaction_ts); AggregatingMergeTree for revenue_snapshots with materialized views; Kafka MSK 64-partition normalized.pos.transactions topic with 7-day retention; S3 Iceberg table for raw event archival beyond 7 days; Redis sorted sets for per-location per-item running sales counters
data.retention_and_deletion: Raw Kafka events: 7-day topic retention, then S3 Iceberg archival; S3 archives compressed Parquet, retained 90 days then expired; ClickHouse OLAP data: 3-year retention with TTL expression; card last-4 masked at ingestion, no unmasked data persisted anywhere in pipeline
data.analytics_events: Revenue by location per 5-minute window, transaction volume per hour per register, inventory depletion events per shift, daily revenue vs prior-week comparison, vendor feed health (events received per location per hour)

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster with 3 services (VendorAdapter, NormalizationJob, AggregationJob); MSK 3-broker kafka.m5.large cluster in private VPC subnets; ClickHouse on 2x EC2 r6i.2xlarge (primary + replica) in same VPC; ElastiCache Redis 7 cache.r6g.large; Grafana on t3.medium EC2
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint (ruff for Python, golangci-lint for Go), unit tests, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy via task definition update; Flink job deployment via S3 JAR upload and ECS task restart; ClickHouse schema migrations via Flyway-compatible migration scripts run as ECS one-off tasks
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping Flink JMX metrics (consumer lag, checkpoint duration, records processed/sec) and Go adapter metrics; Grafana dashboards: pipeline lag heatmap by location, DLQ depth by vendor, alert engine trigger rate; PagerDuty for consumer lag >60s or DLQ depth >100
devops.rollback_strategy: Flink savepoints before each deployment; rollback by reverting ECS task definition and restoring prior Flink savepoint from S3; VendorAdapter rollback via ECR image tag revert; ClickHouse migrations reversible via down-migration scripts

ISS-001.title: No dead-letter queue strategy for malformed POS events
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan has no handling for malformed events from non-standard vendor payloads. A stalled Kafka partition causes location-level dashboard gaps and no recovery path was defined.

history.note: Identified by critic review: missing DLQ strategy

ISS-002.title: Card last-4 masking deferred to post-storage rather than at ingestion
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan described masking as a post-processing step, leaving a window where unmasked card metadata exists in ClickHouse. This conflicts with PCI-DSS scope reduction intent.

history.note: Identified by security review

focus_issues.title: No dead-letter queue strategy for malformed POS events
focus_issues.detail: Missing DLQ handling risks stalling Kafka partitions and causing location-level data gaps.

focus_issues.title: Card last-4 masking deferred to post-storage
focus_issues.detail: Unmasked card metadata window in ClickHouse violates PCI scope reduction intent.

latest_recommendations: Add dead-letter topics for each vendor source and normalization stage with a DLQ consumer that logs, alerts, and allows replay
Move card last-4 masking to VendorAdapter ingestion point before any Kafka publish, not as a ClickHouse post-processing step

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity issues unresolved from round 1

previous_audits.summary: Solid streaming architecture with appropriate tech stack choices, but two high-severity gaps in DLQ handling and PCI scope controls undermine production readiness.
strengths: ClickHouse + Flink combination well-suited to sub-minute aggregation at this scale
Vendor adapter separation allows incremental rollout
Checkpoint strategy and Kafka exactly-once semantics correctly referenced
concerns: No dead-letter queue defined for any stage of the pipeline
Card data masking described as post-storage operation leaving a PCI compliance gap

Unmasked card last-4 in ClickHouse raw ingest contradicts stated PCI-DSS scope reduction

Implement masking in VendorAdapter at publish time, before Kafka write

previous_plan.title: Multi-Location POS Streaming Pipeline v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Real-time POS transaction pipeline ingesting from 50+ restaurant locations via Kafka MSK, normalizing across Toast and future vendor formats with PyFlink, and aggregating into ClickHouse for sub-minute Grafana dashboards and inventory alerts.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Kafka MSK as central event backbone with Go vendor adapters producing raw events, PyFlink normalization and aggregation jobs consuming and writing to ClickHouse, Redis for alert state deduplication.
previous_plan.data_model: pos_transactions, menu_items, inventory_depletions, revenue_snapshots tables in ClickHouse. Masking of card data handled as a ClickHouse materialized view transformation.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: TLS 1.3 on Kafka, mTLS for service mesh. Card last-4 masked via ClickHouse view. PCI-DSS SAQ-A scoping claimed but masking deferred to post-storage.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision addressing two high-severity issues from the auditor: DLQ absence and card masking timing. The DLQ fix requires defining explicit dead-letter topics per vendor and normalization stage with a DLQ consumer service. The card masking fix moves the operation to the VendorAdapter Go service at the point of Kafka publish, before any data hits the normalized topic. Both fixes must be reflected in system_components, workflows, data_model, and security_and_compliance sections to show coherent resolution across the plan.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined dead-letter topics (dlq.pos.toast, dlq.normalization) with a dedicated DLQ Consumer service that logs malformed events to S3, emits Prometheus metric dlq_events_total, and triggers PagerDuty alert when DLQ depth exceeds 50. Added DLQ topics to Kafka topology and DLQ Consumer to system_components. Updated failure_handling in workflows section.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Malformed POS events are isolated to DLQ topics without stalling normalization partition consumers; ops team has replay capability via S3 archive

fix_report.action_taken: Moved card last-4 masking to VendorAdapterService at Kafka publish time. NormalizationMapper now receives pre-masked events. ClickHouse tables only ever receive masked card_last4_masked field. Updated data_model, security_and_compliance, and workflows sections to reflect masking at ingestion boundary.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No unmasked card metadata exists in Kafka topics, ClickHouse, or S3 archives at any point in pipeline lifecycle; PCI-DSS scope reduction claim is defensible

target_output.title: Multi-Location POS Streaming Pipeline v2
target_output.executive_summary: A real-time data pipeline on AWS processing 10,000 POS transactions per minute from 50+ fast-casual restaurant locations. Go vendor adapters ingest Toast (and future Square, NCR Aloha) events into Kafka MSK, applying card-data masking at publish time. PyFlink normalization and aggregation jobs produce sub-minute revenue snapshots and inventory depletion alerts into ClickHouse, served via Grafana ops dashboards. Dead-letter queues isolate malformed vendor payloads without stalling location data streams.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven pipeline with three processing layers on AWS ECS Fargate. Layer 1: Go VendorAdapterService instances per POS vendor poll vendor APIs and produce to raw Kafka topics (raw.pos.toast), applying card last-4 masking before publish. Layer 2: PyFlink NormalizationJob consumes raw topics and emits canonical pos_transaction events to normalized.pos.transactions (64 partitions). Layer 3: PyFlink AggregationJob runs 1-minute tumbling windows keyed by location_id computing revenue_snapshots and evaluating inventory par thresholds to emit inventory_depletion alerts. ClickHouse on EC2 r6i.2xlarge stores OLAP data with MergeTree partitioning by month. Redis 7 provides alert deduplication state. Malformed events at any stage route to dead-letter topics consumed by DLQConsumerService for S3 archival and alerting.
target_output.technology_stack: Stream Processor: Apache Flink 1.18 (PyFlink) on ECS Fargate. Vendor Adapters: Go 1.22 with Kafka-go client. Message Broker: Kafka MSK 3-broker kafka.m5.large, 64 partitions. OLAP: ClickHouse 24.x on 2x EC2 r6i.2xlarge. Alert State: ElastiCache Redis 7 cache.r6g.large. Dashboards: Grafana 10 with ClickHouse and Prometheus datasources. CI/CD: GitHub Actions + ECR + ECS rolling deploy. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager.
target_output.functional_feature_map: POS Vendor Ingestion: Toast webhook receiver and API poller, with DLQ fallback for malformed payloads. Schema Normalization: vendor-specific mappers (ToastMapper, future SquareMapper) producing canonical pos_transaction events. Real-Time Aggregation: 1-minute tumbling revenue windows per location, running sales counters per menu item per shift. Inventory Alert Engine: threshold evaluation against par levels triggering Kafka alert events and Slack/Grafana annotations. Nightly Reconciliation: batch comparison of ClickHouse aggregates vs vendor settlement files writing variance records. DLQ Management: malformed event capture, S3 archival, replay tooling.
target_output.system_components: VendorAdapterService (Go ECS Fargate): one task per vendor, polls Toast API every 5 seconds, masks card last-4 at publish, produces to raw.pos.toast topic. NormalizationJob (PyFlink ECS): consumes raw topics, applies vendor mapper, emits to normalized.pos.transactions, routes unparseable events to dlq.normalization. AggregationJob (PyFlink ECS): stateful keyed streams by location_id with 1-minute tumbling windows, writes revenue_snapshots to ClickHouse, emits inventory_depletion events to alerts.inventory.depletion topic. DLQConsumerService (Go ECS): consumes dlq.pos.toast and dlq.normalization, writes events to S3 dlq-archive bucket with metadata, emits dlq_events_total Prometheus metric, triggers PagerDuty when depth >50. ClickHouse Cluster: 2x r6i.2xlarge primary+replica, MergeTree pos_transactions and AggregatingMergeTree revenue_snapshots. Kafka MSK: 3-broker kafka.m5.large, 64 partitions on normalized topic. ElastiCache Redis 7: sorted sets for per-location per-item running counters. Grafana: ops dashboards with ClickHouse and Prometheus datasources.
target_output.workflows: POS Transaction Ingestion and Normalization: VendorAdapterService receives Toast webhook POST /webhooks/toast or polls GET /orders?since={last_cursor}, extracts transaction fields, masks card last-4 to 'XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-####' in memory before Kafka produce, publishes raw.pos.toast message with location_id, register_id, transaction_ts, items_json, subtotal_cents, tender_type, card_last4_masked. If payload fails schema validation, publishes to dlq.pos.toast instead and logs reason. NormalizationJob ToastMapper transforms raw event to canonical pos_transaction schema and produces to normalized.pos.transactions. Malformed canonical events route to dlq.normalization. Inventory Depletion Alert Flow: AggregationJob maintains Redis sorted sets keyed as 'sales:{location_id}:{item_id}:{shift_period_id}' with ZINCRBY on each transaction. On each window close, evaluates ZSCORE against par threshold from menu_items.par_threshold field. When units_sold >= par_threshold, produces inventory_depletion event to alerts.inventory.depletion Kafka topic, which Grafana annotation webhook and Slack adapter consume. Alert deduplication via Redis SET 'alert:dedup:{location_id}:{item_id}:{shift_period_id}' with 1-hour TTL.
target_output.data_model: pos_transactions (event_id UUID, location_id VARCHAR, register_id VARCHAR, transaction_ts DateTime, items_json String, subtotal_cents Int64, tax_cents Int64, tender_type Enum('cash','card','mobile_pay'), card_last4_masked VARCHAR, vendor Enum('toast','square','ncr_aloha'), ingested_at DateTime) ENGINE=MergeTree PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(transaction_ts) ORDER BY (location_id, transaction_ts). revenue_snapshots (snapshot_id UUID, location_id VARCHAR, window_start DateTime, window_end DateTime, gross_revenue_cents Int64, transaction_count UInt32, avg_ticket_cents Int64) ENGINE=AggregatingMergeTree ORDER BY (location_id, window_start). menu_items (item_id VARCHAR, sku VARCHAR, name VARCHAR, category VARCHAR, current_price_cents Int32, par_threshold_per_shift UInt16, is_tracked_inventory Bool) ENGINE=ReplacingMergeTree. inventory_depletions (alert_id UUID, location_id VARCHAR, item_id VARCHAR, shift_period_id VARCHAR, units_sold UInt32, par_threshold UInt16, triggered_at DateTime) ENGINE=MergeTree. location_registers (register_id VARCHAR, location_id VARCHAR, vendor VARCHAR, last_seen_at DateTime, status Enum('active','offline')). shift_periods (period_id VARCHAR, location_id VARCHAR, shift_type Enum('breakfast','lunch','dinner','late'), start_ts DateTime, end_ts DateTime). vendor_feeds (feed_id UUID, vendor VARCHAR, location_id VARCHAR, last_successful_poll DateTime, events_received_1h UInt32, health_status Enum('healthy','degraded','offline')). reconciliation_runs (run_id UUID, run_date Date, location_id VARCHAR, ch_gross_revenue_cents Int64, vendor_settlement_cents Int64, variance_cents Int64, variance_pct Decimal32(4)).
target_output.api_design: Alert Configuration REST API (FastAPI): POST /v1/alerts/thresholds — body {location_id, item_id, par_threshold, shift_type}, JWT auth required, writes to menu_items ClickHouse table. GET /v1/alerts/thresholds/{location_id} — returns all par configurations for location. GET /v1/alerts/history?location_id=&start=&end= — queries inventory_depletions with time range. GET /v1/pipeline/health — returns per-vendor feed health from vendor_feeds table. GET /v1/dlq/summary — DLQ depth per topic from Prometheus metrics API. POST /v1/dlq/replay/{topic}/{event_id} — triggers reprocessing of S3-archived DLQ event.
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS scope reduction: card last-4 masked to 'XXXX-XXXX-XXXX-####' by VendorAdapterService in Go memory before Kafka produce — no unmasked card data exists in any Kafka topic, ClickHouse table, S3 archive, or log. ClickHouse stores only masked field card_last4_masked VARCHAR. Transport: TLS 1.3 on all MSK connections; mTLS via AWS ACM Private CA between Flink jobs and ClickHouse and between Flink and MSK. Secrets: vendor API credentials in AWS Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation; ECS task roles use least-privilege IAM for MSK produce/consume and S3 write. JWT RS256 for Alert API with 1-hour expiry. CloudTrail audit on all AWS API calls. Quarterly ASV network scans on VPC perimeter. DLQ events in S3 encrypted with SSE-KMS. Internal data governance: reconciliation variance >5% triggers compliance review workflow.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster: VendorAdapterService (1 task per vendor, 0.5 vCPU / 1 GB), NormalizationJob (4 tasks, 2 vCPU / 8 GB each for Flink memory), AggregationJob (4 tasks, 2 vCPU / 8 GB), DLQConsumerService (1 task, 0.25 vCPU / 512 MB). MSK 3-broker kafka.m5.large in private VPC subnets us-east-1a/1b/1c. ClickHouse primary + replica on r6i.2xlarge EC2 with EBS gp3 3000 IOPS. GitHub Actions CI: ruff lint, pytest (90% coverage gate on mapper logic), golangci-lint, Docker build + ECR push, ECS rolling deploy via task definition update. Flink savepoints to S3 before each deploy. ClickHouse DDL migrations via versioned SQL scripts run as ECS one-off tasks with pre-migration snapshot. Environments: dev (single-AZ, shared MSK dev cluster), staging (multi-AZ, production-mirror MSK), production (multi-AZ, dedicated MSK).
target_output.observability: Prometheus scraping: Flink JMX (flink_jobmanager_job_uptime, flink_taskmanager_job_task_buffers_inputQueueLength, records_in/out per operator), Go adapter metrics (pos_events_ingested_total by vendor+location, pos_events_masked_total, dlq_events_total by topic). Grafana dashboards: pipeline lag heatmap by location (Kafka consumer group lag), DLQ depth per vendor topic, revenue aggregation freshness (seconds since last revenue_snapshot write per location), inventory alert trigger rate. Alert rules: consumer_lag_seconds > 60 triggers PagerDuty P2; dlq_events_total rate > 10/min triggers P1; vendor_feed health_status = 'offline' for >2 minutes triggers P2; ClickHouse insert latency p99 > 500ms triggers P3. Structured JSON logs from all services with fields: service, location_id, vendor, event_id, action, duration_ms, error.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated monthly: MSK 3x kafka.m5.large ~$900, ClickHouse 2x r6i.2xlarge ~$1,240, Fargate (NormalizationJob + AggregationJob 8 tasks) ~$1,800, Redis cache.r6g.large ~$280, Fargate (adapters + DLQ) ~$150, S3 + data transfer ~$200, total ~$4,570/month well within $8,000 budget. Scaling: Flink parallelism increase by adding ECS tasks (NormalizationJob scales to 64 tasks matching Kafka partitions); ClickHouse scales vertically to r6i.4xlarge at 50K tx/min; Redis sorted sets tested to 5 million keys at sub-1ms latency. Add new POS vendor by deploying new VendorAdapter ECS task and adding vendor-specific Kafka topic.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Toast vendor adapter with DLQ, NormalizationJob with ToastMapper, AggregationJob with 1-minute revenue windows, inventory alert engine for top 20 items, Grafana ops dashboard, Alert Config API, GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline, PCI masking at ingestion. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Square vendor integration, DLQ replay tooling, reconciliation batch job, historical backfill from S3, alert threshold UI via existing dashboard. Phase 3 (8 weeks): NCR Aloha adapter, predictive par threshold suggestions from 30-day sales patterns, district manager mobile push alerts via SNS, vendor feed health SLA reporting.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: ruff formatting + isort, type hints required on all Flink operator functions, pytest with 90% coverage on mapper and aggregation logic. Go: golangci-lint, table-driven tests for card masking and DLQ routing logic. Kafka consumer groups named by service and environment (e.g., 'normalization-job-prod'). ClickHouse schema changes require backward-compatible ADD COLUMN only in production; breaking changes via new table + migration job. Feature flags (LaunchDarkly) for new vendor rollout enabling per-location activation. ADR docs for mapper schema decisions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: PyFlink checkpoint overhead under 10K tx/min peak causing latency spikes exceeding sub-minute SLA. Mitigation: tune checkpoint interval to 30 seconds with incremental checkpointing; pre-validate under load in staging with realistic Toast payload volumes. Risk: ClickHouse single-shard write bottleneck at multi-vendor scale (50K tx/min). Mitigation: introduce sharding by location_id hash when transaction volume exceeds r6i.4xlarge capacity. Tradeoff: chosen ECS Fargate over EMR for Flink to reduce managed service cost, accepting manual Flink savepoint management. Tradeoff: single ClickHouse cluster over distributed ClickHouse cluster for cost; acceptable at current scale with replica for read HA. Risk: POS vendor API instability causing adapter polling gaps. Mitigation: dead-letter topic capture and S3 archival allow gap-fill replay without data loss.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected PyFlink over Kafka Streams for richer stateful windowing. Confirmed card masking at VendorAdapter ingestion point resolving PCI scope concern. DLQ topics defined per vendor and per normalization stage with S3-backed replay. ClickHouse MergeTree partitioning by month chosen for query performance on 3-year retention window. Reconciliation batch job scheduled nightly at 02:00 local time per location.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision fixing DLQ absence and PCI card masking timing for multi-location POS streaming pipeline.

project_goal.value: Offline-first mobile app for food service workers to complete digital food safety checklists with automatic sync and compliance audit trail generation
project_goal.rationale: Replaces paper-based HACCP logs with a verifiable digital record

target_users.value: Kitchen staff, shift supervisors, and compliance managers at multi-location restaurant groups
target_users.rationale: Covers both data entry roles and compliance oversight roles

project_class.rationale: Offline-first data capture in kitchen environments requires a native or React Native mobile client

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Mobile UI, API backend, SQLite local store, Cognito auth, compliance analytics, manager dashboard

complexity_level.rationale: Offline-first sync with conflict resolution, HACCP compliance rules, and multi-location admin dashboard

risk_level.rationale: Handles confidential compliance records; regulatory consequences if audit trails are tampered with

data_sensitivity.rationale: HACCP audit records and food safety inspection data are confidential regulatory documents

external_exposure.rationale: Access restricted to authenticated staff with location-based RBAC

access_model.value: RBAC: staff complete checklists for their assigned location; supervisors approve and override; compliance managers have cross-location read access
access_model.rationale: Separation of duties required for audit integrity

feature_scope.value: Offline checklist completion, temperature log capture, allergen handling confirmations, equipment sanitization records, sync conflict resolution, health authority export formats
feature_scope.rationale: Full HACCP workflow coverage

mvp_scope.value: Opening and closing food safety checklists, temperature recording, offline capture with sync, basic compliance dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Core safety workflow for pilot with 10 locations

security_baseline.value: Cognito MFA for managers, TLS 1.3 for sync, device-level encryption via Expo SecureStore, tamper-evident audit log hashing
security_baseline.rationale: Compliance records must be immutable and access-controlled

frontend_stack.value: React Native with Expo SDK 51
frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform iOS and Android with Expo managed workflow for OTA updates

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI on ECS Fargate
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async performance for concurrent sync requests from many kitchen devices

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS for server-side records; SQLite via expo-sqlite for offline mobile store
data_platform.rationale: Dual-layer persistence for offline-first architecture

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 with RDS Multi-AZ
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with HA database for backend API

privacy_retention_policy.value: Audit records retained 3 years per FSMA requirements; location profiles retained for contract duration; deleted staff accounts anonymized within 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: FSMA record-keeping mandate and GDPR erasure compliance

future_scope.value: IoT temperature probe Bluetooth integration, automated health department submission API, predictive non-conformance alerts
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap items

constraints.value: Must work with zero connectivity for up to 8-hour kitchen shifts; iOS and Android support required; must export reports accepted by local health departments
constraints.rationale: Kitchen environment constraints and regulatory requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on FastAPI backend, Expo crash reporting via Sentry, sync queue depth and conflict rate metrics in CloudWatch
observability_baseline.rationale: Need visibility into sync health and mobile crash rates

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints with mobile TestFlight/Play Store beta per sprint
execution_preference.rationale: Frequent device testing cycles required for offline UX validation

compliance_context.value: FDA Food Safety Modernization Act (FSMA) record-keeping; HACCP critical control point documentation requirements; local health authority inspection format compliance
compliance_context.rationale: Core regulatory drivers for the product

project.goal: Digital HACCP-compliant food safety checklists with offline-first mobile capture and compliance dashboard
project.target_users: Kitchen staff, shift supervisors, compliance managers
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks, Phase 2 in 6 months

frontend.state: Zustand for local UI, WatermelonDB for offline SQLite sync
frontend.navigation: Expo Router with tab and stack navigators

security.auth: Cognito user pools with MFA for managers
security.encryption: Expo SecureStore for tokens, RDS encryption at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit

devops.ci: Buildkite with iOS simulator and Android emulator test lanes
devops.environments: dev, staging, production

reasoner_reviews.product: Offline-first architecture is non-negotiable given kitchen Wi-Fi dead zones. The admin compliance dashboard must surface overdue checklists and temperature exceedances at a glance for multi-location managers. Health authority export formats vary by jurisdiction and need a pluggable template system.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: WatermelonDB on top of SQLite provides the reactive offline store with sync primitives needed for conflict resolution. The sync protocol should use a last-write-wins strategy with server-side timestamp authority. The FastAPI backend must handle burst sync traffic when multiple devices reconnect after a shift simultaneously.
reasoner_reviews.security: Tamper-evident hashing of completed checklist records is essential — each submitted record should include a SHA-256 hash of its content chained to a per-location audit log sequence number. Cognito MFA should be mandatory for supervisor and manager roles, optional for staff. Device-level encryption via Expo SecureStore protects credentials at rest.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 8-hour offline window requires the WatermelonDB local schema to mirror all active checklist templates and location configuration, pre-loaded at login. Health authority export templates must be versioned to support jurisdiction-specific format updates without an app release.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan did not fully specify the sync conflict resolution strategy for simultaneous submissions from multiple staff members on the same checklist. Also missing: retry queue behavior when sync fails partway through a batch upload.

frontend.app_structure: Expo Router with (auth) and (app) route groups; offline indicator banner in root layout; WatermelonDB provider wrapping app shell
frontend.pages_and_flows: Login screen, Location selector, Checklist list (by shift/type), Checklist detail with CCP item rows, Temperature entry with numeric keypad, Allergen confirmation modal, Sync status screen, Admin dashboard (web view component for managers)
frontend.state_management: WatermelonDB reactive queries for checklist data, Zustand for UI state (selected location, sync status), Expo NetInfo for connectivity detection
frontend.performance_notes: Pre-fetch all active checklist templates and location config on login; defer sync to background task via Expo TaskManager; compress sync payloads with gzip

backend.service_design: FastAPI with routers for checklists, sync, locations, users, and compliance-exports
backend.api_patterns: POST /sync/push for batch upload of completed records, GET /sync/pull for incremental delta since last_sync_timestamp, GET /compliance/export/{format} for health authority reports
backend.failure_handling: Idempotent sync endpoints keyed on client-generated UUID per record; duplicate submissions return 200 with existing record; partial batch failures return per-record status array

security.auth_design: Cognito user pools with MFA enforced for supervisor/manager roles; staff use PIN-only flow via Cognito custom auth challenge; JWT tokens cached in Expo SecureStore
security.authorization_model: RBAC: staff scoped to assigned location_id; supervisors can approve records at their location; compliance_managers have read-all scope
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Append-only audit_trail_events table with SHA-256 content hash per record, sequence number per location; CloudTrail for API key and admin actions
security.incident_response_notes: On detected hash chain break, flag location audit trail as potentially compromised and alert compliance manager with specific sequence range

data.entities: safety_checklists (id, location_id, checklist_type ENUM opening/closing/hourly/custom, template_version, active), checklist_completions (id UUID client-generated, checklist_id, staff_id, location_id, shift_date, submitted_at, synced_at, completion_hash), temperature_records (id, completion_id, probe_point, celsius_value, within_range BOOL, timestamp), allergen_declarations (id, completion_id, allergen_type, confirmed_by_staff_id, confirmed_at), equipment_sanitization_logs (id, completion_id, equipment_id, sanitizer_type, contact_time_seconds, pass BOOL), audit_trail_events (id, location_id, sequence_num, action, actor_id, record_id, content_hash, chained_hash, created_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for server records; WatermelonDB SQLite on device for offline store; S3 for exported compliance report PDFs
data.retention_and_deletion: Checklist completions and temperature records retained 3 years per FSMA; staff accounts anonymized 90 days post-termination; audit trail events immutable

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster with API service (2-6 tasks, auto-scale on CPU 60%); RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ in private subnet; ALB with WAF; Redis ElastiCache single-AZ
devops.ci_cd_design: Buildkite: unit tests, FastAPI integration tests, Expo build for iOS (TestFlight) and Android (Play Store internal track), smoke tests against staging API
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS rolling update with 50% minimum healthy; previous task definition retained for 1-click rollback; database migrations via Alembic with down-migration scripts

ISS-001.title: Sync conflict resolution strategy undefined for concurrent multi-staff submissions
ISS-001.detail: Round 2 plan described last-write-wins but did not specify how conflicts on the same checklist_completion record are detected or resolved when two staff submit simultaneously on the same checklist instance.

history.note: Initially flagged as missing sync design
Plan added last-write-wins mention but lacked implementation specifics

ISS-002.title: Retry queue behavior on partial sync batch failure not specified
ISS-002.detail: If the backend returns a partial failure array during POST /sync/push (e.g., records 1-3 succeeded, records 4-5 failed), the mobile client behavior for retry, re-queue, and deduplication is not described.

history.note: Introduced in critic review of round 2

ISS-003.title: Health authority export template versioning strategy missing
ISS-003.detail: Round 2 added a compliance export endpoint but did not explain how jurisdiction-specific format versions are managed without requiring an app store release.

history.note: Flagged by product reasoner
Addressed by server-side template registry with format_version param

focus_issues.title: Sync conflict resolution strategy undefined for concurrent multi-staff submissions
focus_issues.detail: Round 2 plan described last-write-wins but did not specify how conflicts on the same checklist_completion record are detected or resolved when two staff submit simultaneously on the same checklist instance.

focus_issues.title: Retry queue behavior on partial sync batch failure not specified
focus_issues.detail: If the backend returns a partial failure array during POST /sync/push (e.g., records 1-3 succeeded, records 4-5 failed), the mobile client behavior for retry, re-queue, and deduplication is not described.

latest_recommendations: Define exact conflict resolution semantics for checklist_completion records — specify whether a completion is per-staff-per-shift (unique key) or shareable, and how server adjudicates duplicate client UUIDs
Specify the mobile retry queue: failed records stay in WatermelonDB with status=pending_retry; next sync attempt re-includes them; idempotency key prevents server-side duplicates
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Food Safety Checklist Mobile App — Round 2 Architecture

convergence_state.reason: Two unresolved issues affecting sync reliability and data integrity

previous_audits.summary: Solid offline-first design with good HACCP data model, but sync conflict resolution and retry semantics remain underspecified, creating data integrity risk for regulatory compliance records.
strengths: WatermelonDB choice is well-justified for offline-first with reactive queries
Tamper-evident audit trail hash chain design is strong
RBAC with location-scoped staff access is appropriate
concerns: Conflict resolution semantics missing for concurrent submissions
Partial sync failure retry behavior undefined in mobile client
blocking_issues: Sync conflict resolution must be fully specified before production deployment of compliance records
recommendations: Specify that checklist_completion records are unique per (checklist_id, staff_id, shift_date) — collisions on this key indicate duplicate submissions and should return the existing record

Define the WatermelonDB sync retry queue: failed records flagged with sync_status=failed, retried on next connectivity event with exponential backoff, server idempotency key prevents duplicate inserts
