devops.environments: local development, release

constraints.offline: No external network calls during scan
constraints.distribution: Single statically-linked binary (macOS amd64/arm64, Linux amd64)

reasoner_reviews.product: The MVP read-only scan approach is the right risk management call — applying SSH remediation commands to live POS devices without extensive testing could disrupt point-of-sale operations during service hours. The default credential check must include vendor-specific credential lists for Toast, NCR Aloha, and Unifi rather than generic wordlists to reduce false positives.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Cobra is the correct choice for Go CLI structure. The scanner architecture should use goroutine worker pools for concurrent device scanning to meet the 5-minute /16 SLA — sequential scanning of 65,535 IPs will not complete in time. Recommend a configurable --workers flag defaulting to 100 concurrent scanners. SQLCipher CGo dependency complicates cross-compilation; verify arm64 build pipeline early.
reasoner_reviews.security: Storing device credentials for default-cred testing is the highest-risk aspect of this tool. The credential list must be read-only at runtime — never written to SQLite. SSH private keys must use the OS keychain exclusively. The tool binary should be signed and notarized to prevent tampered distribution. Consider adding a --dry-run flag that simulates remediation without executing SSH commands to reduce operational risk.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The offline constraint is critical for POS network environments. Ensure the CVE/firmware version database is bundled as an embedded Go asset (go:embed) updated only at build time, with a --update-db command that pulls fresh data when the admin is on a non-POS network. The 5-minute SLA for /16 scans requires profiling goroutine pool sizes on typical admin hardware.

backend.service_design: Single Go binary with Cobra command tree. Root commands: 'scan' (subnet discovery + checks), 'report' (generate output from stored findings), 'db' (manage local SQLite), 'remediate' (phase 2 SSH hardening). Subcommands: 'scan devices' (discovery), 'scan firmware' (CVE check), 'scan creds' (default credential test), 'scan ports' (management port check). Each scan command is independently runnable or chained via 'scan all'.
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API. CLI flags: --subnet CIDR (required for scan), --workers int (default 100), --timeout duration (default 3s per device), --output json|html|table (default table), --db-path string (default ~/.posaudit/posaudit.db), --verbose bool, --dry-run bool (for future remediate command). Config file: ~/.posaudit/config.yaml overridable by flags.
backend.failure_handling: Scan errors per device written to structured log file; device marked with scan_status='error' in SQLite; scan continues on per-device failures. SQLite WAL mode for concurrent read during scan. SQLCipher open failure (wrong passphrase) exits with code 2 and clear error message. Goroutine panics recovered with log capture.
backend.business_modules: DeviceScanner (goroutine pool, ICMP ping + TCP connect to identify live hosts), FirmwareChecker (SNMP v2c sysDescr OID pull + version parse + CVE DB lookup), CredentialTester (SSH and HTTP basic auth attempts against bundled credential list by device type), PortScanner (TCP connect to management ports 22/23/80/443/8080/8443/161), ReportGenerator (HTML template + JSON marshaling from SQLite query), DBManager (SQLite WAL mode open/close, schema migration via golang-migrate)

security.auth_design: No user auth; OS file permissions on ~/.posaudit/ directory (0700) restrict access. SQLite DB opened with SQLCipher passphrase derived from OS keychain entry 'posaudit-db-key'; on first run prompts admin to set passphrase and stores in keychain.
security.secrets_management: Credential test list embedded as encrypted go:embed asset, decrypted at runtime with build-time key — prevents casual extraction from binary. SSH private keys for future remediation stored via macOS Security framework SecItemAdd or Linux libsecret; never written to SQLite or disk files. Vendor API keys (future) similarly keychain-stored.
security.abuse_prevention: --dry-run flag for remediate subcommand. Rate limiting on credential tests: 3 attempts per device per credential with 500ms delay to avoid POS device lockout. Scan restricted to subnets specified by admin — no default-scan-everything behavior. Warning prompt before any write operation (remediation).
security.security_risks: Risk: tool binary tampering enabling credential harvesting. Mitigation: binary signing + notarization + SHA256 checksum published on GitHub Release. Risk: credential test list exposure via binary extraction. Mitigation: embedded asset encryption with build-time key.

data.entities: network_devices (device_id TEXT PK, ip_address TEXT, mac_address TEXT, device_type ENUM pos_terminal/kds/wifi_ap/unknown, vendor TEXT, model TEXT, discovered_at DATETIME, last_scanned_at DATETIME), firmware_versions (firmware_id TEXT PK, device_id FK, version_string TEXT, cve_ids_json TEXT, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low/none, checked_at DATETIME), security_findings (finding_id TEXT PK, device_id FK, scan_run_id FK, finding_type ENUM default_creds/open_port/outdated_firmware, severity ENUM critical/high/medium/low, detail TEXT, port_number INT, status ENUM open/remediated, found_at DATETIME), remediation_actions (action_id TEXT PK, finding_id FK, action_type TEXT, applied_at DATETIME, result TEXT, applied_by TEXT), audit_reports (report_id TEXT PK, scan_run_id FK, generated_at DATETIME, format ENUM json/html, file_path TEXT), scan_runs (run_id TEXT PK, subnet CIDR TEXT, started_at DATETIME, completed_at DATETIME, devices_scanned INT, findings_count INT)
data.schema_notes: All TEXT primary keys use UUID v4. SQLite WAL mode enabled for concurrent reads during active scan. golang-migrate for schema versioning with embedded migration files via go:embed. SQLCipher passphrase applied at PRAGMA key before any table access.
data.retention_and_deletion: Findings retained indefinitely in local SQLite; 'db prune --before DATE' command removes scan_runs and associated findings older than specified date; full DB export via 'db export --format json' before pruning recommended in docs.

devops.deployment_topology: No server deployment. Single binary distribution: macOS amd64 (darwin/amd64), macOS arm64 (darwin/arm64), Linux amd64 (linux/amd64). GitHub Releases as distribution channel.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go vet + staticcheck lint, go test ./... with race detector, goreleaser for multi-platform builds with code signing (macOS via Apple Developer cert in GH secrets), SHA256 checksums published in release notes. Triggered on git tag push matching v*.*.*.
devops.observability_stack: Local JSON log file at ~/.posaudit/logs/audit_{YYYYMMDD}.log. Log rotation after 30 days via 'db prune' command. --verbose flag enables debug-level goroutine pool and SNMP trace output. No external metrics.
devops.devops_risks: Risk: SQLCipher CGo dependency complicates cross-compilation. Mitigation: use zig-cc cross-compiler in GitHub Actions for Linux builds; macOS builds on macos-latest runner. Risk: Apple notarization turnaround delay. Mitigation: automate notarization via xcrun notarytool in CI pipeline.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a local-only CLI security audit tool in Go with Cobra targeting POS network devices in franchise restaurant environments. Key design decisions: goroutine worker pool for concurrent subnet scanning to meet 5-minute /16 SLA; SQLCipher for local DB encryption protecting confidential device data; embedded CVE database as go:embed asset for offline operation; OS keychain for SSH key storage avoiding disk exposure. PCI-DSS requirements 6.3 and 8.3.6 directly addressed by firmware CVE checks and default credential detection. No frontend subplan since project_class is cli_tool.
target_output.title: POS Network Device Security Audit CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A Go CLI tool for franchise IT administrators to audit in-store network device security across POS terminals, kitchen display systems, and Wi-Fi access points. Built with Cobra, the tool performs subnet scanning, firmware CVE checks, default credential detection, and management port scanning on isolated POS networks — fully offline with no external network calls. Findings are stored in an encrypted SQLite database and exported as JSON or HTML remediation reports for PCI-DSS compliance documentation.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single statically-linked Go binary with a Cobra command tree organizing scan, report, db, and remediate subcommands. Scanning layer uses a configurable goroutine worker pool (default 100 workers) to perform concurrent ICMP discovery, SNMP firmware probing, TCP port scanning, and credential testing against all live hosts in the target /24 or /16 subnet — achieving /16 completion within 5 minutes on standard admin hardware. An embedded go:embed CVE database (JSON) provides offline firmware version-to-CVE mapping. Findings are persisted to a SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite database in ~/.posaudit/. Report generation queries SQLite and renders JSON or HTML output. No HTTP server, no external API calls during scan operation, no cloud dependencies.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22 with modules. CLI framework: Cobra v1.8 with lipgloss for colored terminal output. Database: SQLite 3.45 via mattn/go-sqlite3 (CGo) with SQLCipher extension. DB migrations: golang-migrate with embedded SQL files. Network: golang.org/x/crypto/ssh for SSH client, gosnmp for SNMP v2c probing, custom TCP port scanner using net.DialTimeout. Embedded assets: go:embed for CVE database JSON and HTML report template. Code signing: goreleaser with Apple Developer cert (macOS) and GPG (Linux). CI: GitHub Actions with goreleaser.
target_output.functional_feature_map: 'posaudit scan all --subnet 10.1.0.0/24' — runs full discovery + firmware + creds + ports pipeline. 'posaudit scan devices --subnet CIDR' — ICMP + TCP SYN discovery, writes network_devices to SQLite. 'posaudit scan firmware --subnet CIDR' — SNMP sysDescr pull, version parse, embedded CVE lookup, writes firmware_versions and security_findings. 'posaudit scan creds --subnet CIDR' — tests vendor-specific default credential pairs via SSH and HTTP basic auth, writes security_findings. 'posaudit scan ports --subnet CIDR' — checks management ports 22/23/80/443/8080/8443/161 for unexpected exposure. 'posaudit report generate --scan-run-id ID --format html' — renders findings to HTML with severity summary. 'posaudit db prune --before 2025-01-01' — removes stale scan data. 'posaudit db update-cve' — pulls updated CVE database from internal server when on non-POS network.
target_output.system_components: DeviceScanner (goroutine pool, ScanWorker goroutines): ICMP echo + TCP connect-based live host detection; sends discovered IPs to channel for downstream checkers. FirmwareChecker: SNMP v2c GET on OID 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1.0 (sysDescr), applies device-type regex parsers for Toast POS firmware strings and Unifi AP model strings, looks up parsed version in embedded CVE JSON index. CredentialTester: iterates vendor-specific credential pairs (ToastDefaultCreds, UnifiDefaultCreds, NCRAlohaCreds) attempting SSH and HTTP basic auth with 3-attempt limit and 500ms inter-attempt delay per device. PortScanner: net.DialTimeout-based TCP connect to management ports list; records open ports as security_findings. ReportGenerator: html/template rendering for HTML output; encoding/json marshaling for JSON output; queries SQLite via scan_run_id. DBManager: SQLCipher passphrase unlocking, WAL mode, golang-migrate schema runner on first open. LogWriter: zerolog structured JSON logger to ~/.posaudit/logs/audit_{date}.log.
target_output.workflows: Full Audit Scan Workflow: admin runs 'posaudit scan all --subnet 10.1.5.0/24 --workers 150 --output html'. Cobra parses flags, DBManager opens SQLCipher DB (prompts passphrase if first run), creates scan_run record. DeviceScanner spawns 150 ScanWorkers, distributes 254 IPs from CIDR across workers. Each worker: ICMP ping (100ms timeout) then TCP connect to port 80/443 — writes live host to network_devices. FirmwareChecker goroutines process discovered devices: SNMP GET sysDescr, parse vendor/model/version, cross-reference embedded CVE JSON, write firmware_versions and severity-classified security_findings. CredentialTester goroutines test vendor-matched default cred pairs with rate limiting. PortScanner goroutines check 7 management ports per device. ReportGenerator queries all findings for scan_run_id, renders HTML with severity breakdown table, writes to ./posaudit-report-{date}.html. Total duration for /24: <30 seconds. Remediation Report Distribution Workflow: IT admin opens generated HTML report, reviews findings sorted by severity (critical/high first), records manual remediation actions for PCI-DSS audit evidence, optionally exports JSON report to vulnerability tracking system via copy-paste or future API integration.
target_output.data_model: scan_runs (run_id TEXT PK UUID, subnet TEXT, started_at DATETIME, completed_at DATETIME, devices_discovered INT, findings_count INT, worker_count INT). network_devices (device_id TEXT PK UUID, run_id FK, ip_address TEXT, mac_address TEXT, device_type TEXT CHECK IN ('pos_terminal','kds','wifi_ap','switch','unknown'), vendor TEXT, model TEXT, discovered_at DATETIME). firmware_versions (firmware_id TEXT PK UUID, device_id FK, run_id FK, snmp_sysDescr TEXT, parsed_version TEXT, cve_ids_json TEXT, highest_severity TEXT CHECK IN ('critical','high','medium','low','none'), checked_at DATETIME). security_findings (finding_id TEXT PK UUID, device_id FK, run_id FK, finding_type TEXT CHECK IN ('default_creds','open_port','outdated_firmware'), severity TEXT CHECK IN ('critical','high','medium','low'), detail TEXT, port_number INT, credential_type TEXT, status TEXT CHECK IN ('open','remediated'), found_at DATETIME). remediation_actions (action_id TEXT PK UUID, finding_id FK, action_type TEXT, applied_at DATETIME, result TEXT, operator TEXT, ssh_command_hash TEXT). audit_reports (report_id TEXT PK UUID, run_id FK, generated_at DATETIME, format TEXT CHECK IN ('json','html'), file_path TEXT, finding_count_critical INT, finding_count_high INT). device_credentials (cred_id TEXT PK UUID, vendor TEXT, device_type TEXT, username TEXT, password_hash TEXT, is_active BOOL) -- bundled read-only seed data, never written at runtime.
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API. CLI command interface: 'posaudit scan all|devices|firmware|creds|ports --subnet CIDR [--workers N] [--timeout DURATION] [--output json|html|table] [--db-path PATH] [--verbose]'. 'posaudit report generate --scan-run-id UUID [--format json|html] [--out FILE]'. 'posaudit report list' — tabular listing of past scan runs with finding counts. 'posaudit db prune --before DATE [--dry-run]'. 'posaudit db update-cve [--source URL]' — pulls updated CVE JSON when on non-POS network. 'posaudit version' — prints binary version, build date, embedded CVE DB version. Exit codes: 0=success, 1=scan completed with findings, 2=fatal error (DB open fail, invalid subnet), 3=permission denied.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SQLCipher AES-256 encryption on SQLite DB via passphrase derived from OS keychain (macOS: Security.framework SecKeychainItem; Linux: libsecret GNOME keyring). Passphrase never stored on disk or in environment variables. SSH private keys for future remediation stored exclusively in OS keychain — never written to SQLite or flat files. Bundled credential test list embedded as go:embed asset with XOR obfuscation under build-time key, preventing casual binary extraction. Binary distributed as signed macOS universal binary (Apple Developer ID) + notarized; Linux binary with GPG signature; SHA256 checksums in GitHub Release. No external network calls during scan — all CVE lookups against embedded JSON asset. PCI-DSS alignment: tool directly checks Req 8.3.6 (default credentials), Req 6.3 (firmware CVE exposure), Req 1.3 (open management ports). HTML/JSON reports serve as audit evidence for quarterly ASV scan documentation. Rate limiting (3 attempts, 500ms delay) on credential tests prevents POS device account lockout.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Distribution via GitHub Releases: goreleaser builds darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64 (universal binary via lipo), linux/amd64. macOS binary signed with Apple Developer cert and notarized via xcrun notarytool in GitHub Actions. GitHub Actions CI pipeline: go vet + staticcheck, go test ./... --race, coverage gate >85% on scanner and DB packages, goreleaser build + sign on tag push. Release artifacts: posaudit_darwin_universal.zip, posaudit_linux_amd64.tar.gz, SHA256SUMS, SHA256SUMS.gpg. Installation: curl-based installer script or manual binary copy to /usr/local/bin. Database migration: golang-migrate auto-runs on DB open if schema version < current. SQLCipher CGo: macOS builds on macos-14 runner, Linux builds using zig-cc cross-compiler for musl-linked static binary.
target_output.observability: Local JSON structured log file ~/.posaudit/logs/audit_{YYYYMMDD}.log using zerolog. Log fields: timestamp, level, subnet, scan_run_id, device_ip, action (discovered/firmware_checked/cred_tested/port_scanned), result, duration_ms, error. --verbose flag enables goroutine pool diagnostics (workers_active, queue_depth, scan_rate_per_sec). Terminal progress: lipgloss progress bar during scan showing 'Scanning 10.1.5.0/24 — 187/254 hosts checked (74%) | 12 findings'. End-of-scan summary table: devices discovered, findings by severity (critical/high/medium/low). No external metrics, APM, or cloud reporting.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero ongoing infrastructure cost — single binary with local SQLite. Development cost: goreleaser Pro license ~$200/year for automated signing workflow; Apple Developer enrollment $99/year for macOS signing. Binary size target <20 MB including embedded CVE database. Performance scaling: worker pool default 100 goroutines handles /24 in <30 seconds; /16 in <5 minutes on 4-core admin workstation. For future multi-location batch mode, parallel SSH connections limited by admin workstation RAM (each Go SSH session ~2 MB); 500 simultaneous connections feasible on 8 GB host.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Cobra command tree scaffold, DeviceScanner with goroutine pool and ICMP+TCP discovery, FirmwareChecker with embedded CVE DB for Toast POS and Unifi AP, CredentialTester with vendor-specific lists and rate limiting, PortScanner for 7 management ports, SQLCipher DB with golang-migrate, JSON and HTML report generation, GitHub Actions CI + goreleaser signing pipeline. Phase 2 (4 weeks): SSH remediation subcommand with --dry-run flag, policy template engine for hardening configurations per device type, OS keychain integration for SSH key storage, 'db update-cve' online update command, NCR Aloha and additional vendor firmware parsers. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Multi-location batch audit mode, vulnerability tracking system export integration, scheduled audit reminders via local cron helper, CVSS score display in reports.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 with strict vet + staticcheck. Table-driven tests for FirmwareChecker version parsing and CredentialTester rate limiting logic. >85% coverage on scanner and DB packages. Goroutine pool implemented with errgroup for structured error propagation — no raw goroutine spawning. All external flags validated via Cobra Args validators before scan start. go:embed CVE database updated in separate versioned JSON file; never hand-edited. SQLCipher pragma key set immediately after open, before any query. No init() functions; all state initialized via explicit constructors.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SQLCipher CGo dependency complicates static cross-compilation. Mitigation: zig-cc for Linux musl builds; macOS builds on native runner. Accept CGo dependency given SQLCipher has no pure-Go equivalent with equivalent security properties. Risk: Credential test rate limiting insufficient for some POS terminal lockout policies. Mitigation: --cred-delay flag for admin-configurable inter-attempt delay; document lockout risk prominently. Risk: Embedded CVE database staleness between releases. Mitigation: db update-cve command and clear display of CVE DB version and date in posaudit version output. Tradeoff: OS keychain dependency over encrypted file for SSH keys — requires keychain access permissions setup but eliminates disk key exposure risk. Risk: Tool binary theft enabling automated POS network enumeration. Mitigation: binary signing + SQLCipher passphrase requirement prevents immediate useful extraction.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected SQLCipher over pure-Go encryption for industry-standard AES-256 at-rest protection. Confirmed goroutine worker pool over sequential scan to meet 5-minute /16 SLA. go:embed for CVE database enables offline operation per POS network air-gap constraint. HTML report template embedded via go:embed — no external template files required. Exit code 1 for 'scan completed with findings' distinguishes from exit code 0 (clean scan) for CI/pipeline integration.

metadata.notes: PCI-DSS-aligned CLI audit tool for POS network device security in franchise restaurant environments.

project_goal.value: Web application for independent restaurant owners to track prep waste, trim waste, and spoilage events against purchased inventory, surfacing highest-cost waste contributors and ordering adjustment suggestions to reduce food cost percentage
project_goal.rationale: Core value proposition for thin-margin restaurant operators

target_users.value: Independent restaurant owners and kitchen managers who track daily food waste and want data-driven ordering quantity guidance
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in product discovery with 12 restaurant operator interviews

project_class.rationale: Browser-based app with backend API and persistent data; not fullstack framework-dependent

capabilities.rationale: Requires user auth, waste logging API, PostgreSQL persistence, and analytics reporting

complexity_level.rationale: CRUD with calculated cost analytics; moderate data model complexity

risk_level.rationale: Internal business data only; no PII beyond user accounts; no payments

data_sensitivity.rationale: Business operational data (waste logs, purchase orders); not personal or financial data

external_exposure.rationale: All data access requires authenticated restaurant owner session

access_model.value: Firebase Auth with email/password and Google OAuth; single-tenant per restaurant with owner and kitchen manager roles
access_model.rationale: Firebase Auth reduces auth infrastructure overhead for small team; restaurant-scoped data isolation required

feature_scope.value: Daily waste event logging, ingredient inventory management, purchase order tracking, weekly food cost reports, ordering quantity suggestions based on waste patterns
feature_scope.rationale: Complete feature set for MVP and immediate post-launch

mvp_scope.value: Waste log entry (ingredient, quantity, waste reason), ingredient inventory list, weekly cost report showing top 5 waste contributors by dollar value
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum feature set that delivers value to operators

security_baseline.value: Firebase Auth JWT validation on all API routes, HTTPS only, parameterized queries (no ORM string interpolation), restaurant-scoped row-level data access
security_baseline.rationale: Standard web app security for internal business data

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with Vite and TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Vite dev server speed, React team familiarity, TypeScript for API contract safety

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 with Hono framework
backend_stack.rationale: Hono is lightweight, fast, and well-suited for a DigitalOcean App Platform Node deployment

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on DigitalOcean Managed Database
data_platform.rationale: Managed PostgreSQL removes operational burden; relational model fits waste-to-inventory join queries

hosting_target.value: DigitalOcean App Platform for API and frontend static serving
hosting_target.rationale: Simpler than Kubernetes for this scale; integrated managed DB and deployment pipeline

privacy_retention_policy.value: Waste log and purchase order data retained indefinitely for trend analysis; Firebase Auth accounts deleted on user request per GDPR Article 17
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Long-term data retention enables multi-year waste trend analysis valuable to operators

future_scope.value: Mobile app for kitchen staff waste logging, integrations with POS systems for automatic depletion tracking, multi-location support for small restaurant groups
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP growth roadmap

constraints.value: Solo developer team; total infrastructure cost under $80/month; must support daily logging by non-technical kitchen staff
constraints.rationale: Budget and team size constraints for an early-stage product

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for API latency and error rate; DigitalOcean App Platform built-in log streaming
observability_baseline.rationale: Lightweight observability stack for a solo developer team

execution_preference.value: Iterative weekly releases; solo developer with feature-branch workflow
execution_preference.rationale: Solo team workflow preference

project.goal: Track restaurant food waste and surface cost reduction opportunities
project.target_users: Independent restaurant owners and kitchen managers

frontend.state: React Query for server state, Zustand for local UI state

backend.auth_validation: Firebase Admin SDK JWT verification middleware

security.auth: Firebase Auth (email/password + Google OAuth)
security.transport: HTTPS only via DigitalOcean App Platform

devops.environments: dev, staging, production

reasoner_reviews.product: The waste logging UX is critical — kitchen staff logging waste during a busy service need a minimal-tap flow: select ingredient from a short list, enter quantity, tap waste reason. A multi-step form will kill adoption. The weekly cost report showing top 5 waste contributors in dollar terms (not percentages) will land best with operators focused on P&L.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Hono on Node.js is the right lightweight choice for DigitalOcean App Platform. Key design decision: the ordering suggestion algorithm needs careful definition — a simple rolling 4-week waste-average-over-purchase-ratio is achievable in SQL without a separate ML service. Restaurant-scoped data isolation must be enforced at the query layer, not just the route layer.
reasoner_reviews.security: Firebase Auth JWT validation must be enforced as Hono middleware on every API route — no route can be accessible without a valid Firebase ID token. Restaurant ID must be extracted from the verified token claims and used in every WHERE clause, not from request parameters that a malicious user could spoof.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: $80/month on DigitalOcean: Basic App Platform plan ($12/month) for API, Static Site for frontend (free), Managed PostgreSQL Basic ($15/month). Total ~$27/month well within budget. Prometheus/Grafana can run on a small Droplet or use DigitalOcean's built-in metrics.

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with React Router v6. Feature folders: /waste-log, /inventory, /purchase-orders, /reports. Shared components: IngredientSelect (searchable dropdown for fast kitchen logging), WasteReasonSelect, DatePicker.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Login, Daily Waste Log (primary kitchen staff view), Ingredient Inventory Management, Purchase Order Entry, Weekly Cost Report (Recharts bar chart of top waste contributors), Settings (restaurant profile, user management)
frontend.state_management: React Query for all server state with 5-minute stale time. Zustand for unsaved waste log draft state to preserve entries if the page is accidentally closed.
frontend.performance_notes: Ingredient list fetched once on app load and cached in React Query. Report charts rendered client-side from API aggregation data — no large dataset transfers to frontend.

backend.service_design: Hono app with route modules: /waste-logs, /ingredients, /purchase-orders, /reports. Firebase Admin SDK middleware verifies JWT and attaches restaurant_id to context on every request.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful JSON with restaurant_id scoped to all queries. Report endpoint computes aggregations server-side in SQL.
backend.business_modules: WasteCalculator: computes dollar value of waste events using current ingredient unit costs. OrderingSuggestionEngine: SQL CTE computing 4-week rolling average waste rate per ingredient versus purchase quantity to generate delta recommendations.
backend.failure_handling: Hono global error handler returns structured JSON errors. Database connection pool errors retried once before 503 response.

security.auth_design: Firebase Auth with email/password and Google OAuth sign-in. Firebase Admin SDK verifyIdToken() in Hono middleware. Short-lived ID tokens (1 hour) with automatic refresh via Firebase client SDK.
security.authorization_model: Two roles: owner (full CRUD + reports + user management) and kitchen_manager (waste log entry and ingredient list read only). Role stored in Firebase custom claims.
security.secrets_management: DigitalOcean App Platform encrypted environment variables for DATABASE_URL, FIREBASE_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON. No secrets in repository.
security.security_risks: Misconfigured restaurant_id scoping could leak one restaurant's waste data to another. Mitigate with integration tests asserting cross-restaurant data isolation on every query.

data.entities: restaurants (id, name, owner_uid, timezone, created_at), ingredients (id, restaurant_id, name, category ENUM produce/protein/dairy/dry_goods/beverage, unit, unit_cost_cents, updated_at), waste_log_entries (id, restaurant_id, ingredient_id FK, waste_reason ENUM spoilage/over_prep/trim/expiry/dropped, quantity DECIMAL, logged_by_uid, logged_at), purchase_orders (id, restaurant_id, ingredient_id FK, quantity, unit_cost_cents, ordered_at), waste_categories (reference table), ordering_suggestions (id, restaurant_id, ingredient_id, suggested_order_qty, computed_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on DigitalOcean Managed Database with daily automated backups. No cache layer — query volumes do not justify Redis at this scale.
data.retention_and_deletion: Waste log entries and purchase orders retained indefinitely. Firebase Auth user deletion triggers a cascade delete of restaurant data on owner account deletion. Ordering suggestions recomputed weekly and previous rows replaced.
data.analytics_events: Weekly cost report queries: SUM(waste_log_entries.quantity * ingredients.unit_cost_cents) GROUP BY ingredient_id, filtered by logged_at BETWEEN week_start AND week_end, ordered by total_waste_cost DESC LIMIT 5.

devops.deployment_topology: DigitalOcean App Platform: two components — API (Node.js service) and Frontend (Static Site). Both auto-deployed from GitHub main branch. Managed PostgreSQL as a resource attached to the App.
devops.environments: Production: DigitalOcean App Platform production environment. Staging: separate App Platform app on staging branch with a separate managed DB instance.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: lint, TypeScript typecheck, Jest unit tests for WasteCalculator and OrderingSuggestionEngine, integration tests against a PostgreSQL test container. All pass required before merge to main.
devops.devops_risks: DigitalOcean App Platform cold start on free-tier dyno sleeping — not applicable since API is on Basic plan (always-on). Database connection exhaustion at high concurrency unlikely for this scale but set pool max to 10.

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass plan for a food waste tracking web app targeting independent restaurants. The critical design decisions are: Firebase Auth JWT middleware enforcing restaurant_id scoping on all API routes to prevent cross-tenant data leaks, a simple SQL-based ordering suggestion algorithm avoiding ML infrastructure complexity, and a minimal-tap waste logging UI designed for non-technical kitchen staff. The $80/month constraint is comfortably met with DigitalOcean App Platform + Managed PostgreSQL at approximately $27/month.
target_output.title: Restaurant Food Waste Tracker Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A private-authenticated web application built with React 18 and Hono on Node.js 22, deployed on DigitalOcean App Platform with PostgreSQL 16. Restaurant kitchen staff log daily waste events by ingredient and reason category; the system calculates real-time dollar values and generates weekly reports showing the top waste cost contributors with ordering quantity adjustment suggestions. Firebase Auth provides restaurant-scoped multi-tenant access control.
target_output.architecture_overview: React 18 SPA served as a static site on DigitalOcean App Platform, communicating with a Hono REST API also on App Platform. PostgreSQL 16 managed database stores waste logs, ingredient inventory, and purchase orders. Firebase Auth issues JWT tokens validated by Firebase Admin SDK middleware on every API route. Restaurant-scoped data isolation is enforced at the SQL query layer using restaurant_id extracted from verified Firebase token claims. No cache layer is required at current scale. The ordering suggestion engine is a SQL CTE computing 4-week rolling waste averages versus purchase quantities, running as a weekly batch computation.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, Vite, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, React Query, Zustand, React Router v6, Recharts. Backend: Node.js 22, Hono framework, Firebase Admin SDK, node-postgres (pg), node-pg-migrate. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on DigitalOcean Managed Database. Auth: Firebase Auth (email/password + Google OAuth). CI/CD: GitHub Actions. Hosting: DigitalOcean App Platform (Basic plan for API, Static Site for frontend). Observability: Grafana + Prometheus, DigitalOcean log streaming.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Daily Waste Log: kitchen staff select ingredient from a searchable dropdown (pre-loaded on app boot), enter quantity, select waste reason (spoilage/over_prep/trim/expiry/dropped), and submit — dollar value computed in real time from ingredient unit cost. Ingredient Inventory: owner creates and prices ingredient catalog with unit cost per UOM. Purchase Order Tracking: owner records purchase quantities and prices, feeding the ordering suggestion algorithm. Weekly Cost Report: Recharts horizontal bar chart of top 5 ingredients by total waste dollar value for the current week, compared to the prior week. Ordering Suggestions: per-ingredient recommended order quantity based on 4-week rolling waste-adjusted depletion rate.
target_output.system_components: DigitalOcean App Platform API service (Hono, Node.js 22, always-on Basic dyno). DigitalOcean App Platform Static Site (React 18 build, global CDN). DigitalOcean Managed PostgreSQL 16 (daily backups, connection pooling via PgBouncer). Firebase Auth (JWT issuance, Google OAuth, custom claims for roles). Firebase Admin SDK (server-side JWT verification middleware). GitHub Actions (CI: lint, typecheck, Jest unit tests, integration tests against pg testcontainer). Grafana + Prometheus (API metrics via prom-client middleware).
target_output.workflows: Waste Log Entry: Kitchen staff opens daily log view, types ingredient name into searchable IngredientSelect component (debounced query against pre-cached ingredient list in React Query) → selects waste reason from WasteReasonSelect → enters quantity → submits POST /waste-logs → Hono middleware verifies Firebase JWT and extracts restaurant_id → WasteCalculator multiplies quantity by ingredient.unit_cost_cents → record inserted with computed dollar value → client React Query cache invalidated → entry appears in today's log. Weekly Report Generation: Cron job (DigitalOcean App Platform scheduled function, every Monday 06:00 UTC) runs OrderingSuggestionEngine SQL CTE → computes 4-week rolling avg waste rate per ingredient → upserts into ordering_suggestions table. Owner opens /reports → GET /reports/weekly?week=2026-W12 → server runs aggregation query → top 5 waste contributors returned with dollar totals and ordering suggestion deltas.
target_output.data_model: restaurants (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, owner_uid VARCHAR Cognito UID, timezone VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ingredients (id UUID PK, restaurant_id UUID FK, name VARCHAR, category ENUM produce/protein/dairy/dry_goods/beverage, unit VARCHAR, unit_cost_cents INTEGER, is_active BOOLEAN, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). waste_log_entries (id UUID PK, restaurant_id UUID FK, ingredient_id UUID FK, waste_reason ENUM spoilage/over_prep/trim/expiry/dropped, quantity DECIMAL(8,3), waste_cost_cents INTEGER computed, logged_by_uid VARCHAR, logged_at TIMESTAMPTZ). purchase_orders (id UUID PK, restaurant_id UUID FK, ingredient_id UUID FK, quantity DECIMAL(8,3), unit_cost_cents INTEGER, ordered_at TIMESTAMPTZ). ordering_suggestions (id UUID PK, restaurant_id UUID FK, ingredient_id UUID FK, suggested_qty DECIMAL(8,3), four_week_avg_waste_qty DECIMAL(8,3), computed_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: Hono REST API at /api/v1. Auth middleware on all routes verifies Firebase ID token and attaches {uid, restaurant_id, role} to context. Endpoints: POST /api/v1/waste-logs (create waste entry, returns computed waste_cost_cents), GET /api/v1/waste-logs?date=2026-03-15 (daily log for staff view), GET /api/v1/ingredients (ingredient list for IngredientSelect, cached), POST /api/v1/ingredients (owner creates ingredient), PUT /api/v1/ingredients/:id (owner updates unit cost), POST /api/v1/purchase-orders (record purchase), GET /api/v1/reports/weekly?week=2026-W12 (aggregated top-5 waste report), GET /api/v1/suggestions (ordering suggestions list).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Firebase Auth JWT verified by Firebase Admin SDK in Hono middleware on every route — no anonymous access possible. restaurant_id extracted from verified token custom claim (not from request body) and injected into all SQL WHERE clauses. Role-based route guards: kitchen_manager role blocked from /ingredients (POST/PUT), /purchase-orders, /reports, /suggestions — owner role only. HTTPS enforced by DigitalOcean App Platform. Parameterized queries via pg library prevent SQL injection. Secrets (DATABASE_URL, FIREBASE_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_JSON) stored in DigitalOcean App Platform encrypted environment variables. Integration tests assert that requests with restaurant_id A cannot retrieve data scoped to restaurant_id B.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: bun install → eslint + tsc --noEmit → Jest unit tests (WasteCalculator, OrderingSuggestionEngine) → Jest integration tests with pg testcontainer → build. Merge to main triggers DigitalOcean App Platform auto-deploy for both API and static frontend. Database migrations run via node-pg-migrate as a pre-deploy step in App Platform release command. Staging environment: separate App Platform app on staging branch with isolated Managed PostgreSQL instance. Rollback: App Platform one-click rollback to previous deploy via dashboard or doctl CLI. Managed DB daily automated backups retained for 7 days.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics exposed at /metrics via prom-client middleware on Hono: http_request_duration_seconds (histogram by route and status), waste_log_entries_created_total (counter), api_errors_total (counter by route). Grafana dashboard: API p50/p99 latency, error rate, waste log submission rate, daily active restaurants. DigitalOcean App Platform log streaming to stdout — structured JSON logs with correlation IDs. Alert: error rate > 2% over 5 minutes triggers email notification.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $27/month: DigitalOcean App Platform Basic (API) $12/month, Managed PostgreSQL Basic $15/month, Static Site free. Grafana on Basic Droplet $6/month optional. Total under $33/month — well within $80 budget. Scaling path: App Platform scales horizontally; PostgreSQL Managed upgrade to Standard plan at > 1000 daily waste log entries. React Query caching eliminates ingredient list redundant queries.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Firebase Auth integration, ingredient catalog CRUD, waste log entry with real-time cost calculation, purchase order entry, weekly cost report with Recharts top-5 bar chart, DigitalOcean App Platform deployment with CI/CD, GitHub Actions test pipeline. Phase 2 (4 weeks post-MVP): Ordering suggestion engine (SQL CTE, weekly cron), multi-user invitation with kitchen_manager role, mobile-responsive UI tuning based on kitchen staff feedback. Phase 3 (future): POS integration for automatic waste depletion (item sold = inventory decremented), multi-location support, data export to CSV.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode for both frontend and backend. Parameterized queries via pg library only — no raw string interpolation. Every API route has a corresponding Jest integration test asserting restaurant_id isolation. React Query cache keys include restaurant_id prefix. Hono route modules import only the middleware and services they need — no global state. PR requires passing CI and 1-reviewer approval before merge to main. Feature flags via environment variable for post-MVP features not yet deployed.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Cross-tenant data leak if restaurant_id is taken from request parameters instead of verified JWT claims. Mitigation: Code review checklist item verifying restaurant_id source on every new route; integration test suite validates isolation. Risk: Ordering suggestion algorithm producing misleading recommendations for ingredients with irregular purchase patterns. Mitigation: Suggestions display confidence indicator (based on number of data weeks) and are advisory, not automatically converted to purchase orders. Tradeoff: No Redis cache accepted to stay within budget and reduce operational complexity for solo developer team; revisit if query latency degrades at scale.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Firebase Auth over Auth0 for lower cost at small user count. Confirmed Hono over Express for better TypeScript types and Cloudflare Workers portability. Chose DigitalOcean App Platform over Heroku for better pricing transparency and managed PostgreSQL integration. Ordering suggestions computed via SQL CTE rather than a separate ML service to stay within solo-developer operational budget.

metadata.notes: Food waste tracking web app for independent restaurants with Firebase Auth multi-tenancy and SQL-based ordering suggestions.

project_goal.value: Decompose a monolithic ETL job processing driver GPS traces, order fulfillment events, and customer delivery ratings into discrete microservice-based pipeline stages — each independently deployable, scalable, and observable — to reduce batch processing latency and enable per-domain team ownership
project_goal.rationale: Current monolith creates deployment coupling and prevents independent scaling of high-volume GPS trace processing

target_users.value: Platform engineering teams owning GPS, fulfillment, and ratings domains; data analysts querying delivery performance metrics
target_users.rationale: Internal platform consumers — not end-user facing

project_class.rationale: Event-driven pipeline decomposition across Kafka topics with microservice processing stages

capabilities.rationale: Stream processing, batch enrichment jobs, third-party event integrations, and ClickHouse analytics

complexity_level.rationale: High-volume GPS trace ingestion at delivery aggregator scale with microservice decomposition complexity

risk_level.rationale: Driver and customer location data is personal data requiring GDPR/CCPA controls; pipeline failures affect driver performance scoring

data_sensitivity.rationale: Driver GPS traces and customer delivery addresses are personal data under GDPR and CCPA

external_exposure.rationale: Pipeline stages communicate within GCP VPC; no public endpoints

access_model.value: Service-to-service authentication via GCP Workload Identity and Kafka ACLs per topic; analyst access to ClickHouse via individual user accounts with role-based query permissions
access_model.rationale: Zero-trust service auth for pipeline stages; separate analyst access model for ClickHouse

feature_scope.value: GPS trace geospatial enrichment microservice, delivery rating aggregation microservice, order fulfillment event processing microservice, dead-letter queue handling, anomaly flagging, ClickHouse warehouse writes, per-service observability
feature_scope.rationale: Full decomposition scope of the existing monolith pipeline stages

mvp_scope.value: GPS trace enrichment service and delivery rating aggregation service deployed independently, with the fulfillment event processor following in Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: GPS and ratings are highest-value domains for independent scaling

security_baseline.value: Kafka mTLS between producers and brokers, topic-level ACLs per microservice, driver GPS data pseudonymized at pipeline entry, TLS 1.3 for all inter-service HTTP
security_baseline.rationale: Defense in depth for personal location data in transit and at pipeline boundaries

frontend_stack.value: N/A - data pipeline with no frontend component
frontend_stack.rationale: Pipeline processes internal events; no UI required

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Kafka Streams (Faust-streaming) per microservice
backend_stack.rationale: Python ML-ecosystem compatibility for geospatial enrichment; Faust for Kafka stream processing

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for analytics warehouse writes; Redis 7 for processing state and idempotency keys; Kafka MSK equivalent on GCP (Confluent Cloud)
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse for high-throughput analytical writes; Redis for join windows and deduplication

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot for all microservice containers
hosting_target.rationale: Kubernetes for independent scaling of each pipeline stage

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw GPS trace events purged after 90 days; pseudonymized route segments retained 2 years; customer delivery address hashed before ClickHouse writes; GDPR erasure jobs run weekly
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR data minimization and retention limits for personal location data

future_scope.value: Real-time driver ETA prediction service, customer delivery experience scoring model, PII tokenization service replacing manual pseudonymization
future_scope.rationale: Next-generation services enabled by clean microservice decomposition

constraints.value: Must maintain 100% data parity with monolith output during migration period; GPS enrichment P95 latency under 2 seconds; no data loss on service restart (Kafka offset commit after ClickHouse write)
constraints.rationale: Zero-regression migration constraint and latency SLA

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for per-service metrics, Kafka consumer lag dashboards, ClickHouse write latency tracking
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard observability for Kafka-based pipeline systems

execution_preference.value: Strangler fig migration: deploy new microservices consuming same Kafka topics as monolith, validate output parity before decommissioning monolith stage
execution_preference.rationale: Lowest-risk migration pattern for production pipeline

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 25 data minimization for driver location processing; CCPA consumer data rights for customer delivery data; driver data processing under employment/contractor data agreements; pseudonymization required before cross-service data sharing
compliance_context.rationale: Personal data of both drivers (GPS) and customers (delivery ratings and addresses) triggers GDPR and CCPA obligations

project.goal: Decompose monolithic ETL into independently deployable Kafka microservice pipeline stages
project.target_users: Platform engineering and data analytics teams

backend.framework: Faust-streaming for Kafka stream processing per microservice

security.auth: GCP Workload Identity for service-to-service; Kafka mTLS + ACLs

data.cache: Redis 7 for join windows and idempotency

reasoner_reviews.product: The strangler-fig migration pattern is correct for production pipeline decomposition — running new services in parallel and validating parity before decommissioning eliminates data loss risk. The MVP scope (GPS enrichment + ratings aggregation) covers the two highest-volume, highest-value pipeline stages and provides the team with deployment independence for those domains immediately.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Faust-streaming on Python is the right choice given the ML-ecosystem geospatial enrichment requirements. The GPS enrichment service should use a road network graph library (osrm-backend or shapely with OSM data) for coordinate-to-segment mapping — this must be pre-loaded into service memory at startup, not queried per-event, to hit the 2-second P95 requirement. The delivery rating aggregation join window of GPS completion event + customer rating should use Redis sorted sets with a 15-minute timeout to handle delivery-to-rating event lag.
reasoner_reviews.security: Driver GPS data must be pseudonymized at the Kafka consumer entry point of each microservice — the raw driver_id replaced with a HMAC-SHA256 pseudonym derived from a rotation key — before any processing or ClickHouse writes. This limits PII exposure to the topic boundary. Kafka ACLs must restrict each microservice to only its consumer group and produce topics; cross-service Kafka access is prohibited.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 100% data parity validation requires a comparison harness running both old monolith output and new microservice output through a diff check on a sampled subset of records before promoting. The harness should be part of the CI/CD pipeline gate, not a manual step.

backend.service_design: Three independently deployed Faust microservices on GKE: gps-enrichment-svc (consumes raw-gps-traces topic, outputs enriched-gps-segments topic), delivery-rating-svc (consumes order-fulfillment-events and customer-rating-events topics, joins on order_id, outputs delivery-quality-records topic), fulfillment-event-svc (Phase 2). Each service has its own Kafka consumer group, Faust app instance, and GKE Deployment.
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API — purely Kafka event-driven. Services expose Prometheus metrics endpoint on :8080/metrics. Health check on :8080/health returns Kafka consumer lag and last processed offset.
backend.failure_handling: Dead-letter topics: gps-enrichment-svc.DLT and delivery-rating-svc.DLT receive events that fail schema validation or geospatial enrichment. Prometheus alert fires when DLT consumer lag exceeds 1000 events. Kafka offset committed only after successful ClickHouse write (at-least-once delivery with idempotency key in Redis to deduplicate retries).
backend.scaling_notes: GPS enrichment scales horizontally by Kafka partition count (currently 32 partitions on raw-gps-traces). Each GKE pod handles one partition; HPA scales pods on Kafka consumer lag metric via KEDA.

security.auth_design: GCP Workload Identity assigned per GKE ServiceAccount per microservice. Confluent Cloud Kafka uses mTLS certificates rotated every 90 days via cert-manager on GKE. Each service has a dedicated Kafka service account with ACLs permitting only its consumer group and output topics.
security.authorization_model: Kafka ACLs: gps-enrichment-svc READ on raw-gps-traces, WRITE on enriched-gps-segments and gps-enrichment-svc.DLT. delivery-rating-svc READ on order-fulfillment-events and customer-rating-events, WRITE on delivery-quality-records.
security.privacy_controls: Driver GPS pseudonymization: HMAC-SHA256 of driver_id using rotation key from GCP Secret Manager, applied at Faust agent entry before any processing. Customer delivery addresses hashed with SHA-256 before ClickHouse writes. Pseudonymization key rotation every 30 days with dual-key overlap window.

data.entities: driver_gps_traces topic: {trace_id, driver_id_raw, lat, lng, speed_kmh, heading, timestamp}. enriched_gps_segments topic: {segment_id, driver_id_pseudonym, road_segment_id, segment_distance_m, route_efficiency_score, from_ts, to_ts}. order_fulfillment_events topic: {order_id, driver_id_pseudonym, restaurant_id, pickup_ts, dropoff_ts, delivery_duration_s}. delivery_quality_records ClickHouse table: (order_id, driver_id_pseudonym, delivery_duration_s, rating_score FLOAT, rating_ts, route_efficiency_score, anomaly_flag BOOLEAN, processed_at).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse: delivery_quality_records table with MergeTree engine, partitioned by toYYYYMM(processed_at), ordered by (driver_id_pseudonym, processed_at). Redis: sorted sets keyed by order_id for rating join window (score = fulfillment event timestamp, TTL 15 minutes). Idempotency keys stored as Redis SET entries with 24-hour TTL.
data.retention_and_deletion: Kafka topics: raw-gps-traces retention 7 days, enriched-gps-segments 30 days. ClickHouse: delivery_quality_records TTL processed_at + 730 DAYS (2 years). Weekly GDPR erasure job runs SELECT DISTINCT driver_id_pseudonym WHERE erasure_requested, then ALTERs ClickHouse rows to NULL driver field.

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot cluster in GCP us-central1. Each microservice as separate GKE Deployment with dedicated ServiceAccount. KEDA for Kafka consumer lag-based autoscaling. Confluent Cloud Kafka in same GCP region for low-latency topic access.
devops.ci_cd_design: CircleCI pipeline: unit tests (pytest), integration tests against local Kafka via Testcontainers, Docker build, push to Artifact Registry, GKE rolling deploy via kubectl. Parity validation harness runs as CI job before promoting new service version to production.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scrapes /metrics from each service pod. Grafana dashboards: Kafka consumer lag per topic/partition, GPS enrichment P95 latency, delivery rating join success rate, DLT queue depth. Alert: consumer lag >10K events OR DLT depth >500 triggers PagerDuty.

target_output.thinking_summary: Decomposing a high-volume monolithic ETL into independent Kafka microservices using the strangler-fig pattern. The key architectural decisions are: Faust-streaming for Python-native Kafka processing compatible with geospatial enrichment libraries; KEDA for Kafka consumer lag-based autoscaling so GPS enrichment scales linearly with partition count; driver GPS pseudonymization at Kafka consumer entry to limit PII exposure across services; and at-least-once delivery with Redis idempotency keys to guarantee no data loss on service restart without requiring exactly-once Kafka semantics.
target_output.title: Food Delivery Data Pipeline Microservices Decomposition Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A microservices decomposition of a monolithic food delivery ETL pipeline, splitting GPS trace enrichment, order fulfillment processing, and delivery rating aggregation into three independently deployable Faust-streaming Python services on GKE Autopilot, consuming from and producing to Confluent Cloud Kafka topics. ClickHouse receives enriched delivery quality records for analytics queries. Driver GPS data is pseudonymized at service entry using HMAC-SHA256 before any processing or storage. A strangler-fig migration strategy runs new services in parallel with the monolith while a parity validation CI harness confirms output equivalence before decommissioning each monolith stage.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven microservices architecture on GCP GKE Autopilot with Confluent Cloud Kafka as the backbone. Three Faust-streaming Python services — gps-enrichment-svc, delivery-rating-svc, and fulfillment-event-svc (Phase 2) — each own a Kafka consumer group and a set of output topics. Services are decoupled: GPS enrichment reads raw-gps-traces and writes enriched-gps-segments; delivery rating service reads both order-fulfillment-events and customer-rating-events topics, joins on order_id using Redis sorted sets as a 15-minute window store, and writes delivery-quality-records to ClickHouse. KEDA scales each service's GKE Deployment based on Kafka consumer lag per partition, enabling GPS enrichment to scale to 32 parallel pods matching the topic partition count under peak lunch/dinner delivery volumes. All inter-service communication is Kafka-mediated; no synchronous HTTP between pipeline stages.
target_output.technology_stack: Stream processing: Python 3.12, Faust-streaming 0.10, confluent-kafka-python driver. Geospatial enrichment: shapely, pyproj, OSM road network data pre-loaded to service memory. Analytics sink: ClickHouse client (clickhouse-connect 0.7). Join window store: Redis 7 via redis-py async client. Kubernetes: GKE Autopilot, KEDA 2.14 for lag-based HPA. Container images: python:3.12-slim base. CI/CD: CircleCI with Testcontainers for local Kafka integration tests. Observability: Prometheus client_python, Grafana. Secret management: GCP Secret Manager via Workload Identity.
target_output.functional_feature_map: GPS Trace Enrichment Service: consumes raw driver GPS coordinate events, maps coordinate sequences to OSM road segments using pre-loaded network graph, calculates route efficiency scores (actual distance / optimal distance), pseudonymizes driver_id at entry, writes enriched segments to Kafka and summary metrics to ClickHouse. Delivery Rating Aggregation Service: consumes order fulfillment completion events and customer 1-5 star rating webhook events, joins on order_id within 15-minute Redis window, computes delivery quality record with duration, efficiency score, and rating, flags anomalies (rating 1 with >90-min delivery), writes to ClickHouse delivery_quality_records. Dead-letter handling: schema validation failures and enrichment errors route to service-specific DLT topics for reprocessing after manual investigation. Parity validation harness: CI job that runs monolith and new service outputs against same sample batch and diffs records.
target_output.system_components: Confluent Cloud Kafka (GCP us-central1): topics — raw-gps-traces (32 partitions, 7-day retention), enriched-gps-segments (32 partitions, 30-day retention), order-fulfillment-events (16 partitions), customer-rating-events (8 partitions), delivery-quality-records (16 partitions), DLT topics per service. GKE Autopilot: gps-enrichment-svc Deployment (KEDA HPA on raw-gps-traces lag), delivery-rating-svc Deployment (KEDA HPA on fulfillment event lag). Memorystore Redis 7: join window store and idempotency key cache. ClickHouse (self-managed on GKE or ClickHouse Cloud): delivery_quality_records MergeTree table, partitioned by processing month. GCP Secret Manager: Kafka mTLS certificates, pseudonymization HMAC keys. Prometheus + Grafana: per-service metrics and lag dashboards.
target_output.workflows: GPS Trace Enrichment Flow: Driver app emits GPS coordinate event to raw-gps-traces Kafka topic. gps-enrichment-svc Faust agent consumes event, applies driver_id HMAC pseudonymization using rotation key from GCP Secret Manager, maps lat/lng sequence to nearest OSM road segments using pre-loaded shapely network graph, computes route efficiency score, produces enriched-gps-segments event, commits Kafka offset after Redis idempotency key write, then batches ClickHouse writes every 500 events or 5 seconds. Delivery Rating Aggregation Flow: Order fulfillment completion event arrives on order-fulfillment-events topic; delivery-rating-svc Faust agent stores event in Redis sorted set keyed by order_id with score=timestamp and TTL 15 minutes. Customer rating webhook event arrives on customer-rating-events topic; service performs Redis GET for matching order_id, joins records, computes delivery quality record with anomaly flag logic (rating=1 AND duration > 5400s), writes to delivery-quality-records ClickHouse table with idempotency check, commits Kafka offset. Unmatched fulfillment events (no rating within 15 min) write with rating_score=NULL and anomaly_flag=NULL for analyst review.
target_output.data_model: raw-gps-traces Kafka event schema: {trace_id VARCHAR, driver_id VARCHAR (pseudonymized downstream), lat DOUBLE, lng DOUBLE, speed_kmh FLOAT, heading SMALLINT, event_ts TIMESTAMPTZ}. enriched-gps-segments Kafka event: {segment_id VARCHAR, driver_id_pseudonym VARCHAR, road_segment_osm_id BIGINT, segment_distance_m FLOAT, route_efficiency_score FLOAT (0.0-1.0), segment_start_ts TIMESTAMPTZ, segment_end_ts TIMESTAMPTZ}. delivery_quality_records ClickHouse table: (order_id VARCHAR, driver_id_pseudonym VARCHAR, restaurant_id VARCHAR, delivery_duration_s INT, route_efficiency_score FLOAT nullable, rating_score FLOAT nullable, rating_ts TIMESTAMPTZ nullable, anomaly_flag BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, anomaly_reason VARCHAR nullable, processed_at TIMESTAMPTZ) ENGINE=MergeTree() PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(processed_at) ORDER BY (driver_id_pseudonym, processed_at) TTL processed_at + INTERVAL 2 YEAR. processing_checkpoints table: (service_name VARCHAR, topic VARCHAR, partition INT, last_committed_offset BIGINT, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: No external HTTP API — purely Kafka event-driven. Internal management endpoints per service: GET :8080/health (Kafka consumer connection status, last_processed_offset, last_processed_ts), GET :8080/metrics (Prometheus exposition format: kafka_consumer_lag, gps_enrichment_latency_seconds histogram, enrichment_errors_total, dlq_events_total, clickhouse_write_duration_seconds). ClickHouse analytics access: read-only SQL over ClickHouse HTTP interface on port 8123, access controlled by ClickHouse user accounts with per-role SELECT grants on delivery_quality_records.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Kafka: Confluent Cloud with mTLS client certificates per service, rotated every 90 days via cert-manager. Topic ACLs: each service principal restricted to READ on its consumer topics and WRITE on its output topics — no cross-service topic access. Driver GPS pseudonymization: HMAC-SHA256 of raw driver_id using rotation key from GCP Secret Manager; applied in Faust agent before any record processing. Key rotation every 30 days with 7-day dual-key overlap window to allow backfill processing. Customer delivery address: SHA-256 hashed before ClickHouse writes — no plaintext addresses in analytics tables. GCP Workload Identity per GKE ServiceAccount — no static credentials. GDPR weekly erasure job: scans deletion_requests table, ALTERs delivery_quality_records to set driver_id_pseudonym='ERASED' for matching pseudonyms. CCPA data inventory maintained in BigQuery for consumer right-to-know requests.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot cluster us-central1. Each microservice deployed as independent Kubernetes Deployment with dedicated ServiceAccount, ConfigMap for Faust config, and SecretProviderClass mounting GCP Secret Manager secrets as volumes. KEDA ScaledObject per service: lagThreshold 1000 per partition for gps-enrichment-svc, lagThreshold 500 for delivery-rating-svc. CircleCI pipeline: on push to main — pytest unit tests with Testcontainers Kafka + Redis, mypy type checks, Docker build, push to Artifact Registry, GKE rolling update with maxUnavailable=0. Parity harness CI job runs against staging before promoting to production: samples 10K records from both monolith and new service outputs, runs structural diff, fails build if >0.1% divergence. Rollback: GKE rollout undo to previous image within 60 seconds.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics per service: kafka_consumer_lag (partition label), record_processing_duration_seconds (P50/P95/P99 histogram), enrichment_errors_total (error_type label), dlq_events_total (service label), clickhouse_write_batch_size histogram, redis_join_window_hits_total vs redis_join_window_misses_total. Grafana dashboards: consumer lag heatmap by partition, GPS enrichment throughput events/sec, delivery rating join success rate %, DLT queue depth time series. Alerts: consumer lag >50K events sustained 5 minutes (PagerDuty P1), DLT depth >5000 (PagerDuty P2), pseudonymization key within 7 days of rotation deadline (Slack reminder).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot: node provisioning per pod request; GPS enrichment at full scale (32 pods) ~$800/month in compute. Confluent Cloud Kafka: ~$400/month for throughput tier covering peak GPS volume. ClickHouse Cloud: ~$300/month for delivery_quality_records at 100M records/month write volume. Redis Memorystore 1GB: ~$30/month. Total: ~$1530/month at full scale. Scaling path: KEDA allows GPS enrichment to scale from 2 to 32 pods automatically; ClickHouse scales storage independently of compute.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Migration (6 weeks): Deploy gps-enrichment-svc alongside monolith consuming same raw-gps-traces topic. Run parity harness for 2 weeks comparing outputs. Validate KEDA autoscaling under simulated peak load. Decommission monolith GPS stage. Phase 2 (4 weeks): Deploy delivery-rating-svc. Validate Redis join window behavior against monolith rating aggregation output. Implement GDPR erasure job. Decommission monolith rating stage. Phase 3 (6 weeks): fulfillment-event-svc, real-time driver ETA prediction service integration, PII tokenization service replacing manual HMAC pseudonymization.
target_output.development_guidelines: Each microservice is an independent Python package with its own pyproject.toml, Dockerfile, and CircleCI config. No shared code between services except a common pip package for Kafka schema definitions and the pseudonymization HMAC utility. Integration tests use Testcontainers (Kafka + Redis + ClickHouse) — no mocking of external dependencies in integration test suite. Faust agents must be idempotent: every agent checks Redis idempotency key before processing. Dead-letter topic consumers must be deployed alongside main consumers in production — never leave DLT topics unmonitored. All schema changes require backward-compatible Avro schema evolution before deploying the new consumer.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: OSM road network graph pre-loaded into GPS enrichment service memory (~2GB) delays pod startup time and increases memory requests. Mitigation: pre-warm pod pool with min-replicas 2; use GKE node pool with sufficient memory (n2-standard-4). Risk: Redis join window TTL too short for slow customer rating submissions; ratings arriving >15 minutes post-delivery lost as unmatched. Mitigation: DLT topic captures unmatched events; nightly batch job attempts late-join against ClickHouse for events within 24 hours. Tradeoff: Faust-streaming over Apache Flink for GPS enrichment — Python ecosystem advantage for geospatial libraries at the cost of lower throughput ceiling than JVM Flink; acceptable at current GPS event volume. Tradeoff: at-least-once delivery with Redis deduplication over Kafka exactly-once transactions — simpler operational model at the cost of Redis as a correctness dependency.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Faust-streaming over Kafka Streams (JVM) for Python geospatial library compatibility. Chose Redis sorted sets for join window over Faust's built-in table for explicit TTL control and cross-pod visibility. Selected strangler-fig over big-bang cutover to eliminate data loss risk. Confirmed ClickHouse over BigQuery for sub-second analytical query performance on driver performance dashboards.

metadata.notes: High-scale food delivery pipeline microservices decomposition; GPS pseudonymization and strangler-fig migration are key architectural decisions. Marked weak as several sections are at minimum detail level.

project_goal.value: API-first data pipeline aggregating customer review data from Google Maps, Yelp, TripAdvisor, and OpenTable, normalizing ratings and sentiment across platforms, and exposing a unified REST API for reputation management dashboards
project_goal.rationale: Restaurant groups need a single view of reputation across all review platforms

target_users.value: Restaurant group marketing and operations teams consuming aggregated reputation data via API
target_users.rationale: B2B API consumers, not end consumers

project_class.rationale: Core purpose is ingestion, normalization, and exposure of review data — not a user-facing app

capabilities.rationale: Scheduled pull ingestion, platform API integrations, normalized data storage, and REST API output

complexity_level.rationale: Multiple platform integrations with normalization logic, scheduled jobs, and a REST API layer — moderate complexity

risk_level.rationale: No payment data, no health data — internal business intelligence use case

data_sensitivity.rationale: Review data is publicly sourced but treated as internal business intelligence

external_exposure.rationale: REST API requires API key authentication; not publicly accessible

access_model.value: API key authentication per restaurant group client; keys scoped to specific location_ids
access_model.rationale: Multi-tenant API with location-level data isolation

feature_scope.value: Scheduled review pull from 4 platforms, star rating normalization to unified 1-5 scale, sentiment scoring via text analysis, webhook registration for push notification on new ingestion, cursor-based paginated REST API
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for v1 reputation pipeline

mvp_scope.value: Google Maps and Yelp ingestion, rating normalization, basic sentiment scoring, GET /reviews and GET /reputation-scores endpoints
mvp_scope.rationale: Two highest-volume platforms for MVP with core API endpoints

security_baseline.value: API key authentication with key rotation support, TLS 1.3, rate limiting per API key, no raw review text with personal names exposed in API without data masking
security_baseline.rationale: Protect platform API credentials and manage API key lifecycle

frontend_stack.value: None — API-only service
frontend_stack.rationale: data_pipeline with public_api capability; no UI component

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI on GCP Cloud Run
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async performance for concurrent platform API calls; Cloud Run for serverless scaling

data_platform.value: MongoDB 7 for review document storage; Redis 7 for API response caching and rate limit counters
data_platform.rationale: Document store suits variable review schemas across platforms; Redis for low-latency API caching

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Run with Cloud Scheduler for batch jobs
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless containers for API; Cloud Scheduler for 4-hour ingestion cadence

privacy_retention_policy.value: Raw review documents retained 24 months rolling; aggregated reputation scores retained indefinitely; reviews containing reviewer full names anonymized after 90 days per GDPR data minimization
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR data minimization for personally identifying reviewer data

future_scope.value: TripAdvisor and OpenTable platform additions, ML-based topic extraction from review text, sentiment trend alerting
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 platform expansion and analytics depth

constraints.value: Platform API rate limits must be respected — Google Maps 50 QPS, Yelp 5 QPS, TripAdvisor 10 QPS; GCP budget under $600/month
constraints.rationale: Platform ToS compliance and budget constraint

observability_baseline.value: Google Cloud Monitoring for Cloud Run metrics, structured logs with review ingestion counts and sentiment scoring latency, alerting on platform API error rates
observability_baseline.rationale: Need visibility into ingestion health per platform

execution_preference.value: API-first development: design and document OpenAPI spec before implementation
execution_preference.rationale: Dashboard consumers need stable API contract before starting integration

compliance_context.value: GDPR data minimization for reviewer personal names in stored review data; platform API Terms of Service compliance for data aggregation and storage durations
compliance_context.rationale: GDPR and platform ToS are the primary compliance constraints

project.goal: Unified review aggregation pipeline and REST API for restaurant reputation management
project.target_users: Restaurant group marketing and ops teams via API

backend.framework: FastAPI with async httpx for platform API calls

security.auth: API key in Authorization header, key stored hashed in MongoDB
security.rate_limiting: 100 requests/minute per API key via Redis counters

data.cache: Redis 7 Memorystore for API response caching and rate limit counters
data.ingestion: Cloud Scheduler triggering Cloud Run jobs every 4 hours

devops.environments: dev (manual trigger), staging, production

confirmed_decisions.sentiment: simple VADER-based scoring for MVP, upgrade to dedicated model in Phase 2

reasoner_reviews.product: The 4-hour pull cadence is a practical compromise given Yelp's 5 QPS rate limit and the number of locations to cover. Webhook support for near-real-time updates should be scoped to platforms that offer push notification (OpenTable has webhooks; Google and Yelp do not). The normalized 1-5 star scale must account for TripAdvisor's 1-5 bubble rating and Yelp's 1-5 half-star granularity.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI with async httpx is well-suited for concurrent platform API calls within the ingestion job. The ingestion pipeline should use a per-platform adapter pattern so each platform's quirks are isolated. MongoDB document storage suits the varying review schema across platforms without schema migrations on platform changes. Redis for API response caching with a configurable TTL avoids redundant MongoDB queries for hot reputation score requests.
reasoner_reviews.security: API keys must be stored as bcrypt hashes in MongoDB — never in plaintext. Key rotation requires issuing a new key with an overlap grace period before revoking the old one. Data masking for reviewer full names should be applied at write time to MongoDB documents, not at read time, to ensure GDPR compliance even if the API layer is bypassed.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Yelp's 5 QPS limit with potentially 500+ locations requires token bucket rate limiting in the ingestion adapter with a distributed lock via Redis to prevent multiple Cloud Run instances from exceeding the limit. GCP budget of $600/month is achievable: Cloud Run scales to zero when not processing, MongoDB Atlas M10 cluster ~$57/month, Redis Memorystore Basic ~$30/month.

backend.service_design: Two Cloud Run services: ingestion-worker (triggered by Cloud Scheduler) and api-service (always-warm minimum 1 instance). Shared platform adapter package for Google Maps, Yelp, TripAdvisor, OpenTable.
backend.api_patterns: GET /v1/reviews?location_id=X&platform=Y&since=ISO8601&cursor=Z (cursor-based pagination over MongoDB _id). GET /v1/reputation-scores?location_id=X&period=30d. POST /v1/webhooks (register push notification endpoint). DELETE /v1/webhooks/{id}.
backend.background_jobs: Cloud Scheduler triggers ingestion-worker every 4 hours; ingestion-worker iterates location_profiles with active platform credentials, calls platform adapters with rate limiting, normalizes and upserts to MongoDB.
backend.failure_handling: Platform API failures logged with error_code and retry queued for next scheduled run; partial ingestion failures (some locations succeed) do not abort the full job; dead-letter entries tracked in aggregation_runs collection

security.auth_design: API key extracted from Authorization: ApiKey {key} header; bcrypt hash lookup in MongoDB api_keys collection; failed key lookups rate-limited to 10 attempts/minute per IP
security.secrets_management: Platform API credentials (Google Maps API key, Yelp Client ID/Secret, etc.) stored in GCP Secret Manager; injected as environment variables into Cloud Run at deploy time
security.abuse_prevention: Redis sliding window rate limiter per API key — 100 requests/minute; 429 response with Retry-After header

data.entities: review_sources (id, platform ENUM google/yelp/tripadvisor/opentable, location_id, external_location_id, credentials_secret_ref, last_polled_at, active). raw_reviews (MongoDB document: _id, source_id, platform, external_review_id, reviewer_name_masked, rating_original, rating_normalized FLOAT, review_text, sentiment_score FLOAT, ingested_at, review_date). normalized_reviews (computed view or aggregation pipeline over raw_reviews). sentiment_scores (id, location_id, platform, period_start, period_end, avg_rating FLOAT, avg_sentiment FLOAT, review_count INT, computed_at). aggregation_runs (id, run_at, platform, locations_attempted, locations_succeeded, reviews_ingested, errors_json).
data.retention_and_deletion: TTL index on raw_reviews.ingested_at for 24-month rolling deletion; reviewer_name_masked field populated at write time by replacing full names with initials + redacted marker after 90 days via scheduled Mongo aggregation pipeline

devops.deployment_topology: Two Cloud Run services in us-central1: ingestion-worker (min 0, max 3 instances, triggered by Cloud Scheduler) and api-service (min 1, max 5, HTTP traffic). MongoDB Atlas M10 cluster in GCP us-central1. Redis Memorystore Basic 1GB in same region.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest with mock platform adapters, FastAPI integration tests against MongoDB Atlas dev cluster, Docker build and push to GCP Artifact Registry, Cloud Run deploy via gcloud run deploy with traffic split for canary
devops.observability_stack: Google Cloud Monitoring: Cloud Run request count, latency, error rate; custom metrics: reviews_ingested_per_run, platform_api_error_rate, sentiment_scoring_latency; Cloud Logging structured JSON

target_output.thinking_summary: This is an API-first data pipeline with two primary concerns: reliable multi-platform review ingestion with rate limit compliance, and a clean normalized REST API for downstream dashboard consumers. The platform adapter pattern isolates each integration. MongoDB is well-suited for the variable review schemas. Key design decisions: VADER sentiment for MVP simplicity, bcrypt-hashed API keys stored in MongoDB, per-platform rate limiting via Redis token buckets, and two separate Cloud Run services for ingestion and API to allow independent scaling.
target_output.title: Restaurant Review Aggregation Pipeline and Reputation API
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 FastAPI data pipeline on GCP Cloud Run that ingests customer reviews from Google Maps, Yelp, TripAdvisor, and OpenTable on a 4-hour scheduled cadence, normalizes star ratings to a unified 1-5 float scale, computes VADER-based sentiment scores, and exposes aggregated reputation data via an authenticated REST API with cursor-based pagination and webhook registration. Two Cloud Run services handle ingestion and API responsibilities independently. MongoDB 7 Atlas stores review documents with GDPR-compliant reviewer name masking applied at write time.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two Cloud Run services in GCP us-central1 share a MongoDB 7 Atlas M10 cluster and a Redis 7 Memorystore instance.
