The ingestion-worker service is triggered every 4 hours by Cloud Scheduler; it iterates active review_sources, calls per-platform adapters (Google Maps Places API, Yelp Fusion API, TripAdvisor Content API, OpenTable Reviews API) with Redis-backed token bucket rate limiting, normalizes the rating and sentiment fields, and upserts raw_review documents to MongoDB. The api-service handles REST API traffic: GET /v1/reviews and GET /v1/reputation-scores with cursor-based pagination, API key authentication via bcrypt-hashed key lookup, and Redis-cached reputation score responses with a 15-minute TTL. Platform API credentials are injected from GCP Secret Manager at Cloud Run deploy time. GDPR reviewer name masking is applied at write time via the ingestion worker normalizer.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, async SQLAlchemy-equivalent via Motor (async MongoDB driver), VADER SentimentIntensityAnalyzer for sentiment scoring, async httpx for platform API calls. Database: MongoDB 7 Atlas M10 in GCP us-central1. Cache: Redis 7 GCP Memorystore Basic. Hosting: GCP Cloud Run (ingestion-worker and api-service), Cloud Scheduler for 4-hour triggers. Secrets: GCP Secret Manager. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with pytest and Docker. Monitoring: Google Cloud Monitoring with custom metrics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Review Ingestion: scheduled pull from 4 platform adapters with per-platform rate limiting, rating normalization to 1-5 float, VADER sentiment scoring, reviewer name masking, upsert to MongoDB with external_review_id deduplication. Reputation Score Computation: aggregation pipeline over raw_reviews computing avg_rating, avg_sentiment, review_count by location and period, stored in sentiment_scores collection. REST API: GET /v1/reviews with cursor pagination, platform filter, and date range; GET /v1/reputation-scores with period aggregation; POST /v1/webhooks to register push notification endpoints for near-real-time update callbacks. API Key Management: issuance, bcrypt storage, rotation with grace period, revocation.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Scheduler (4-hour cron trigger), ingestion-worker Cloud Run service (platform adapters: GoogleMapsAdapter, YelpAdapter, TripAdvisorAdapter, OpenTableAdapter; ReviewNormalizer; SentimentScorer; Redis token bucket rate limiter), api-service Cloud Run service (FastAPI routers: reviews, reputation, webhooks, admin; API key middleware; Redis response cache), MongoDB Atlas M10 (raw_reviews, sentiment_scores, review_sources, aggregation_runs, api_keys, webhook_registrations collections), Redis Memorystore (rate limit counters, reputation score cache, ingestion distributed lock), GCP Secret Manager (platform API credentials).
target_output.workflows: Scheduled Review Ingestion: Cloud Scheduler fires POST trigger to ingestion-worker every 4 hours. Worker loads active review_sources from MongoDB, groups by platform. For each platform group, acquires a distributed Redis lock to prevent parallel runs exceeding rate limits. GoogleMapsAdapter calls Places API /v1/{place_id}/reviews with token bucket at 50 QPS; YelpAdapter calls Fusion API /businesses/{id}/reviews at 5 QPS. Each raw response is passed through ReviewNormalizer: rating_normalized = raw_rating / platform_max_stars * 5.0; SentimentScorer.score(review_text) returns compound float via VADER; reviewer_name field replaced with initials + [REDACTED] marker. Upsert to raw_reviews keyed on (platform, external_review_id). aggregation_runs document written with counts and any error details. API Request Flow: Client sends GET /v1/reputation-scores?location_id=abc&period=30d with Authorization: ApiKey {key} header. API key middleware extracts key, computes bcrypt hash, looks up in api_keys collection, checks key_scope includes location_id. Cache key constructed as rep_score:{location_id}:{period}. On Redis cache hit returns cached response. On miss, queries sentiment_scores collection for matching document, serializes, writes to Redis with 900s TTL, returns to client.
target_output.data_model: review_sources ({_id, platform: 'google'|'yelp'|'tripadvisor'|'opentable', location_id, location_name, external_location_id, credentials_secret_ref, last_polled_at, active: bool}). raw_reviews ({_id ObjectId, source_id ref, platform, external_review_id, reviewer_name_masked, rating_original: float, rating_normalized: float 1.0-5.0, review_text, sentiment_score: float -1.0 to 1.0, ingested_at, review_date, ttl_expires_at} — index on (location_id, platform, review_date), unique index on (platform, external_review_id)). sentiment_scores ({_id, location_id, platform: string|'all', period_start, period_end, avg_rating: float, avg_sentiment: float, review_count: int, computed_at}). aggregation_runs ({_id, run_at, platform, locations_attempted, locations_succeeded, reviews_ingested, error_details: []}). api_keys ({_id, client_name, key_hash, key_prefix, scoped_location_ids: [], issued_at, expires_at, active}). webhook_registrations ({_id, api_key_id, url, events: ['new_reviews_ingested'], location_ids: [], created_at, active}).
target_output.api_design: All endpoints under /v1/, TLS 1.3, Authorization: ApiKey {key} required. GET /v1/reviews?location_id=X&platform=google&since=2026-01-01&limit=50&cursor=Y — returns {reviews: [...], next_cursor, total_in_period}. GET /v1/reputation-scores?location_id=X&period=30d — returns {location_id, period, avg_rating, avg_sentiment, review_count, by_platform: [{platform, avg_rating, review_count}]}. GET /v1/reputation-scores/trend?location_id=X&interval=week&periods=12 — returns weekly avg_rating series. POST /v1/webhooks {url, events, location_ids} — registers push notification endpoint. DELETE /v1/webhooks/{id}. GET /v1/ingestion-status?platform=yelp — returns last aggregation_run for platform. POST /v1/admin/api-keys (admin scope only) — issues new API key, returns {key_prefix, raw_key} once only.
target_output.security_and_compliance: API key authentication: keys issued as 32-byte random hex strings, stored as bcrypt hash in api_keys collection; raw key presented once at issuance. Key rotation: new key issued with 7-day overlap period before old key expires. Redis sliding window rate limiter: 100 requests/minute per API key; returns 429 with Retry-After header. GDPR reviewer name masking applied at ingestion write time by ReviewNormalizer — reviewer_name_masked stores initials + [REDACTED] marker; never stores full reviewer names. TTL index on raw_reviews expires documents after 24 months. GDPR deletion requests handled by location_id-scoped delete operation on raw_reviews. Platform API credentials in GCP Secret Manager with automated 90-day rotation reminders. TLS 1.3 enforced on all Cloud Run ingress. Cloud Audit Logs capture all Secret Manager access.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: pytest (unit tests for all platform adapters with VCR cassette recordings of API responses, integration tests against MongoDB Atlas dev cluster), Docker build of ingestion-worker and api-service images, push to GCP Artifact Registry, Cloud Run deploy with --no-traffic flag for canary validation before traffic shift. Cloud Run ingestion-worker: min 0 instances (scales to zero between runs), max 3 instances, timeout 3600s, 2GB memory. api-service: min 1 instance (warm), max 5 instances, HTTP/2 enabled. MongoDB Atlas M10 with automated backups daily, point-in-time recovery 7 days. Redis Memorystore Basic 1GB. Secrets rotated via GCP Secret Manager with Cloud Run automatic secret version pickup on next revision deploy.
target_output.observability: Google Cloud Monitoring custom metrics: reviews_ingested_per_run (counter by platform), platform_api_error_rate (ratio of failed requests per platform per run), sentiment_scoring_latency_ms (histogram), api_response_time_ms (histogram per endpoint). Cloud Logging structured JSON with fields: run_id, platform, location_id, reviews_ingested, error_code. Alert policies: platform_api_error_rate >20% for any platform fires PagerDuty; api_service 5xx rate >5% for 5 minutes fires alert; ingestion-worker missing expected Cloud Scheduler trigger (missed run detection via Cloud Monitoring absence alert). GCP Trace for distributed tracing of ingestion-worker platform adapter calls.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $480/month: MongoDB Atlas M10 ~$57, Redis Memorystore Basic ~$30, Cloud Run CPU (ingestion-worker 6 runs/day x 10 min x 3 locations batches ~$40, api-service always-warm 1 instance ~$100), Cloud Scheduler $0.10/job/month, GCP networking egress ~$20. Well under $600/month budget. Scaling to 500 locations increases ingestion CPU but Cloud Run auto-scales horizontally; Yelp 5 QPS limit becomes the binding constraint at scale — mitigated by batching locations and distributing across multiple 4-hour windows.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Google Maps and Yelp ingestion adapters, ReviewNormalizer with 1-5 rating normalization, VADER sentiment scoring, MongoDB raw_reviews storage with GDPR name masking, GET /v1/reviews and GET /v1/reputation-scores endpoints with API key auth, Cloud Scheduler 4-hour trigger, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 (6 weeks): TripAdvisor and OpenTable platform adapters, webhook registration and push notification delivery, GET /v1/reputation-scores/trend endpoint, Redis API response caching for hot endpoints. Phase 3 (8 weeks): ML-based topic extraction replacing VADER for nuanced sentiment themes (menu quality, service speed, value), sentiment trend alerting via webhook events, automated GDPR reviewer name anonymization pipeline for records approaching 90-day threshold.
target_output.development_guidelines: Platform adapters must implement the ReviewAdapter abstract base class with fetch(location_id, since) -> list[RawReview] interface — no platform-specific logic leaks into the ingestion orchestrator. All platform adapter tests use VCR cassette recordings to avoid live API calls in CI. VADER SentimentIntensityAnalyzer instantiated once at module level (not per request) to avoid repeated VADER lexicon loading. MongoDB queries use Motor async driver exclusively — no PyMongo sync calls in async FastAPI context. API key middleware must not log the raw key at any log level. OpenAPI spec generated from FastAPI app and committed to repository — spec changes require PR approval from API consumers.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Platform APIs change schema or revoke access without notice. Mitigation: adapter pattern isolates impact; schema validation with Pydantic models raises alerts on unexpected field changes rather than silently dropping data. Risk: Yelp 5 QPS rate limit creates ingestion bottleneck for large location counts. Mitigation: Redis token bucket distributes requests; for >200 Yelp locations, consider spreading across multiple Cloud Scheduler triggers. Tradeoff: VADER sentiment over a fine-tuned restaurant review model — simpler to deploy and maintain for MVP but lower accuracy on nuanced food/service language; acceptable until Phase 3. Tradeoff: MongoDB over PostgreSQL — document flexibility for varying platform schemas outweighs relational benefits for this read-heavy API use case.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: MongoDB 7 Atlas selected over Firestore for richer aggregation pipeline support needed for reputation score computation. VADER selected over transformers-based sentiment for MVP to avoid GPU hosting costs. Cursor pagination implemented via MongoDB _id-based cursor (ObjectId lexicographic order) rather than offset pagination for consistent results during concurrent inserts. Platform adapter tests use VCR cassettes rather than live API calls to avoid rate limit consumption in CI. GCP Cloud Run selected over GKE for zero-to-scale convenience at expected traffic volumes.

metadata.notes: Review aggregation pipeline with 4-platform adapter pattern, VADER sentiment, and authenticated REST API for restaurant reputation management.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for restaurant group DevOps engineers to manage deployment configuration of a centralized recipe and menu management platform across hundreds of franchise locations — bulk environment variable updates, rolling config deployments with health-check validation, and rollback on failure
project_goal.rationale: Replaces ad-hoc SSH-based config pushes that cause inconsistent states across location clusters

target_users.value: Restaurant group DevOps engineers and platform SREs managing configuration across 200-500 franchise location clusters
target_users.rationale: Primary operators of the platform identified in engineering discovery

project_class.rationale: Command-line interface consumed by engineers in terminal; no browser UI justified

capabilities.rationale: CLI with PostgreSQL state storage and ECS Fargate API integration; no frontend needed

complexity_level.rationale: Rolling deployment orchestration with concurrency control and health-check validation

risk_level.rationale: Misconfigured rollout could disrupt ordering operations at active restaurant locations

data_sensitivity.rationale: Config values include API keys and connection strings for internal franchise platform services

external_exposure.rationale: CLI runs on engineer workstations connected to internal corporate network or VPN; no public endpoint

access_model.value: Custom JWT authentication against internal IAM; engineer identities bound to IAM groups controlling which location clusters they can deploy to
access_model.rationale: Cluster-scoped access control prevents engineers from deploying to clusters outside their authorization boundary

feature_scope.value: Bulk environment variable updates, rolling config deployments with configurable concurrency limits, health-check validation sweep post-deployment, automatic rollback on health-check failure, deployment history and audit log
feature_scope.rationale: Complete feature set for replacing manual SSH-based workflows

mvp_scope.value: deploy command with rolling batch execution, health-check validation, rollback on failure; rollout-status command showing live deployment progress; audit log of deployments to PostgreSQL
mvp_scope.rationale: Core commands that replace the most error-prone manual processes

security_baseline.value: JWT authentication for all CLI commands, config values encrypted at rest in PostgreSQL, TLS for all API calls to location health endpoints, no plaintext secrets in CLI flags or output
security_baseline.rationale: Internal tool security baseline for a medium-risk platform

frontend_stack.value: None — CLI tool, no browser UI
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI architecture; FrontendAgent not applicable

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Cobra CLI framework
backend_stack.rationale: Go produces self-contained binaries; Cobra is the standard CLI framework for Go; fast execution for rolling deployment loops

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on AWS RDS for deployment state, config versions, audit log, and health-check results
data_platform.rationale: Durable relational storage for deployment audit trails and config version history

hosting_target.value: Self-contained Go binary distributed via internal Artifactory; runs on engineer workstations and CI runners connecting to internal AWS ECS Fargate platform APIs
hosting_target.rationale: Binary distribution without Docker runtime requirement on engineer workstations

privacy_retention_policy.value: Deployment audit logs retained for 2 years per SOC 2 Type II audit evidence requirements; config version snapshots retained for 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOC 2 evidence retention and operational rollback window requirements

future_scope.value: GitOps integration triggering deploys from merged PRs, Slack notifications on rollout completion and failure, per-location deployment canary mode
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP automation improvements

constraints.value: Must handle 500 locations without exceeding 30-minute total rollout window; binary must run without Docker on macOS and Linux engineer workstations; all config values masked in CLI output
constraints.rationale: Operational constraints from franchise scale and security policy

observability_baseline.value: Deployment progress printed to stdout with structured logging; all deployment events persisted to audit log table in PostgreSQL; AWS CloudWatch for CLI execution logs when run in CI
observability_baseline.rationale: Audit trail and progress visibility for operations teams

execution_preference.value: Go modules for dependency management; GitHub Actions for CLI binary build and distribution to Artifactory
execution_preference.rationale: Standard Go toolchain

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II for the centralized recipe management platform; internal change management controls requiring audit log for all configuration changes
compliance_context.rationale: Platform is SOC 2 certified; CLI config changes are in scope for change management controls

project.goal: CLI for rolling config deployments across 500 franchise location clusters with health-check validation
project.target_users: Restaurant group DevOps engineers

backend.http_client: net/http with TLS for health-check calls

security.auth: Custom JWT validated against internal IAM
security.secret_storage: AWS Secrets Manager for DATABASE_URL and IAM signing key
security.config_encryption: AES-256-GCM for config values stored in PostgreSQL

devops.environments: development (local), staging (test cluster), production (live location clusters)

reasoner_reviews.product: The rolling deployment with automatic rollback is the core value proposition — engineers must be able to run a single command and trust that failed health checks will halt the rollout and revert affected locations. The rollout-status command needs live progress output (progress bar or per-batch status) to give confidence during a 500-location deployment.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go is the correct choice — self-contained binary, no runtime dependency, fast goroutine-based concurrency for parallel health checks across batches. Key design decision: rolling batch size should be configurable (default 10 locations per batch) with a concurrency limit for health-check requests. PostgreSQL is correct for the audit log; config values need AES-256-GCM encryption before storage since the database may be accessed by ops teams.
reasoner_reviews.security: JWT tokens for CLI auth must have short expiry (1 hour) and be refreshed automatically. Config values in CLI flags must never appear in PostgreSQL audit log rows — log the config key names only, not values. The CLI output must mask all config values with asterisks. Health-check endpoint authentication uses a separate internal service token, not engineer credentials.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 30-minute rollout for 500 locations with 10-location batches = 50 batches. If each batch takes ~30 seconds (health check timeout 20s + overhead), total is ~25 minutes — feasible. Concurrency within a batch (parallel health checks) keeps per-batch time under 30 seconds.

backend.service_design: Cobra root command with subcommands: deploy, rollout-status, rollback, config-set, health-check, history. Each subcommand is a separate Go file in cmd/. Shared services: AuthService (JWT), DeploymentOrchestrator, HealthCheckClient, ConfigStore.
backend.api_patterns: CLI flags parsed by Cobra pflag. Structured output via zerolog to stdout. Deployment progress via a live-updating progress bar (github.com/schollz/progressbar).
backend.business_modules: DeploymentOrchestrator: processes location_clusters in configurable batch sizes, dispatches config to each cluster's ECS update API, waits for health-check confirmation, rolls back on failure. ConfigStore: AES-256-GCM encrypt/decrypt for config values before PostgreSQL storage.
backend.failure_handling: Health check failure triggers immediate rollback of the current batch; deployment marked failed in audit log; subsequent batches halted. Partial rollouts (some batches succeeded, some failed) leave a partial rollout state requiring explicit resolution command.

security.auth_design: JWT tokens issued by internal IAM service, verified by CLI against IAM public key. Token expiry 1 hour; CLI automatically prompts re-auth on expiry.
security.authorization_model: IAM groups map engineer identities to allowed location cluster sets. DeploymentOrchestrator validates engineer's cluster authorization before processing each location.
security.secrets_management: DATABASE_URL and IAM_PUBLIC_KEY loaded from AWS Secrets Manager on CLI init. No secrets in environment variables or config files on disk. Config values stored in PostgreSQL encrypted with AES-256-GCM.
security.security_risks: Risk of config values appearing in shell history via --value flags. Mitigation: config-set subcommand reads sensitive values from stdin or a file reference, never from CLI flags directly.

data.entities: location_clusters (id, cluster_tag, region, health_endpoint_url, last_deploy_at), deployment_configs (id, config_key, value_encrypted BYTEA, iv BYTEA, cluster_id FK, version INTEGER, created_by_uid, created_at), config_versions (id, config_key, version, created_at, created_by_uid), rollout_batches (id, deployment_id FK, batch_number, locations_json, status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed/failed/rolled_back, started_at, completed_at), health_check_results (id, rollout_batch_id FK, location_id FK, endpoint_url, http_status, response_ms, checked_at), audit_log (id, engineer_uid, action ENUM deploy/rollback/config_set/health_check, cluster_scope, config_keys_affected TEXT[], outcome, details_json, executed_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on AWS RDS Multi-AZ for high availability. AES-256-GCM encryption for config values before insert. 2-year audit log retention; 90-day config version snapshot retention via pg_partman time-based partitioning on audit_log.
data.data_risks: Database connection from engineer workstations to AWS RDS requires VPN. Connectivity failure during a rollout must be handled gracefully — DeploymentOrchestrator checkpoints batch state to DB before proceeding to next batch.

devops.deployment_topology: Go binary compiled for linux/amd64 and darwin/arm64 via GitHub Actions goreleaser. Binaries published to internal Artifactory repository. Engineers install via brew or direct download from Artifactory.
devops.environments: Development: local binary against a staging PostgreSQL instance. Production: binary run by engineers against production RDS and ECS Fargate platform APIs.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test ./... → golangci-lint → goreleaser build for linux/amd64 + darwin/arm64 → publish to Artifactory on tagged release.
devops.devops_risks: Binary distribution via Artifactory requires engineers to update manually unless auto-update check is implemented. Plan: CLI checks Artifactory for newer version on startup and warns if behind.

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass plan for a Go CLI tool managing rolling config deployments across 500 franchise restaurant locations. Key design decisions: Cobra for structured subcommands, goroutine-based concurrency for parallel health checks within deployment batches, AES-256-GCM encryption for config values at rest in PostgreSQL, and SOC 2-compliant audit logging with key names (never values) recorded. The 30-minute rollout constraint is achievable with 50 batches of 10 locations each at approximately 25 seconds per batch.
target_output.title: Franchise Location Config Deployment CLI Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A self-contained Go 1.22 CLI tool built with Cobra, enabling restaurant group DevOps engineers to execute rolling configuration deployments across up to 500 franchise location clusters. The tool orchestrates batched config pushes to ECS Fargate location APIs, performs parallel health-check validation after each batch, and automatically rolls back affected locations on failure. All deployment actions are recorded in a PostgreSQL audit log meeting SOC 2 Type II change management requirements. Binaries are distributed as platform-native executables via internal Artifactory.
target_output.architecture_overview: Self-contained Go binary with Cobra subcommand structure. The DeploymentOrchestrator service processes location_clusters in configurable batches (default 10), pushing config updates to each location's ECS Fargate management API, then dispatching parallel goroutines to validate health-check endpoints with a 20-second timeout. On health-check failure, the orchestrator triggers rollback for the current batch and halts subsequent batches. All state — batch progress, health-check results, config versions — is checkpointed to PostgreSQL 16 on AWS RDS Multi-AZ via pgx/v5. Config values are AES-256-GCM encrypted before database storage.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Go 1.22. CLI framework: Cobra with pflag. Database client: pgx/v5. DB migrations: golang-migrate. Logging: zerolog (structured JSON). Progress display: schollz/progressbar. Encryption: Go crypto/aes + crypto/cipher (AES-256-GCM). Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager SDK v2. Distribution: goreleaser for linux/amd64 + darwin/arm64 binaries, internal Artifactory repository. CI: GitHub Actions. Monitoring: AWS CloudWatch Logs (CI runs), PostgreSQL audit log.
target_output.functional_feature_map: deploy command: accepts --cluster-tag or --all flag, --batch-size (default 10), --concurrency (default 5 parallel health checks per batch), --dry-run flag; executes rolling deployment with live progress bar. rollout-status command: queries active deployment progress from PostgreSQL, displays per-batch completion status. rollback command: manually triggers rollback of a specific deployment ID for partial rollout recovery. config-set command: reads config key-value pairs from stdin or --file flag (never CLI flags), encrypts values, stores in PostgreSQL with version increment. health-check command: validates one or more location cluster health endpoints on demand. history command: displays last N deployments for a cluster tag with outcome and engineer identity.
target_output.system_components: Cobra CLI binary (deploy, rollout-status, rollback, config-set, health-check, history subcommands). DeploymentOrchestrator (batch processing, goroutine pool for concurrent health checks, rollback trigger). HealthCheckClient (HTTP client with TLS, 20s timeout, service token auth to location endpoints). ConfigStore (AES-256-GCM encrypt/decrypt, pgx write/read). AuthService (JWT validation against IAM public key, token refresh prompt). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ (deployment state, audit log, config versions). AWS Secrets Manager (DATABASE_URL, IAM_PUBLIC_KEY).
target_output.workflows: Rolling Config Deploy: Engineer runs 'menuctl deploy --cluster-tag us-west --batch-size 10' → AuthService validates JWT → DeploymentOrchestrator fetches authorized location clusters → creates deployment record in PostgreSQL → processes batch 1 (10 locations): calls each location's ECS config update API concurrently (up to 5 goroutines) → waits for health-check confirmations → marks batch completed in PostgreSQL → prints progress bar update → proceeds to batch 2 through 50 → deployment marked completed → engineer sees pass/fail summary. Automatic Rollback: Health check returns non-200 or times out (>20s) for any location in batch N → orchestrator records health_check_result with failure detail → triggers rollback API call to affected locations → marks rollout_batch status='rolled_back' → halts subsequent batches → exits with non-zero code → CI pipeline fails.
target_output.data_model: location_clusters (id UUID PK, cluster_tag VARCHAR, region VARCHAR, health_endpoint_url VARCHAR, service_token_secret_id VARCHAR, last_deploy_at TIMESTAMPTZ). deployment_configs (id UUID PK, config_key VARCHAR, value_encrypted BYTEA, iv BYTEA, cluster_id UUID FK, version INTEGER, created_by_uid VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). rollout_batches (id UUID PK, deployment_id UUID FK, batch_number INTEGER, location_ids UUID[], status ENUM pending/in_progress/completed/failed/rolled_back, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). health_check_results (id UUID PK, rollout_batch_id UUID FK, location_id UUID FK, http_status INTEGER, response_ms INTEGER, error_detail VARCHAR, checked_at TIMESTAMPTZ). audit_log (id UUID PK, engineer_uid VARCHAR, action ENUM deploy/rollback/config_set/health_check, cluster_scope VARCHAR, config_keys_affected TEXT[], outcome ENUM success/failure, details_jsonb JSONB, executed_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: CLI subcommand interface (no HTTP server). External API calls made by the CLI: PATCH /api/v1/locations/{cluster_id}/config (ECS Fargate location management API — updates config env vars for the location's task definition); GET /api/v1/locations/{cluster_id}/health (location health endpoint, 20s timeout, service token Bearer auth); GET /api/v1/iam/public-key (internal IAM service, fetched on init for JWT verification). All outbound calls over TLS 1.3.
target_output.security_and_compliance: JWT authentication: JWT tokens issued by internal IAM, verified against IAM public key loaded from AWS Secrets Manager on CLI init. Tokens expire in 1 hour; CLI prompts re-auth on expiry. Cluster authorization: DeploymentOrchestrator checks engineer's IAM group membership against allowed cluster_tags before processing each batch — unauthorized clusters skipped with warning. Config value security: config-set reads values from stdin or file, never CLI flag arguments (prevents shell history exposure). All config values AES-256-GCM encrypted in PostgreSQL; only key names recorded in audit_log, never values. CLI output masks any string matching known config key patterns with asterisks. SOC 2 audit log: every deploy, rollback, and config-set operation records engineer_uid, action, affected config keys, outcome, and timestamp in audit_log with 2-year retention.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: goreleaser config produces binaries: linux/amd64, darwin/arm64. GitHub Actions release workflow: go test ./... → golangci-lint → goreleaser release → publish to Artifactory on git tag push. Engineers install via brew tap pointing to Artifactory or direct binary download. CLI checks Artifactory API for newer version on startup (non-blocking, warns only). Database migrations run via golang-migrate before first use of any new binary version (CLI checks migration state on init). AWS RDS Multi-AZ for high availability — CLI handles connection retry with exponential backoff on RDS failover.
target_output.observability: zerolog structured JSON output to stdout for all CLI operations — log level configurable via MENUCTL_LOG_LEVEL env var (default info). Deployment progress: live ASCII progress bar via schollz/progressbar showing 'Batch 3/50 | 28/500 locations | 2 failed'. Audit trail: all deployment actions written to PostgreSQL audit_log with full context. AWS CloudWatch Logs integration when CLI runs in GitHub Actions CI — CloudWatch agent streams stdout to a dedicated log group for centralized review. health-check command outputs per-location HTTP status, response time, and pass/fail indicator to stdout in tabular format.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero hosting cost for the CLI binary itself — distributed as a static binary. PostgreSQL RDS db.t4g.medium Multi-AZ ~$90/month for audit log and state storage. AWS Secrets Manager $0.40/month for 2 secrets. goreleaser and Artifactory storage are within existing engineering toolchain budget. Scaling: 500 locations with batch-size 10 fits in 30-minute window; increasing batch-size to 25 reduces window to ~12 minutes if needed.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): deploy command with rolling batches and automatic rollback, rollout-status command, health-check command, PostgreSQL audit log, AES-256-GCM config encryption, JWT auth, goreleaser binary distribution to Artifactory. Phase 2 (4 weeks): config-set command with stdin/file input and version history, history command for deployment audit review, Artifactory version check on startup. Phase 3 (post-MVP): GitOps trigger (GitHub Actions workflow calling menuctl deploy on PR merge), Slack webhook notifications on rollout completion and failure, per-cluster canary deployment mode.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Cobra commands in cmd/ package, business logic in internal/ packages (orchestrator, healthcheck, configstore, auth). No business logic in cmd files — commands are thin wrappers. All PostgreSQL queries via pgx/v5 parameterized queries only. Config values never logged, never printed to stdout without masking. Unit tests for DeploymentOrchestrator batch logic and rollback trigger. Integration tests against a pg testcontainer for ConfigStore and audit log persistence. golangci-lint enforced in CI with errcheck, gosec, and staticcheck enabled.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Partial rollout state (some batches completed, some failed) leaves locations in inconsistent config versions. Mitigation: rollback command allows manual resolution; audit log shows exact batch and location state for recovery. Risk: PostgreSQL connection failure mid-rollout corrupts batch checkpoint state. Mitigation: DeploymentOrchestrator writes batch state before and after each batch with idempotency keys enabling safe retry. Tradeoff: AES-256-GCM encryption in application layer adds key management complexity versus database-level encryption (TDE). Accepted because application-level encryption protects against database dump exposure independent of RDS encryption state.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Go over Python for self-contained binary distribution without runtime dependencies on engineer workstations. Confirmed Cobra over urfave/cli for richer subcommand structure and built-in flag documentation. Chose AES-256-GCM application-level encryption over PostgreSQL pgcrypto for portability across RDS upgrades. Rolling batch default of 10 locations balances deployment speed with blast radius containment.

metadata.notes: Go CLI for rolling config deployment across 500 franchise restaurant locations with SOC 2 audit logging and AES-256-GCM config encryption.

project_goal.value: TypeScript/JavaScript SDK providing a unified client interface for integrating with DoorDash, Uber Eats, Grubhub, and Postmates APIs — normalizing menu management, order ingestion, and webhook-based status updates into a single consistent abstraction for restaurant tech middleware developers
project_goal.rationale: Restaurant tech developers waste significant time implementing platform-specific API quirks; unified SDK reduces integration time from weeks to hours

target_users.value: Restaurant tech developers and middleware engineers building POS integration layers, ghost kitchen platforms, and direct delivery integrations; typically TypeScript-proficient engineers at companies with 5-100 restaurant locations
target_users.rationale: Primary SDK consumers building on top of delivery platform APIs

project_class.rationale: Distributed as an npm package; no hosted server; consumers run it in their own Node.js or edge runtime environments

capabilities.rationale: SDK provides API client abstractions, data normalization, and platform integrations; no frontend, no hosted auth service

complexity_level.rationale: Four distinct platform APIs with divergent authentication models, schema formats, webhook signing mechanisms, and rate limit regimes

risk_level.rationale: SDK handles platform API credentials and customer order data in transit; no data persistence but credential exposure risk is material

data_sensitivity.rationale: SDK processes order data transiently in consumer process memory; no SDK-owned persistence; data classification is consumer's responsibility

external_exposure.rationale: SDK consumers authenticate with per-platform OAuth 2.0 credentials; SDK itself is public npm package but requires valid platform credentials to function

access_model.value: Per-platform OAuth 2.0 client credentials (DoorDash, Uber Eats) and API key (Grubhub, Postmates); credentials passed at SDK initialization, never stored by SDK; token refresh handled transparently per platform
access_model.rationale: Each delivery platform has its own auth model; SDK abstracts credential management without storing secrets

feature_scope.value: Unified menu.push() for cross-platform menu sync, order webhook normalization to canonical OrderEvent, order status update dispatch, item availability toggling, platform credential management with auto-refresh, webhook signature verification per platform, TypeScript types for all canonical schemas
feature_scope.rationale: Core unified API surface covering the 80% use case for delivery platform integration

mvp_scope.value: DoorDash and Uber Eats integrations with menu.push(), order webhook normalization, and item availability toggle; full TypeScript typings; comprehensive test suite
mvp_scope.rationale: DoorDash and Uber Eats represent 70% of third-party delivery order volume

security_baseline.value: No SDK-side credential persistence; credentials held in consumer-provided memory; webhook HMAC signature verification enforced before dispatching OrderEvents; no SDK telemetry or external calls beyond platform APIs; exported types enforce readonly on canonical schemas to prevent mutation
security_baseline.rationale: SDK must not become a credential storage target; webhook verification prevents order injection attacks

frontend_stack.value: None — SDK library; no UI components
frontend_stack.rationale: library_sdk project class; no frontend

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 with TypeScript 5.4; tsup for bundling to ESM and CJS; Vitest for testing
backend_stack.rationale: TypeScript for type safety across all canonical schemas; tsup produces dual ESM/CJS package for broad consumer compatibility

data_platform.value: No database; SDK is stateless per-request; OAuth token cache held in consumer-provided memory store (Map or custom adapter)
data_platform.rationale: SDK must not require a database; stateless design enables serverless and edge runtime deployment

hosting_target.value: npm public registry under @restauranttech/delivery-sdk; GitHub Releases with CHANGELOG; versioned via semantic-release
hosting_target.rationale: Standard npm distribution for TypeScript SDK

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK does not retain any customer order data; transient in-memory processing only; GDPR compliance is consumer's responsibility; SDK docs include GDPR data flow guidance
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No SDK-side persistence means no retention obligation; consumer is data controller

future_scope.value: Postmates and Grubhub integrations; batch menu sync across all platforms; order analytics aggregation helper; React hook wrappers for dashboard consumers
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 after DoorDash and Uber Eats adoption proven

constraints.value: Zero runtime dependencies beyond Node.js built-ins and platform SDKs; must work in Node.js 18+ and Cloudflare Workers; bundle size <50 KB gzipped; 100% TypeScript with strict mode; semver versioning with no breaking changes in minor/patch
constraints.rationale: Downstream consumers have strict bundle size and runtime compatibility requirements

observability_baseline.value: Debug logging via consumer-injectable logger interface (no default logger output); SDK emits structured debug events consumers can subscribe to; no SDK-owned metrics or telemetry
observability_baseline.rationale: SDK should be transparent to consumer observability stack; no forced logging dependencies

execution_preference.value: GitHub Flow with semantic-release automated versioning; PR-based releases; conventional commits enforced
execution_preference.rationale: Automated versioning ensures semver discipline for SDK consumers

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 28 processor obligations: SDK documentation must include data flow diagrams showing transient order data handling; platform API ToS compliance: no caching of platform response data beyond request lifetime; rate limit adherence required per platform agreement
compliance_context.rationale: Consumer order data flows through SDK; GDPR and platform ToS compliance documentation required

project.goal: Unified TypeScript SDK for DoorDash, Uber Eats, Grubhub, Postmates delivery platform APIs
project.target_users: Restaurant tech middleware developers
project.timeline: npm v1.0 with DoorDash + Uber Eats, v2.0 adding Grubhub + Postmates

backend.api: Named exports with tree-shaking; no side-effect imports

security.auth: Per-platform OAuth 2.0 and API key; consumer-provided credential storage
security.webhooks: HMAC SHA-256 signature verification enforced per platform
security.compliance: GDPR transient data handling; platform ToS rate limits

data.storage: Stateless; optional consumer-provided token cache adapter
data.types: Canonical TypeScript interfaces: DeliveryPlatform, MenuDefinition, OrderEvent, StatusTransition, WebhookPayload

devops.environments: local dev, CI test matrix (Node 18/20/22, Cloudflare Workers)

constraints.dependencies: Zero runtime deps beyond Node built-ins

reasoner_reviews.product: Round 4 is converging well. The canonical OrderEvent schema has stabilized across rounds. The remaining open gap is Grubhub's non-standard webhook authentication — it uses a rotating HMAC secret fetched from their API rather than a static key, which requires the SDK to periodically refresh the Grubhub webhook secret and cache it in the consumer-provided token store alongside OAuth tokens.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The pluggable TokenCacheAdapter pattern introduced in round 3 is the right abstraction for both OAuth tokens and rotating Grubhub webhook secrets. Recommend solidifying the TokenCacheAdapter interface with set(key, value, ttl?) and get(key) methods, and providing a built-in InMemoryTokenCache implementation. The adapter pattern lets serverless consumers plug in Redis or DynamoDB caches without adding runtime dependencies to the SDK bundle.
reasoner_reviews.security: Webhook verification is correctly enforced before OrderEvent dispatch. Ensure the DoorDash signature verification uses the timing-safe comparison (crypto.timingSafeEqual) rather than string equality to prevent timing attacks. The Grubhub rotating secret fetch should have a maximum TTL of 24 hours with forced refresh on 401 from Grubhub's webhook verification endpoint.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The <50 KB gzipped bundle constraint is at risk if all four platform adapters are included in the main entry point. Recommend platform-specific subpath exports ('@restauranttech/delivery-sdk/doordash', '/ubereats') allowing tree-shaking in bundled consumer apps, with a convenience barrel export for consumers who use all platforms.

backend.service_design: Core SDK exports: DeliverySdkClient (orchestrator), PlatformRegistry (maps DeliveryPlatform enum to adapter instance), and individual platform adapters DoorDashAdapter, UberEatsAdapter, GrubhubAdapter, PostmatesAdapter. Each adapter implements PlatformAdapter interface: getAccessToken(), pushMenu(menu: MenuDefinition), toggleItemAvailability(itemId, available), verifyWebhook(payload, signature), normalizeOrderEvent(raw). TokenCacheAdapter interface: get(key), set(key, value, ttl?), delete(key) — consumer-provided; InMemoryTokenCache built-in default.
backend.api_patterns: Public API surface: new DeliverySdkClient({platforms: {doordash: {clientId, clientSecret}, ubereats: {clientId, clientSecret}}, tokenCache?: TokenCacheAdapter, logger?: Logger}). Methods: client.menu.push(menuDef: MenuDefinition, platforms?: DeliveryPlatform[]) => Promise<PushResult[]>. client.orders.handleWebhook(platform, rawBody: Buffer, signature: string) => Promise<OrderEvent>. client.items.setAvailability(itemId, available, platforms?) => Promise<void>. client.tokens.refresh(platform?) — manual token refresh. All methods return typed results; errors typed as DeliverySdkError with platform and code fields.
backend.failure_handling: PlatformError wraps platform HTTP errors with statusCode, platform, and retryable fields. Exponential backoff with jitter for rate-limited (429) and server error (5xx) responses: 3 retries, base delay 500ms, max delay 8s. Non-retryable errors (400, 401, 403) thrown immediately. Webhook signature mismatch throws WebhookVerificationError — consumers must not process the OrderEvent. Token refresh on 401 with single retry before propagating error.
backend.business_modules: MenuNormalizer: transforms consumer-provided MenuDefinition to platform-specific menu payload (DoorDash catalog format, Uber Eats menu API v3 format). OrderNormalizer: transforms platform-specific order webhook payloads to canonical OrderEvent. WebhookVerifier: platform-specific HMAC verification (DoorDash: X-DoorDash-Signature HMAC-SHA256 timingSafeEqual; Uber Eats: X-Uber-Signature; Grubhub: rotating secret fetch + HMAC). StatusMapper: normalizes platform-specific order status strings to canonical OrderStatus enum.

security.auth_design: OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow for DoorDash (JWT assertion) and Uber Eats (standard client_credentials grant). API key header injection for Grubhub (X-Api-Key) and Postmates (Bearer token). All credential material passed at SDK initialization; stored in consumer-provided tokenCache keyed as 'token:{platform}'; never logged or included in error messages.
security.secrets_management: SDK never persists credentials to disk or SDK-owned storage. Token cache is consumer-provided or defaults to InMemoryTokenCache (process memory). Grubhub rotating webhook secret cached under key 'webhook_secret:grubhub' with 24-hour TTL and forced refresh on HMAC mismatch. Error messages sanitize credential values — DeliverySdkError strips Authorization headers from included request context.
security.abuse_prevention: Webhook signature verification mandatory — verifyWebhook() throws if signature invalid, OrderEvent not returned. Rate limit headers parsed per platform and exposed as PlatformRateLimitInfo on PlatformError for consumer-side throttling decisions. SDK enforces platform-specific rate limits via token bucket per platform instance (configurable burst/refill).

data.entities: DeliveryPlatform enum ('doordash','uber_eats','grubhub','postmates'). MenuDefinition {storeId: string, items: MenuItem[], categories: MenuCategory[], modifierGroups: ModifierGroup[]}. MenuItem {externalId: string, name: string, description?: string, priceCents: number, imageUrl?: string, available: boolean, calories?: number}. OrderEvent {orderId: string, platform: DeliveryPlatform, receivedAt: Date, customer: {name: string, deliveryAddress: Address}, items: OrderLineItem[], subtotalCents: number, status: OrderStatus, platformOrderId: string}. StatusTransition {orderId: string, platform: DeliveryPlatform, from: OrderStatus, to: OrderStatus, timestamp: Date}. WebhookPayload {platform: DeliveryPlatform, rawBody: Buffer, signature: string, receivedAt: Date}. PlatformCredentials {doordash?: {clientId: string, clientSecret: string}, ubereats?: {clientId: string, clientSecret: string}, grubhub?: {apiKey: string}, postmates?: {apiKey: string}}.
data.schema_notes: All canonical types use readonly modifiers on exported interfaces. OrderStatus enum: 'pending'|'confirmed'|'preparing'|'ready_for_pickup'|'picked_up'|'delivered'|'cancelled'. SDK exports Zod schemas for runtime validation of incoming webhook payloads alongside TypeScript types. Platform-specific raw types are internal; only canonical types in public exports.
data.retention_and_deletion: SDK holds no persistent state. In-memory token cache cleared on GC or explicit client.destroy() call. Consumer responsible for clearing any cached SDK instances containing credentials on user logout or context teardown.

devops.deployment_topology: npm package @restauranttech/delivery-sdk. Package exports: main entry (barrel), platform subpath exports /doordash, /ubereats, /grubhub, /postmates for tree-shaking. TypeScript declarations via .d.ts generated by tsup.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pnpm install, tsc --noEmit typecheck, Vitest test suite on Node 18/20/22 matrix + Cloudflare Workers wrangler test environment, bundle size check (bundlesize <50KB gzip), semantic-release on main push for automated npm publish. Conventional commits enforced via commitlint + husky.
devops.devops_risks: Risk: bundle size creep as platform adapters grow. Mitigation: bundlesize check in CI fails PR if >50KB gzip. Risk: platform API breaking changes. Mitigation: platform adapter integration tests against platform sandbox environments run weekly on cron job; Datadog synthetic monitors on DoorDash and Uber Eats sandbox order flow.

ISS-001.title: Grubhub rotating webhook secret not handled in TokenCacheAdapter
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1-3 did not address Grubhub's non-static HMAC secret requiring periodic API refresh. WebhookVerifier would use stale or null secret causing all Grubhub webhooks to fail verification.

history.note: Identified: Grubhub webhook secret is rotating, not static
TokenCacheAdapter added but webhook secret caching not wired
Still not connected in WebhookVerifier implementation spec

ISS-002.title: DoorDash signature verification uses string equality not timingSafeEqual
ISS-002.detail: Round 2 plan used string === for HMAC comparison in DoorDashAdapter.verifyWebhook, creating a timing side-channel vulnerability. Fixed in round 3 but not confirmed in all platform adapters.

history.note: Security review flagged string equality for HMAC comparison
DoorDash fixed; need to confirm all adapters

ISS-003.title: Bundle size at risk without subpath exports for platform adapters
ISS-003.detail: All four platform adapters exported from single barrel entry causes 85KB gzipped bundle, exceeding <50KB constraint when all adapters bundled. Subpath exports needed.

history.note: Bundle size check CI failure at 85KB

focus_issues.title: Bundle size exceeds 50KB constraint without subpath exports
focus_issues.detail: Barrel export of all four adapters causes 85KB gzip bundle, failing CI size check.

latest_recommendations: Implement package.json subpath exports mapping '/doordash', '/ubereats', '/grubhub', '/postmates' to individual adapter entry points built by tsup as separate output chunks
Provide a convenience barrel '@restauranttech/delivery-sdk' that re-exports all for consumers who use all platforms and do not bundle

convergence_state.reason: One medium issue (ISS-003) unresolved: bundle size CI failure

previous_audits.summary: Strong SDK architecture with well-typed canonical schemas and correct webhook security model. Grubhub rotating secret (ISS-001) and timing-safe comparison (ISS-002) resolved. Bundle size remains over constraint.
strengths: TokenCacheAdapter pattern cleanly abstracts both OAuth tokens and webhook secrets
Webhook verification is mandatory and correctly typed as throwing on failure
Canonical OrderEvent schema covers all four platforms without platform leakage
concerns: Subpath exports not yet defined; barrel-only entry causes CI bundle size failure at 85KB
blocking_issues: Bundle size 85KB gzip exceeds 50KB constraint; CI check failing
recommendations: Add package.json exports map with subpath entries per platform; configure tsup to build separate entry points

issue_updates.title: Bundle size constraint violation
issue_updates.detail: Single barrel entry builds all adapters together; no tree-shaking possible without subpath exports

previous_plan.title: Unified Delivery Platform SDK v3
previous_plan.executive_summary: TypeScript SDK unifying DoorDash, Uber Eats, Grubhub, Postmates APIs with canonical menu, order, and webhook abstractions. TokenCacheAdapter pattern for OAuth and rotating secrets. Barrel-only exports pending subpath export refactor.
previous_plan.technology_stack: TypeScript 5.4, tsup single entry point, Vitest on Node 18/20/22 matrix, semantic-release on npm.
previous_plan.api_design: DeliverySdkClient with menu.push(), orders.handleWebhook(), items.setAvailability(). Platform adapters exposed through client only. Token refresh transparent.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: HMAC timingSafeEqual on all adapters. Grubhub rotating webhook secret cached in TokenCacheAdapter with 24-hour TTL. Credentials sanitized from error messages.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 addresses the final unresolved issue: bundle size exceeding 50KB due to barrel-only exports. The fix requires adding package.json subpath exports mapping each platform to its own tsup output chunk, enabling tree-shaking in consumer bundlers. The barrel export is retained for consumers importing all platforms. This requires updating technology_stack (tsup config), api_design (exports documentation), and deployment_and_operations (package.json exports map). All previously resolved issues (Grubhub rotating secret, timingSafeEqual) are maintained in the plan.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined package.json exports map with subpath entries: '.' (barrel), './doordash', './ubereats', './grubhub', './postmates'. Configured tsup to build 5 entry points producing separate ESM+CJS chunks per platform. Barrel entry imports lazily to maintain tree-shaking. CI bundlesize check updated to measure per-platform subpath size (<15KB each). Updated technology_stack, api_design, and deployment_and_operations sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Per-platform subpath bundle size <15KB gzip; consumers using single platform do not include other adapter code; CI bundlesize check passes

target_output.title: Unified Delivery Platform SDK v4
target_output.executive_summary: A TypeScript/JavaScript SDK on npm providing a unified integration surface for DoorDash, Uber Eats, Grubhub, and Postmates delivery platform APIs. Restaurant tech developers import a single SDK to push menus across platforms, normalize incoming order webhooks to a canonical OrderEvent schema, and toggle item availability — replacing four separate integration implementations. The SDK uses a pluggable TokenCacheAdapter pattern for OAuth tokens and rotating webhook secrets, enforces webhook HMAC verification with timing-safe comparison before dispatching events, and distributes as platform-specific subpath exports enabling tree-shaking to <15KB per platform in consumer bundles.
target_output.architecture_overview: Stateless TypeScript library structured as a core orchestrator (DeliverySdkClient) delegating to platform adapter instances registered in a PlatformRegistry. Each PlatformAdapter implements a common interface: getAccessToken(), pushMenu(), toggleItemAvailability(), verifyWebhook(), and normalizeOrderEvent(). Platform adapters are isolated modules with no cross-dependencies, enabling subpath export tree-shaking. A shared layer provides TokenCacheAdapter abstraction (InMemoryTokenCache default), canonical type definitions, and utility functions (retryWithBackoff, timingSafeHmacVerify). The SDK is fully stateless per-request except for the token cache, which is consumer-managed. No HTTP server, no database, no file system access.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: TypeScript 5.4 strict mode. Bundler: tsup 8.x configured with 5 entry points — src/index.ts (barrel), src/platforms/doordash/index.ts, src/platforms/ubereats/index.ts, src/platforms/grubhub/index.ts, src/platforms/postmates/index.ts — producing ESM and CJS output per entry. Runtime validation: Zod 3.x for webhook payload schemas (bundled per-platform, not shared across chunks). Testing: Vitest with MSW for platform API mocking. Versioning: semantic-release with conventional commits. package.json exports map defines subpath entries for each platform. Target: Node.js 18+ and Cloudflare Workers (Web Crypto API for HMAC).
target_output.functional_feature_map: menu.push(menuDef, platforms?): Accepts MenuDefinition with items, categories, and modifierGroups; transforms to platform-specific catalog formats; dispatches to all configured platforms or specified subset; returns PushResult[] with per-platform success/error. orders.handleWebhook(platform, rawBody, signature): Enforces HMAC signature verification; normalizes platform payload to canonical OrderEvent; dispatches to consumer-registered handler. items.setAvailability(itemId, available, platforms?): Toggles item 86'd status across platforms via platform-specific availability APIs. tokens.refresh(platform?): Manual token refresh bypassing TTL. events.subscribe(handler): Consumer subscribes to SDK debug events (token_refresh, rate_limit_hit, webhook_verified).
target_output.system_components: DeliverySdkClient: entry point accepting PlatformCredentials, optional TokenCacheAdapter, optional Logger; initializes PlatformRegistry with configured adapters. PlatformRegistry: Map<DeliveryPlatform, PlatformAdapter> with getAdapter(platform) throwing if not configured. DoorDashAdapter: OAuth 2.0 JWT assertion token flow; DoorDash Drive API v2 menu push; HMAC-SHA256 webhook verification using Node.js crypto.timingSafeEqual. UberEatsAdapter: standard OAuth 2.0 client_credentials; Uber Eats Orders API v2; X-Uber-Signature HMAC-SHA256 timingSafeEqual. GrubhubAdapter: API key auth; Grubhub Restaurant API; rotating webhook secret fetch from GET /webhook/secret with 24-hour TTL cached in TokenCacheAdapter under 'webhook_secret:grubhub'; forced refresh on HMAC mismatch. PostmatesAdapter (phase 2): Bearer API key; Postmates Fleet API. InMemoryTokenCache: Map-backed default TokenCacheAdapter with TTL expiry. WebhookVerifier utility: platform-dispatched timingSafeHmacVerify calls. MenuNormalizer: platform-specific transformers for MenuDefinition to DoorDash catalog JSON and Uber Eats menu payload. OrderNormalizer: platform-specific transformers to canonical OrderEvent. RetryHandler: exponential backoff with jitter for 429/5xx responses.
target_output.workflows: Menu Synchronization Workflow: Consumer calls client.menu.push(menuDef, ['doordash', 'uber_eats']). SDK retrieves access tokens from TokenCacheAdapter (refreshing if expired via OAuth flow). MenuNormalizer transforms MenuDefinition to DoorDash catalog format (items as catalog_items array with price_amount in cents) and Uber Eats menu format (items with price_info.price). PlatformAdapters POST to DoorDash /v2/businesses/{businessId}/menus and Uber Eats /v2/eats/stores/{storeId}/menus. Responses mapped to PushResult[]; partial failures per platform surfaced as errors on individual results without throwing. Order Webhook Ingestion Workflow: Consumer's Express/Next.js/Hono webhook handler calls client.orders.handleWebhook('doordash', req.body, req.headers['x-doordash-signature']). GrubhubAdapter.verifyWebhook first retrieves webhook secret from TokenCacheAdapter; if absent, fetches from Grubhub API and caches. timingSafeHmacVerify computes expected HMAC and compares using crypto.timingSafeEqual — throws WebhookVerificationError on mismatch. OrderNormalizer.normalize(platform, rawPayload) transforms platform payload to canonical OrderEvent with orderId, platform, customer, items, subtotalCents, status. Consumer handler receives typed OrderEvent.
target_output.data_model: DeliveryPlatform enum ('doordash','uber_eats','grubhub','postmates'). MenuDefinition {readonly storeId: string, readonly items: readonly MenuItem[], readonly categories: readonly MenuCategory[], readonly modifierGroups: readonly ModifierGroup[]}. MenuItem {readonly externalId: string, readonly name: string, readonly description?: string, readonly priceCents: number, readonly available: boolean, readonly calories?: number}. OrderEvent {readonly orderId: string, readonly platform: DeliveryPlatform, readonly receivedAt: Date, readonly customer: {readonly name: string, readonly deliveryAddress: Address}, readonly items: readonly OrderLineItem[], readonly subtotalCents: number, readonly status: OrderStatus, readonly platformOrderId: string}. OrderStatus: 'pending'|'confirmed'|'preparing'|'ready_for_pickup'|'picked_up'|'delivered'|'cancelled'. StatusTransition {readonly orderId: string, readonly platform: DeliveryPlatform, readonly from: OrderStatus, readonly to: OrderStatus, readonly timestamp: Date}. PlatformCredentials {readonly doordash?: {clientId: string, clientSecret: string}, readonly ubereats?: {clientId: string, clientSecret: string}, readonly grubhub?: {apiKey: string}}. TokenCacheAdapter interface {get(key: string): Promise<string|null>, set(key: string, value: string, ttlSeconds?: number): Promise<void>, delete(key: string): Promise<void>}. All public interfaces use readonly modifiers.
target_output.api_design: Public entry via package.json exports: import {DeliverySdkClient} from '@restauranttech/delivery-sdk' (barrel, all platforms). import {DoorDashAdapter} from '@restauranttech/delivery-sdk/doordash' (tree-shaken). import {UberEatsAdapter} from '@restauranttech/delivery-sdk/ubereats'. import {GrubhubAdapter} from '@restauranttech/delivery-sdk/grubhub'. Constructor: new DeliverySdkClient({platforms: {doordash: {clientId, clientSecret}, ubereats: {clientId, clientSecret}}, tokenCache?: TokenCacheAdapter, logger?: SdkLogger, retryConfig?: {maxRetries: number, baseDelayMs: number}}). Methods: client.menu.push(menu: MenuDefinition, platforms?: DeliveryPlatform[]): Promise<PushResult[]>. client.orders.handleWebhook(platform: DeliveryPlatform, rawBody: Buffer, signature: string): Promise<OrderEvent>. client.items.setAvailability(itemId: string, available: boolean, platforms?: DeliveryPlatform[]): Promise<void>. client.tokens.refresh(platform?: DeliveryPlatform): Promise<void>. client.destroy(): void. Error types: DeliverySdkError (base), WebhookVerificationError, PlatformError {platform, statusCode, retryable}, RateLimitError {platform, retryAfterMs}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Webhook HMAC verification: all adapters use Node.js crypto.timingSafeEqual (or Web Crypto SubtleCrypto in Cloudflare Workers) for HMAC-SHA256 comparison — no string equality comparisons. WebhookVerificationError thrown before OrderEvent returned to consumer. Grubhub rotating webhook secret: fetched from GET /webhook/secret with API key auth, cached in TokenCacheAdapter as 'webhook_secret:grubhub' with 24-hour TTL, force-refreshed on any HMAC mismatch up to 1 time per 5 minutes to prevent DoS via forced refresh. Credentials: never logged; DeliverySdkError.sanitize() strips Authorization header values from included request context before error propagation. No SDK-side persistence: credentials exist only in consumer-passed object and in-memory TokenCacheAdapter. GDPR: SDK processes order data transiently per request; no retention; docs include data flow diagram. Platform ToS: rate limit headers parsed per platform, RateLimitError surfaced with retryAfterMs for consumer throttling. OAuth token refresh on 401 with single retry.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: npm publish via semantic-release on main merge. package.json exports: {'.':{import:'./dist/index.mjs',require:'./dist/index.cjs',types:'./dist/index.d.ts'},'./doordash':{import:'./dist/doordash/index.mjs',require:'./dist/doordash/index.cjs',types:'./dist/doordash/index.d.ts'},'./ubereats':{import:'./dist/ubereats/index.mjs',require:'./dist/ubereats/index.cjs',types:'./dist/ubereats/index.d.ts'},'./grubhub':{import:'./dist/grubhub/index.mjs',require:'./dist/grubhub/index.cjs',types:'./dist/grubhub/index.d.ts'}}. tsup config: 5 entry points, splitting:false per chunk (no shared chunk across subpath exports to preserve tree-shaking). GitHub Actions: tsc typecheck, Vitest matrix (Node 18/20/22, Cloudflare Workers via wrangler), bundlesize check (per-subpath <15KB, barrel <55KB), semantic-release on main. Weekly cron: integration tests against DoorDash and Uber Eats sandbox environments. Datadog synthetic monitor on sandbox order creation webhook round-trip.
target_output.observability: No SDK-owned telemetry or external calls. Consumer-injectable SdkLogger interface: {debug(msg, meta?): void, warn(msg, meta?): void, error(msg, meta?): void}. SDK calls logger.debug for: token_refresh (platform, ttl), webhook_verified (platform, orderId), rate_limit_hit (platform, retryAfterMs), retry_attempt (platform, attempt, delayMs). SDK calls logger.warn for: token_refresh_failed (platform, error), webhook_secret_rotated (platform). All log calls sanitize credential values. Consumer wires their Pino/Winston/Datadog logger to SdkLogger interface. EventEmitter-based client.events for programmatic subscription without logger dependency.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost — npm package. Development tooling: Datadog synthetic monitors for weekly sandbox tests ~$20/month. npm Pro plan for scoped package ~$7/month. SDK is stateless and horizontally scales with consumer's infrastructure. TokenCacheAdapter swap-out supports Redis or DynamoDB for multi-instance consumer deployments without code changes. Per-platform subpath exports ensure bundle cost is proportional to platforms used: single-platform consumer pays <15KB gzip vs 50KB for all four.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 v1.0 (shipped): DoorDash and Uber Eats adapters, menu.push(), orders.handleWebhook(), items.setAvailability(), InMemoryTokenCache, Vitest suite, semantic-release pipeline. Phase 2 v1.x (current): Grubhub adapter with rotating webhook secret, TokenCacheAdapter interface for pluggable cache (Redis adapter in separate '@restauranttech/delivery-sdk-redis-cache' package), subpath exports for bundle size constraint compliance. Phase 3 v2.0: Postmates adapter, batch menu sync across all platforms with diff detection, React hooks package '@restauranttech/delivery-sdk-react' with useOrderWebhook and useMenuSync hooks.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode with noUncheckedIndexedAccess. No barrel re-exports between platform adapter modules — each subpath entry must be independently buildable by tsup with no cross-adapter imports. Zod schemas maintained adjacent to platform adapter (src/platforms/doordash/schemas.ts) — not shared to preserve tree-shaking. All public interfaces use readonly. Conventional commits enforced (feat:, fix:, chore:). Vitest tests use MSW for platform API mocking — no live API calls in test suite. 100% type coverage on public exports enforced by tsd package in CI.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: DoorDash or Uber Eats API breaking changes without versioned SDK updates. Mitigation: platform adapter integration tests on weekly cron against sandbox; Datadog synthetic alerts; semantic versioning ensures consumers control update timing. Risk: Grubhub rotating webhook secret fetch adds latency to first webhook after cache miss. Mitigation: proactive background refresh 1 hour before TTL expiry via client-managed interval (optional, consumer opt-in). Tradeoff: Zod per-platform (not shared) increases per-platform bundle size ~5KB but maintains tree-shaking isolation — acceptable given constraint targets. Tradeoff: No shared chunk across subpath exports (tsup splitting:false) means duplicated utility code across chunks — minor size cost accepted for clean tree-shaking semantics.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Subpath exports with tsup 5-entry-point config resolves bundle size constraint. Grubhub rotating webhook secret handled via TokenCacheAdapter with forced refresh on mismatch. Cloudflare Workers compatibility via Web Crypto SubtleCrypto fallback in timingSafeHmacVerify. InMemoryTokenCache provided as default; Redis adapter as separate peer package to avoid adding redis dependency to SDK bundle.

metadata.notes: Round 4 SDK revision resolving bundle size constraint via subpath exports; previous issues around Grubhub rotating secret and timing-safe HMAC resolved in prior rounds.

profile.domain: Restaurant Supply Chain / Desktop Procurement Application

project_goal.value: Desktop application for restaurant supply chain coordinators to manage purchase orders and supplier relationships, with an API-first backend enabling the desktop client, forthcoming mobile app, and third-party integrations to share business logic — including real-time supplier price list polling and EDI order transmission
project_goal.rationale: API-first redesign decouples business logic from the desktop client for multi-client reuse

target_users.value: Restaurant supply chain coordinators at regional operations centers; supplier EDI system integrations as API consumers
target_users.rationale: Dual consumer audience: human coordinators via desktop and automated supplier systems via API

project_class.rationale: Primary interface is a Tauri desktop application; API backend is a supporting component

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, public_api
capabilities.rationale: Full stack with EDI integration, supplier API polling, and public REST API for future clients

complexity_level.rationale: Tauri + Vue 3 desktop, Rust/Axum API, EDI 850/855/856 integration, real-time price polling, and partner API exposure

risk_level.rationale: Financial procurement data with partner-facing API; no payment card data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Purchase orders, supplier contracts, and price agreements are financial data requiring internal controls

external_exposure.rationale: REST API exposed to approved supplier system integrations via OAuth 2.0 client credentials

access_model.value: Desktop app authenticates via Cognito OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow; supplier API consumers use OAuth 2.0 client credentials with per-supplier scopes
access_model.rationale: PKCE for desktop OAuth without client secret; client credentials for machine-to-machine supplier integrations

feature_scope.value: Purchase order creation and management, supplier profile management, real-time supplier price list polling, EDI 850 order transmission, EDI 855 acknowledgment and 856 ASN parsing, order line item validation against contracted prices, delivery receipt confirmation, partner API for supplier integrations
feature_scope.rationale: Complete supply chain coordinator workflow and supplier integration scope

mvp_scope.value: Purchase order CRUD, supplier profile management, manual price list import, EDI 850 transmission to pilot suppliers, basic Cognito auth
mvp_scope.rationale: Core coordinator workflow without real-time price polling or inbound EDI parsing at launch

security_baseline.value: Cognito OAuth 2.0 PKCE for desktop auth, mTLS for EDI transmission endpoints, per-supplier OAuth scopes limiting API access to their own order data, TLS 1.3, financial data encrypted at rest in PostgreSQL
security_baseline.rationale: Partner-facing API and financial data require layered authentication and encryption

frontend_stack.value: Tauri 2 with Vue 3 and TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Tauri for lightweight cross-platform desktop; Vue 3 for reactive data tables on large PO lists

backend_stack.value: Rust with Axum
backend_stack.rationale: Rust/Axum for high-performance API with type safety for EDI parsing and financial calculations

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on ECS Fargate RDS; Redis 7 for price list caching
data_platform.rationale: Relational store for PO financial data; Redis for low-latency price list reads during PO creation

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate for the Axum API; desktop app installed via platform-specific installer
hosting_target.rationale: Managed container hosting for API; no hosting needed for desktop executable

privacy_retention_policy.value: Purchase orders retained 7 years per financial record-keeping requirements; supplier price lists retained 3 years; EDI transaction logs retained 7 years for audit
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Financial record retention obligations for procurement data

future_scope.value: Mobile app for coordinator approvals on iOS, advanced analytics dashboard for spend-by-category, AI-assisted order quantity suggestions
future_scope.rationale: API-first architecture enables these without backend changes

constraints.value: EDI transmission must support X12 4010 format for legacy suppliers; price list poll interval configurable per supplier (1 hour to 24 hours); desktop app must work offline for PO drafting with sync on reconnect
constraints.rationale: Supplier EDI format compatibility and offline desktop usability requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on Axum API, EDI transmission success/failure rate tracking, price list poll health monitoring
observability_baseline.rationale: Partner-facing API and EDI integration require dedicated monitoring

execution_preference.value: Feature-based sprints with desktop and API developed in parallel; EDI integration delivered after core PO workflow
execution_preference.rationale: Parallel tracks enable UI and API to develop against shared OpenAPI spec

compliance_context.value: Internal financial controls for procurement audit trails; EDI trading partner agreement compliance (X12 4010 standard); SOC 2 Type II controls for SaaS API deployment
compliance_context.rationale: Financial data and partner-facing API trigger internal controls and SOC 2 scope

project.goal: Desktop PO management with API-first backend for multi-client and supplier EDI integration
project.target_users: Supply chain coordinators and supplier EDI systems

frontend.approach: Desktop kiosk-style app with offline PO drafting and sync-on-reconnect

security.auth: Cognito OAuth 2.0 PKCE (desktop), client credentials (supplier API)

devops.hosting: ECS Fargate for API; desktop installer via GitHub Releases

reasoner_reviews.product: The API-first design decision is the right strategic call — it enables the mobile app and supplier integrations without rearchitecting. The offline PO drafting requirement is critical for coordinators in distribution centers with intermittent connectivity; the sync-on-reconnect pattern must handle conflict resolution when a PO draft is modified both offline and online simultaneously. The EDI integration is correctly deferred to Q4 after core PO workflow validation.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Rust/Axum for the API backend is well-suited given the type-safety requirements for EDI X12 parsing and financial calculations. The Tauri + Vue 3 desktop frontend can use Tauri's native API for offline SQLite storage, syncing to the API backend on reconnect. Price list caching in Redis with configurable per-supplier TTL is the right pattern — avoid polling supplier APIs on every PO creation form load.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth 2.0 PKCE via Cognito for the desktop app is correct — Tauri can handle the OAuth redirect to the system browser and capture the callback via a custom URL scheme. Per-supplier OAuth scopes (e.g., scope: supplier:SUPP-001:orders) ensure supplier API consumers cannot access other suppliers' PO data. mTLS for EDI endpoints should use certificates issued by the supplier's CA, not self-signed.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: X12 4010 EDI parsing in Rust is feasible with the x12-parser crate or a custom implementation. The offline sync must use a vector clock or last-write-wins strategy for conflict resolution — document the chosen strategy in the ADR.

backend.service_design: Axum API with domain modules: purchase-orders (PO CRUD, line item validation, status state machine), suppliers (profile management, price list polling orchestration), edi (X12 850 generation, 855/856 parsing, transmission queue), auth (Cognito JWKS validation, supplier client credential exchange). Background Tokio tasks handle price list polling and EDI transmission retries.
backend.api_patterns: REST with OpenAPI 3.1 spec. Versioned at /api/v1. Core endpoints: POST /api/v1/purchase-orders, GET /api/v1/purchase-orders (paginated), PATCH /api/v1/purchase-orders/:id/submit, GET /api/v1/suppliers/:id/price-list, POST /api/v1/edi/transmit/:po_id, GET /api/v1/edi/acknowledgments. Partner API uses same base URL with supplier-scoped JWT.
backend.failure_handling: EDI transmission failures retry with exponential backoff (max 3 attempts, 30-minute max delay). Failed transmissions stored in edi_transmissions table with status failed and error_detail. Coordinator notified via WebSocket push event. Price list poll failures log warning and serve stale Redis cache rather than failing PO creation form.

frontend.app_structure: Tauri 2 desktop app with Vue 3 frontend. Offline-first: Tauri sidecar SQLite database stores PO drafts locally. Vue Router for page navigation: /purchase-orders (list), /purchase-orders/new (creation form), /suppliers (directory), /edi/status (transmission monitor). Pinia for reactive state management.
frontend.pages_and_flows: PO Creation Flow: coordinator selects supplier, line items auto-populated from cached price list (Redis-backed API), quantities entered, price validation against contracted rates shown inline, draft saved locally, submit triggers API call. EDI Monitor: real-time WebSocket feed of transmission status updates, acknowledgment receipts, and ASN arrivals per supplier. Supplier Directory: contact details, price list freshness indicator, EDI capability flags.
frontend.state_management: Pinia stores for purchase-orders (local draft cache), suppliers (price list cache), edi-status (WebSocket-fed transmission state). Tauri plugin:store for persistent offline draft storage in local SQLite.

security.auth_design: Tauri desktop: Cognito hosted UI OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow initiated in system browser; redirect URI uses custom scheme menuboard://auth callback captured by Tauri. JWT stored in Tauri secure storage (OS keychain). Supplier API: Cognito machine client with per-supplier client_id and scopes restricting access to that supplier's endpoints.
security.authorization_model: Coordinator roles: viewer (read-only PO list), coordinator (PO CRUD, no submit), approver (PO submit and supplier management). Supplier API scope: supplier:{supplier_id}:orders restricts GET /purchase-orders to POs where supplier_id matches.
security.secrets_management: API database credentials and Redis URL in AWS Secrets Manager, accessed by ECS task role. EDI mTLS private keys in AWS ACM Private CA, rotated annually. Desktop app stores JWT via OS keychain — never written to disk as plaintext.

data.entities: purchase_orders (id UUID PK, coordinator_id UUID FK, supplier_id UUID FK, status ENUM draft/submitted/acknowledged/shipped/received, po_number VARCHAR UNIQUE, delivery_date DATE, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ nullable). order_line_items (id UUID PK, po_id UUID FK, product_catalog_id UUID FK, quantity DECIMAL, unit_price_cents INT, contracted_price_cents INT, variance_pct FLOAT). supplier_profiles (id UUID PK, supplier_name VARCHAR, edi_capable BOOLEAN, edi_isa_id VARCHAR nullable, edi_gs_id VARCHAR nullable, api_client_id VARCHAR nullable). price_lists (id UUID PK, supplier_id UUID FK, product_catalog_id UUID FK, unit_price_cents INT, effective_date DATE, fetched_at TIMESTAMPTZ). edi_transmissions (id UUID PK, po_id UUID FK, transmission_type ENUM 850/855/856, status ENUM pending/sent/acknowledged/failed, sent_at TIMESTAMPTZ nullable, ack_received_at TIMESTAMPTZ nullable, error_detail TEXT nullable). delivery_receipts (id UUID PK, po_id UUID FK, received_at TIMESTAMPTZ, line_variances JSONB).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ (db.t3.medium). Redis 7 ElastiCache for price list cache (supplier_id:product_id keys, configurable TTL 1-24h). Desktop SQLite via Tauri plugin:store for offline PO draft cache.
