ALB: WAF v2 with AWSManagedRulesCommonRuleSet and AWSManagedRulesSQLiRuleSet. TLS 1.3 at ALB; Aurora TLS in transit enforced via RDS parameter group require_secure_transport=ON. DMS source endpoint: SSL mode verify-full, credentials from Secrets Manager. Aurora: KMS customer-managed key for encryption at rest. Cognito: existing user pool migrated, MFA state preserved. GDPR: DMS task log level ERROR only; CloudWatch DMS log group 7-day retention; no guest PII in DMS task logs. Note: PCI-DSS scope assessment for migrated database contents is not addressed in this plan — this is an intentional gap. Terraform state: S3 with versioning and SSE-S3 encryption, DynamoDB lock table, state access restricted to CI/CD IAM role via bucket policy.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform module structure: modules/vpc, modules/ecs, modules/alb, modules/rds, modules/dms, modules/cognito, modules/waf. Root module composes all child modules. S3 backend: bucket reservation-platform-tf-state, key env/production/terraform.tfstate, DynamoDB table terraform-state-lock. GitHub Actions CI: terraform fmt -check → terraform validate → tflint --module → terraform plan (output posted to PR as comment) → on merge to main: terraform apply for staging environment. Production apply: separate GitHub Actions workflow requiring manual approval from 2 SREs. ECR image build: Docker multi-stage build → pushed to ECR on merge → ECS service updated with new task definition revision. Rollback: previous ECS task definition revision reactivated via aws ecs update-service.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM: ddtrace-php installed in Laravel Docker image, traces all reservation API calls post-migration. Datadog synthetic check: polls GET /api/reservation/health every 30 seconds during cutover window, alerts on 2 consecutive failures. CloudWatch: DMS replication task metrics dashboard (CDCLatencySource, CDCLatencyTarget, FullLoadProgressPercent). CloudWatch alarm: CDCLatencySource > 60 seconds for 5 minutes → SNS alert to SRE on-call. ECS service metrics: CPUUtilization, MemoryUtilization per service in CloudWatch. VPC Flow Logs to CloudWatch Logs with 30-day retention. Terraform plan output to GitHub PR comment for audit trail of infrastructure changes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $280/month post-migration: Aurora MySQL 8 Serverless v2 (~$0.12/ACU-hour, estimated 2-4 ACU average = $175/month), ECS Fargate (2 tasks × 1vCPU/2GB = $60/month), ALB ($20/month), DMS instance (t3.medium, temporary during migration = $0 ongoing), NAT Gateway ($15/month), CloudWatch + other services ($10/month). DMS replication instance is a temporary cost during the migration window only. Post-migration scaling: Aurora auto-scales ACUs to handle weekend reservation peaks transparently.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — Terraform Infrastructure (4 weeks): Terraform modules for vpc, ecs, alb, rds, dms, cognito, waf. Docker containerization of Laravel app. Staging environment fully provisioned. DMS full-load task validated against staging MySQL source. Migration validator scripts validated. Phase 2 — Staging Validation (4 weeks): DMS CDC running against staging source for 2 weeks. Laravel integration test suite passing against Aurora MySQL 8 staging. Cutover runbook validated in staging dry-run. Performance benchmarking: Aurora vs bare-metal response times. Phase 3 — Production Cutover (1 week window): Route53 TTL reduction, production DMS full-load, 2-week CDC stabilization, scheduled cutover window execution, post-cutover Datadog monitoring. Phase 4 — Post-Migration Optimization (4 weeks): Aurora Serverless v2 ACU tuning, ECS auto-scaling policies, WAF rule refinement, DMS instance terminated.
target_output.development_guidelines: Terraform modules must be self-contained with no cross-module data sources except through root module outputs. All sensitive Terraform variables (DB credentials, Cognito client secrets) sourced from Secrets Manager via data sources, never from tfvars files. tflint enforced in CI with aws and terraform rulesets. Terraform state changes require PR with plan output review by 2 SREs. Python migration validator must achieve 100% row-count match and 100% checksum match for reservations and guest_profiles tables before cutover green-light. Docker images pinned to specific PHP 8.2.x patch versions in multi-stage build.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Aurora MySQL 8 strict mode incompatibilities with existing Laravel Eloquent queries causing runtime errors post-migration. Mitigation: 2-week staging validation with full regression test suite required before production cutover. Risk: DMS replication lag spike during high-write Friday evening period extending cutover window beyond 5 minutes. Mitigation: cutover window scheduled for Tuesday 02:00 UTC (lowest reservation volume); abort criteria if lag > 2 minutes at T-0. Risk: Cognito user pool migration losing existing sessions. Mitigation: rolling deployment with session graceful expiry — no forced logout. Tradeoff: Aurora Serverless v2 over provisioned instance accepted for cost efficiency at variable load; minimum 0.5 ACU prevents full cold start.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Aurora MySQL 8 Serverless v2 over RDS MySQL 8 provisioned for auto-scaling cost efficiency. Confirmed DMS CDC over mysqldump+restore for zero-downtime capability. Chosen Route53 weighted routing over DNS TTL-only approach for more controlled traffic shifting during cutover. Terraform chosen over CDK to match existing team IaC expertise.

quality_flags: technology_mismatch: security section references Aurora PostgreSQL in one sentence while contract specifies Aurora MySQL
compliance_gap: PCI-DSS scope assessment required by compliance_context but not addressed in security_and_compliance section
metadata.notes: First-pass migration architecture for restaurant reservation platform. Intentionally flawed with Aurora MySQL/PostgreSQL mismatch and missing PCI-DSS scope assessment.

project_goal.value: Deploy and manage a low-latency message broker network connecting kitchen display systems, order-taking tablets, and expeditor screens across a high-volume quick-service restaurant — ensuring sub-200ms order routing from cashier to cook stations with graceful device reconnection and failover
project_goal.rationale: Current serial order printing introduces 3-5 second delays and ticket loss on printer jams; digital KDS routing eliminates paper dependency and reduces order-to-kitchen latency

target_users.value: Restaurant operations engineers deploying and maintaining the KDS network; kitchen staff and cashiers as end-users of the connected devices
target_users.rationale: Engineering team owns infrastructure; kitchen staff interact via KDS devices not with infrastructure directly

project_class.rationale: Platform and networking infrastructure — Kubernetes, message broker, device connectivity — not a user-facing application

capabilities.rationale: Infrastructure deployment, Go message broker service, real-time MQTT order routing; no frontend application or database-heavy backend

complexity_level.rationale: Sub-200ms latency SLA, stateful device session management, Kubernetes on bare-metal, graceful failover under peak lunch rush load

risk_level.rationale: Infrastructure failure stops order flow and halts restaurant revenue; compromise of MQTT broker enables order manipulation

data_sensitivity.rationale: Order tickets are internal operational data; no PII, no payment data passes through this infrastructure

external_exposure.rationale: MQTT broker and Kubernetes cluster run on bare-metal in restaurant server room with no internet-facing ports

access_model.value: Custom JWT for MQTT client authentication; device certificates (mTLS) for KDS and tablet MQTT connections; Kubernetes RBAC for cluster administration; no public access
access_model.rationale: Device-level authentication prevents unauthorized MQTT clients from injecting or consuming order tickets

feature_scope.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes cluster deployment for 3-node restaurant server hardware, custom Go MQTT broker with routing rule engine, KDS device registry with heartbeat monitoring, order ticket routing from cashier topic to station-specific topics, reconnection queue with replay on device restore, expeditor screen alert on ticket gap exceeding threshold
feature_scope.rationale: Full infrastructure scope for KDS network deployment

mvp_scope.value: Single-node Kubernetes deploy, Go MQTT broker with static routing rules (cashier -> grill/fryer/salad stations), device heartbeat monitoring, reconnection queue for up to 60-second gaps, Grafana latency dashboard
mvp_scope.rationale: Single-node validates the latency SLA and device management patterns before multi-node HA deployment

security_baseline.value: MQTT client authentication via device JWT signed with restaurant-specific secret; TLS 1.3 on all MQTT connections; Kubernetes secrets for broker signing keys; no MQTT broker accessible from outside restaurant LAN
security_baseline.rationale: Order ticket manipulation via unauthorized MQTT publish is a revenue and operational integrity risk

frontend_stack.value: None — infrastructure project; Grafana for ops dashboards only
frontend_stack.rationale: No application UI; operational visibility via Grafana

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 for custom MQTT broker and routing engine; Kubernetes 1.30 on bare-metal with kubeadm
backend_stack.rationale: Go for low-latency broker; Kubernetes for container orchestration and rolling restarts on bare-metal hardware

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for device registry and routing rules persistence; Redis 7 for in-flight ticket queue and device session state
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for durable device and routing configuration; Redis for sub-millisecond in-flight queue operations

hosting_target.value: Bare-metal Kubernetes on 3x Intel NUC or equivalent restaurant server hardware; no cloud dependency
hosting_target.rationale: Restaurant POS networks are LAN-isolated; cloud connectivity not assumed; all infrastructure must run locally

privacy_retention_policy.value: Order tickets retained in Redis queue for 60-minute replay window; PostgreSQL device registry retained indefinitely; no personal customer data in ticket payloads
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: 60-minute replay sufficient for gap recovery; no retention obligations for anonymous order data

future_scope.value: Multi-node HA Kubernetes with etcd cluster, automatic failover between broker replicas, remote monitoring via VPN for multi-location fleet management
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 after single-node validates operational patterns

constraints.value: Sub-200ms end-to-end order routing latency; all infrastructure on restaurant LAN with no internet dependency; hardware budget of 3 commodity NUC-class servers per location; must survive single server failure in multi-node phase
constraints.rationale: Operational and hardware constraints for restaurant deployment environment

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus on local Kubernetes; MQTT broker metrics (connections, message rate, routing latency); device heartbeat dashboard; no cloud monitoring dependency
observability_baseline.rationale: Local observability required for restaurant LAN isolation; no cloud monitoring

execution_preference.value: Ansible playbooks for bare-metal provisioning; Helm charts for Kubernetes application deployment; GitHub Actions for CI
execution_preference.rationale: Ansible for repeatable bare-metal setup across restaurant locations; Helm for K8s application lifecycle

compliance_context.value: High-risk infrastructure: change management required for broker routing rule updates to prevent production order routing failures; incident post-mortem required for any event exceeding 200ms latency SLA during peak service hours
compliance_context.rationale: Risk_level high requires documented change management and incident response processes

project.goal: Deploy low-latency MQTT broker network on bare-metal Kubernetes for KDS order routing
project.target_users: Restaurant ops engineers and kitchen staff

backend.framework: Custom MQTT broker with CONNECT/PUBLISH/SUBSCRIBE handlers; routing rule engine
backend.integrations: PostgreSQL for device registry; Redis for in-flight queue

security.auth: Device JWT for MQTT client auth; mTLS for device-broker connections
security.network: LAN-only; no internet-facing ports; Kubernetes NetworkPolicy restricts MQTT to restaurant VLAN

data.db: PostgreSQL 16 for device registry and routing rules
data.cache: Redis 7 for in-flight ticket queue and session state

devops.environments: single-node dev/MVP, 3-node production

reasoner_reviews.product: The KDS network infrastructure addresses a real operational pain point. The 200ms SLA is achievable on LAN with a well-tuned Go broker, but the routing rule engine design is underspecified — specifically how routing rules map order item categories (grill items, fryer items, salad items) to station-specific MQTT topics. Static rules in config are fine for MVP but need a clear data model for the configurable phase.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Custom Go MQTT broker is the right choice given the latency SLA and LAN-only constraint — managed MQTT services like AWS IoT Core add cloud round-trip latency. The broker should use a topic hierarchy of 'kds/{restaurant_id}/{station_id}' so future multi-restaurant deployments share broker infrastructure. Redis sorted sets work well for the in-flight queue keyed by ticket_id with score as submission timestamp for TTL-based expiry.
reasoner_reviews.security: Device JWT authentication prevents rogue MQTT clients but the signing key rotation story is absent. If a KDS device is stolen or compromised, the restaurant must be able to revoke that device's token without rebooting the broker. A Redis-backed token revocation list checked on MQTT CONNECT is required. Also, Kubernetes NetworkPolicy must explicitly restrict MQTT port 1883/8883 to the restaurant VLAN CIDR — the plan should specify the NetworkPolicy manifest.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Three NUC-class servers (Intel NUC 12 or equivalent, 16 GB RAM, 256 GB SSD) are sufficient for single-location MVP. kubeadm on bare-metal requires static IP assignment and a L2 load balancer (MetalLB) for Kubernetes service exposure on the LAN. The plan should specify the MetalLB configuration for the MQTT service IP.

backend.service_design: KdsBrokerService (Go): custom MQTT v3.1.1 server handling CONNECT (JWT validation + device registration), PUBLISH (routing engine dispatch), SUBSCRIBE (station topic subscription). RoutingEngine: configurable rules mapping item_category ENUM to station topic; rules loaded from PostgreSQL routing_rules table on startup and on SIGHUP reload. DeviceRegistryService (Go): device heartbeat processor writing last_seen_at to device_health_checks table; detects offline devices when heartbeat gap >15 seconds.
backend.failure_handling: In-flight ticket queue: on PUBLISH to cashier/{restaurant_id}/orders topic, broker writes ticket_id to Redis LPUSH in-flight:{station_id} queue before routing to station topic. Station KDS device ACKs receipt via PUBLISH to kds/{restaurant_id}/{station_id}/ack — broker removes ticket from in-flight queue via LREM. On device reconnect (MQTT CONNECT), broker reads pending tickets from Redis in-flight:{station_id} and re-PUBLISHes in order. Reconnection replay window: 60 minutes (Redis key TTL on in-flight queue).
backend.scaling_notes: Single Go binary handles up to 500 concurrent MQTT connections per broker instance (validated in load test). Restaurant peak: 20-30 concurrent device connections. Single Kubernetes pod sufficient for MVP; future HA uses active-passive replica with Redis replication for session state failover.
backend.background_jobs: Heartbeat monitor goroutine: checks last_seen_at per device every 10 seconds; updates device_health_checks; triggers expeditor alert (PUBLISH to kds/{restaurant_id}/expeditor/alerts) when any station KDS offline >15 seconds. Ticket gap monitor: checks in-flight:{station_id} queue depth every 30 seconds; alerts expeditor if any station has >5 unacknowledged tickets.

security.auth_design: Device JWT: HS256 signed with per-restaurant secret stored in Kubernetes Secret; payload: {device_id, station_id, restaurant_id, role: 'kds'|'cashier'|'expeditor', iat, exp: 24h}. Validated on MQTT CONNECT; invalid JWT closes TCP connection. Redis revocation list: SET revoked:{jti} with TTL matching token exp; broker checks on CONNECT.
security.secrets_management: Restaurant MQTT signing key in Kubernetes Secret mounted as environment variable to KdsBrokerService pod. Key rotation: new key added to Kubernetes Secret, broker hot-reloads via SIGHUP, new device tokens issued with new key, old tokens expire within 24 hours. PostgreSQL credentials in Kubernetes Secret via Helm values.
security.security_risks: Risk: stolen KDS device used to inject fraudulent order tickets. Mitigation: JWT revocation list in Redis; device_id blacklisting via 'posaudit device revoke' CLI (future). Risk: MQTT broker pod restart drops in-flight tickets. Mitigation: Redis in-flight queue persists across pod restarts; reconnection replay restores pending tickets.

data.entities: kitchen_stations (station_id TEXT PK, restaurant_id TEXT FK, station_type ENUM grill/fryer/salad/beverage/expo, mqtt_topic TEXT, max_ticket_queue INT), display_devices (device_id TEXT PK, station_id FK, device_type ENUM kds/cashier_tablet/expeditor, firmware_version TEXT, last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM online/offline/reconnecting), order_tickets (ticket_id TEXT PK, restaurant_id TEXT, cashier_device_id FK, items_json JSONB, routed_stations TEXT[], submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM pending/routed/acked/completed), routing_rules (rule_id TEXT PK, restaurant_id FK, item_category TEXT, target_station_id FK, priority INT, is_active BOOL), device_health_checks (check_id TEXT PK, device_id FK, checked_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM healthy/offline, latency_ms INT), failover_events (event_id TEXT PK, device_id FK, disconnected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, reconnected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, tickets_replayed INT)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 for device registry and routing rules: kitchen_stations, display_devices, routing_rules, failover_events. Redis 7 for in-flight ticket queues (LPUSH/LREM per station), device session state (HSET device_session:{device_id}), and JWT revocation list (SET revoked:{jti}).
data.consistency_notes: Routing rule updates require broker SIGHUP reload to take effect; no hot rule updates to prevent mid-service routing disruption. In-flight queue uses Redis LPUSH/LREM atomic operations for ticket acknowledgment safety. PostgreSQL used for durable configuration only; no transactional order processing in PostgreSQL hot path.

devops.deployment_topology: Single-node MVP: 1x NUC-class server running kubeadm single-node cluster with KdsBrokerService pod, PostgreSQL pod, Redis pod, Prometheus+Grafana pod. Production: 3-node kubeadm cluster with MetalLB L2 load balancer exposing MQTT service on static LAN IP (e.g., 10.1.1.50:8883). KdsBrokerService deployed as Deployment with 1 replica (active-passive HA in phase 2).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go vet + golangci-lint, go test ./... race detector, Docker build + GitHub Container Registry push, Ansible playbook dry-run validation. Deployment: Helm chart with values per environment (single-node, 3-node). kubeadm node provisioning via Ansible playbook: OS hardening (Ubuntu 22.04 LTS), containerd, kubeadm init, MetalLB install, Helm install. Helm upgrade via Ansible post-provisioning.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping KdsBrokerService custom metrics (mqtt_connections_active, mqtt_publish_latency_ms histogram, routing_engine_rule_matches_total, in_flight_queue_depth per station). Grafana dashboards: order routing latency p50/p95/p99, in-flight queue depth heatmap by station, device online/offline status table, failover event timeline. Alertmanager: routing_latency_p99 > 200ms triggers PagerDuty; any station KDS offline > 15s triggers PagerDuty; in-flight queue depth > 10 for any station triggers Slack.
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback to previous release version. Pod restart restores Redis-persisted in-flight queue state. kubeadm upgrade requires manual node drain + cordon for bare-metal rolling upgrade. Ansible playbook idempotent for re-running failed provisioning steps.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing bare-metal Kubernetes infrastructure for a QSR kitchen display system order routing network. Key decisions: custom Go MQTT broker over managed MQTT services (eliminates cloud round-trip latency for sub-200ms SLA on LAN); Redis in-flight queue for reconnection replay (60-minute window); MetalLB for MQTT service LAN IP on bare-metal cluster; device JWT with Redis revocation list for token-level revocation without broker restart. This is a weak quality row with thin sections — several plan sections meet minimum word counts but lack deep specificity.
target_output.title: QSR Kitchen Order Routing Infrastructure
target_output.executive_summary: Bare-metal Kubernetes infrastructure deploying a custom Go MQTT broker network to connect kitchen display systems, cashier tablets, and expeditor screens in a quick-service restaurant. Order tickets route from cashier MQTT publish to station-specific topics within 200ms. Redis persists in-flight queues enabling reconnection replay for devices offline up to 60 minutes.
target_output.architecture_overview: Bare-metal Kubernetes cluster on 3 NUC-class restaurant servers running a custom Go MQTT broker service that handles device authentication via JWT, routes order tickets to kitchen station topics using configurable routing rules, and maintains Redis-backed in-flight queues for reconnection replay. MetalLB exposes the MQTT service on a static LAN IP. PostgreSQL stores device registry and routing rules. Prometheus and Grafana provide local latency monitoring. No cloud dependency — all infrastructure operates on restaurant LAN.
target_output.technology_stack: Broker: Go 1.22 custom MQTT v3.1.1 server. Orchestration: Kubernetes 1.30 via kubeadm on bare-metal Ubuntu 22.04 LTS. Load Balancer: MetalLB L2 mode for MQTT LAN IP. Queue: Redis 7 for in-flight tickets and JWT revocation. Database: PostgreSQL 16 for device registry. Provisioning: Ansible playbooks. Package management: Helm charts. CI: GitHub Actions. Monitoring: Prometheus + Grafana + Alertmanager.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Order ticket routing from cashier tablet PUBLISH to station KDS SUBSCRIBE via routing rules. Device authentication via HS256 JWT on MQTT CONNECT. In-flight queue with reconnection replay for station KDS devices. Device heartbeat monitoring with expeditor alert on KDS offline. Routing rule hot-reload via SIGHUP. JWT revocation list for compromised device tokens. Grafana latency dashboard with per-station routing latency percentiles.
target_output.system_components: KdsBrokerService (Go pod): MQTT v3.1.1 server, JWT validator, routing engine, in-flight queue manager. PostgreSQL pod: device registry, routing rules, failover events. Redis pod: in-flight queues per station, device session state, JWT revocation list. Prometheus pod: scrapes KdsBrokerService metrics endpoint. Grafana pod: routing latency and device health dashboards. MetalLB: L2 advertiser for MQTT service static LAN IP.
target_output.workflows: Order Ticket Routing: cashier tablet publishes to MQTT topic 'kds/{restaurant_id}/orders' with JSON payload containing ticket_id, items_json, and timestamp. KdsBrokerService RoutingEngine matches each item category against routing_rules table (loaded on startup). Broker writes ticket_id to Redis LPUSH in-flight:{station_id} for each matched station, then PUBLISHes to 'kds/{restaurant_id}/{station_id}/tickets'. Station KDS receives ticket and displays. KDS ACKs via PUBLISH to 'kds/{restaurant_id}/{station_id}/ack/{ticket_id}'; broker removes from Redis in-flight queue via LREM. Latency measured from cashier PUBLISH timestamp to station PUBLISH timestamp; logged as mqtt_publish_latency_ms metric. Device Reconnection Replay: KDS device reconnects after network interruption via MQTT CONNECT with valid JWT. Broker validates JWT against revocation list in Redis. Broker reads Redis LRANGE in-flight:{station_id} 0 -1, re-PUBLISHes each pending ticket to device in submission order. Expeditor screen receives PUBLISH to 'kds/{restaurant_id}/expeditor/alerts' if gap duration exceeded configurable threshold.
target_output.data_model: kitchen_stations (station_id TEXT PK, restaurant_id TEXT, station_type TEXT, mqtt_topic TEXT, max_queue INT). display_devices (device_id TEXT PK, station_id FK, device_type TEXT, last_seen_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status TEXT). order_tickets (ticket_id TEXT PK, restaurant_id TEXT, items_json JSONB, routed_stations TEXT[], submitted_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status TEXT). routing_rules (rule_id TEXT PK, restaurant_id TEXT, item_category TEXT, target_station_id FK, priority INT). device_health_checks (check_id TEXT PK, device_id FK, checked_at TIMESTAMPTZ, latency_ms INT). failover_events (event_id TEXT PK, device_id FK, disconnected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, reconnected_at TIMESTAMPTZ, tickets_replayed INT).
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API exposed to devices. MQTT topic interface: PUBLISH 'kds/{restaurant_id}/orders' (cashier tablets). SUBSCRIBE 'kds/{restaurant_id}/{station_id}/tickets' (station KDS). PUBLISH 'kds/{restaurant_id}/{station_id}/ack/{ticket_id}' (KDS acknowledgment). SUBSCRIBE 'kds/{restaurant_id}/expeditor/alerts' (expeditor screen). Internal management: GET /healthz (Kubernetes liveness probe). GET /metrics (Prometheus scrape endpoint). POST /admin/routing-rules/reload (trigger SIGHUP equivalent via HTTP for Helm hooks).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Device JWT HS256 signed with per-restaurant secret in Kubernetes Secret. JWT payload: device_id, station_id, restaurant_id, role, exp 24h. Validated on MQTT CONNECT. Redis revocation list SET revoked:{jti} checked on each CONNECT. TLS 1.3 on MQTT port 8883. Kubernetes NetworkPolicy restricts MQTT service to restaurant VLAN CIDR. Kubernetes RBAC for cluster admin access. Change management: routing rule updates require SIGHUP reload with 5-minute monitoring window post-reload.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Ansible playbook provisions bare-metal nodes: Ubuntu 22.04 LTS, containerd, kubeadm init (single-node MVP), MetalLB install with L2 advertisement for 10.1.1.50-10.1.1.60 pool, Helm install. Helm chart deploys KdsBrokerService, PostgreSQL (Bitnami chart), Redis (Bitnami chart), kube-prometheus-stack. GitHub Actions CI: golangci-lint, go test race detector, Docker build + GHCR push, Helm lint. Deployment via Ansible Helm upgrade task. Rollback: helm rollback kds-broker to prior release.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics from KdsBrokerService: mqtt_connections_active (gauge), mqtt_publish_latency_ms (histogram, buckets 10/50/100/200/500ms), in_flight_queue_depth (gauge per station_id label), jwt_revocation_checks_total. Grafana dashboards: routing latency p99 time series, queue depth per station, device online status table. Alertmanager rules: latency_p99 > 200ms and queue_depth > 10 and device_offline > 15s.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Hardware cost: 3x Intel NUC 12 (~$400 each) = $1,200 one-time per location. No ongoing cloud cost. Redis and PostgreSQL run on same Kubernetes cluster. Single Go broker pod handles 30 device connections comfortably. Phase 2 HA adds active-passive broker replica using Redis replication for session state.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): kubeadm single-node, Go MQTT broker with static routing rules, device JWT auth, Redis in-flight queue, reconnection replay, Prometheus + Grafana dashboards, Ansible provisioning playbook. Phase 2 (8 weeks): 3-node kubeadm with MetalLB, routing rule dynamic reload, JWT revocation list, expeditor alert system, Helm chart for multi-location deploy.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 with golangci-lint. Unit tests for routing engine rule matching and JWT validation. Integration test for MQTT CONNECT/PUBLISH/SUBSCRIBE flow using paho.mqtt.golang client against test broker instance. Ansible playbooks tested via molecule with Docker driver. Helm chart values schema validated in CI.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: single broker pod restart drops active MQTT connections causing 5-30 second reconnection gap during lunch rush. Mitigation: Redis in-flight queue preserves unacknowledged tickets; reconnect replay restores station queue. Phase 2 active-passive eliminates restart downtime. Risk: bare-metal hardware failure with no cloud failover. Mitigation: Phase 2 3-node cluster tolerates single-node failure. Risk: routing rule misconfiguration causing items to route to wrong station. Mitigation: SIGHUP reload with 5-minute monitoring window and automated Grafana alert on routing anomaly.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Custom Go broker chosen over EMQX to eliminate Java/Erlang runtime dependency on restaurant hardware. MetalLB L2 mode for MQTT LAN IP avoids need for external load balancer hardware. Redis chosen over PostgreSQL for in-flight queue for sub-millisecond LPUSH/LREM operations. JWT exp of 24 hours with revocation list provides balance between re-auth frequency and operational continuity.

project_goal.value: Python SDK providing a unified client interface for food service IoT sensor APIs — connected refrigeration units, fryer oil quality monitors, and dishwasher temperature probes — with device registry client, telemetry stream subscription, and alert threshold configuration
project_goal.rationale: Food service tech teams need a consistent programmatic interface across heterogeneous sensor hardware APIs to build automated monitoring pipelines

target_users.value: Food service technology DevOps engineers and backend developers building automated sensor monitoring pipelines and custom alerting integrations
target_users.rationale: Technical developers who consume the SDK to build upstream monitoring applications

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a Python package via PyPI; no hosted platform or UI — pure library SDK

capabilities.rationale: SDK abstracts sensor hardware API integrations, telemetry data schemas, and alert configuration — no frontend or auth system

complexity_level.rationale: Async telemetry stream subscription, alert threshold configuration with device-side acknowledgment, and unified device registry abstraction

risk_level.rationale: SDK handles operational IoT data; no financial or personal data; failures in consuming application, not the SDK itself

data_sensitivity.rationale: Telemetry data (temperatures, sensor readings) is internal operational data for food service equipment

external_exposure.rationale: SDK calls authenticated sensor vendor APIs using OAuth 2.0 credentials provided by the consuming application

access_model.value: OAuth 2.0 client credentials per sensor platform; consuming application provides credentials to SDK at initialization; SDK handles token refresh transparently
access_model.rationale: Each sensor vendor API uses OAuth 2.0 client credentials; SDK manages the credential lifecycle

feature_scope.value: Device registry client for device enumeration and metadata, async telemetry stream subscription via async iterator, alert threshold configuration with device acknowledgment and retry, unified sensor type abstractions for refrigeration/fryer/dishwasher
feature_scope.rationale: Core SDK feature set enabling monitoring pipeline development

mvp_scope.value: DeviceRegistryClient, TelemetryStream async iterator, AlertConfig client with acknowledgment retry — all supporting at least two sensor vendor backends
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum SDK surface area to enable a useful monitoring pipeline

security_baseline.value: OAuth 2.0 tokens stored in memory only (no disk persistence), TLS 1.2+ for all sensor API calls, no credential logging in SDK debug output
security_baseline.rationale: SDK security baseline for handling vendor API credentials in developer workstation and CI environments

frontend_stack.value: None — Python library SDK; no UI
frontend_stack.rationale: Library SDK; no browser or desktop UI

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with asyncio
backend_stack.rationale: Async I/O required for non-blocking telemetry stream consumption; Python 3.12 for latest asyncio improvements

data_platform.value: None — SDK does not manage its own data store; consuming applications own storage
data_platform.rationale: Library SDK; storage responsibility belongs to the consuming application

hosting_target.value: PyPI public package registry (foodsensor-sdk)
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Python package distribution via PyPI for developer install via pip

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK does not retain data; consuming application is responsible for sensor data retention policies. FDA FSMA temperature monitoring data retention requirements are the consumer's responsibility.
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SDK is a pass-through client; no data persistence in the SDK itself

future_scope.value: Additional sensor vendor backends, synchronous API variant for non-async consumers, Prometheus metrics exporter mixin
future_scope.rationale: Post-v1.0 feature extensions based on developer feedback

constraints.value: Zero required dependencies beyond httpx and Python standard library asyncio; Python 3.10+ compatibility; no private sensor vendor APIs — only publicly documented vendor API integrations
constraints.rationale: Minimal dependency footprint to avoid version conflicts in consuming applications; broad Python version compatibility

observability_baseline.value: Python standard logging module integration with DEBUG/INFO/WARNING levels; consuming application wires log handlers; Datadog integration in consuming monitoring pipeline (not in SDK itself)
observability_baseline.rationale: SDK uses standard logging; observability is the consuming application's concern

execution_preference.value: Semantic versioning; GitHub Actions for CI and PyPI release; ruff + mypy for code quality
execution_preference.rationale: Standard Python OSS development toolchain

project.goal: Python SDK for food service IoT sensor API integration
project.target_users: DevOps engineers building sensor monitoring pipelines

security.auth: OAuth 2.0 client credentials per vendor backend
security.tls: TLS 1.2+ for all vendor API calls

devops.distribution: PyPI via twine + trusted publisher OIDC

reasoner_reviews.product: The async iterator interface for TelemetryStream is the SDK's most important ergonomic decision. Developers building monitoring pipelines will use 'async for reading in stream.subscribe(device_id)' patterns — the SDK must handle reconnection, backpressure, and generator cleanup transparently. The AlertConfig acknowledgment retry is the second most important workflow to get right.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Python SDK architecture decisions: use Abstract Base Classes (ABCs) to define the vendor backend protocol that each sensor platform adapter must implement — this allows consuming applications to mock backends in tests. httpx async client is the correct minimal dependency for vendor API calls. OAuth 2.0 token refresh must be handled in a thread-safe async manner using asyncio.Lock to prevent concurrent refresh races. The unified sensor type abstraction should use dataclasses with type-annotated fields, not raw dicts.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth 2.0 tokens must never be logged at DEBUG level — mask access_token values in any SDK logging. Token refresh race condition (concurrent API calls triggering simultaneous refresh) must be prevented with asyncio.Lock per vendor credential instance. SDK must use httpx with verify=True (default TLS cert verification) and never allow consumers to set verify=False without a warning.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero non-standard dependencies beyond httpx is achievable. httpx has its own HTTP/2 and TLS support. No aiohttp dependency needed. Python 3.10 compatibility means using TypeAlias syntax only if guarded by TYPE_CHECKING.

backend.service_design: Package structure: foodsensor/core/ (base classes, auth, exceptions), foodsensor/devices/ (DeviceRegistryClient, DeviceMetadata dataclass), foodsensor/telemetry/ (TelemetryStream, SensorReading dataclass), foodsensor/alerts/ (AlertConfigClient, AlertThreshold dataclass), foodsensor/vendors/ (vendor adapter submodules: frigid_api/, fryeriq_api/, dischwash_api/).
backend.api_patterns: Public API surface: foodsensor.DeviceRegistryClient, foodsensor.TelemetryStream, foodsensor.AlertConfigClient, foodsensor.SensorType (Enum), foodsensor.exceptions (SensorAPIError, AuthenticationError, DeviceNotFoundError, AlertAcknowledgmentError). All public classes are ABC implementations; vendor adapters are not part of the public API surface.
backend.business_modules: VendorBackend ABC: abstract methods list_devices(), get_device_metadata(), stream_telemetry(device_id), set_alert_threshold(device_id, threshold), get_alert_acknowledgment(device_id). OAuth2ClientCredentialsAuth: asyncio.Lock-protected token refresh, in-memory token cache with expiry check before each request. SensorReading dataclass: device_id, sensor_type (SensorType enum), value FLOAT, unit STRING, timestamp DATETIME, quality_score FLOAT.
backend.failure_handling: SensorAPIError hierarchy: AuthenticationError (401, triggers token refresh retry once), DeviceNotFoundError (404), RateLimitError (429, with retry_after from header), VendorAPIUnavailableError (5xx, with retry guidance). TelemetryStream reconnection: exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, max 30s) on connection drop; consuming generator receives a StreamReconnectedEvent sentinel to detect gaps.

security.auth_design: OAuth2ClientCredentialsAuth class: stores client_id and client_secret in memory only (never written to disk or logged). asyncio.Lock prevents concurrent token refresh on simultaneous API calls. access_token masked as '***' in all SDK log messages at WARNING and below.
security.secrets_management: No secrets managed by SDK — consuming application provides credentials at SDK initialization. SDK documentation explicitly warns against passing credentials via environment variable interpolation in config files.
security.security_risks: Consuming application may log SDK objects containing credential references. Mitigation: OAuth2ClientCredentialsAuth implements __repr__ that returns '<OAuth2ClientCredentialsAuth client_id=...>' without exposing secret.

data.entities: DeviceMetadata (dataclass: device_id STRING, device_type SensorType ENUM refrigeration/fryer_oil/dishwasher, location_name STRING, vendor_backend STRING, firmware_version STRING, registered_at DATETIME). SensorReading (dataclass: device_id STRING, sensor_type SensorType, value FLOAT, unit STRING, timestamp DATETIME, quality_score FLOAT 0.0-1.0). AlertThreshold (dataclass: device_id STRING, metric SensorType, min_value FLOAT optional, max_value FLOAT optional, notification_channels LIST[STRING], acknowledged BOOL, acknowledged_at DATETIME optional). No database — SDK is stateless; consuming application owns all storage.
data.storage_design: No storage in SDK. In-memory only: OAuth token cache (Dict[str, TokenEntry] keyed by client_id), active TelemetryStream async generator registry for cleanup on context manager exit.
data.data_risks: Token cache grows unbounded if consuming application creates many OAuth2ClientCredentialsAuth instances. Mitigation: document recommendation to create one instance per vendor credential pair and reuse across SDK client instances.

devops.deployment_topology: Python package: src/ layout, pyproject.toml with [project] metadata and optional dependencies. Published to PyPI via GitHub Actions trusted publisher OIDC (no API token stored in GitHub secrets).
devops.environments: Development: local pytest-asyncio tests against mock vendor API (respx for httpx request mocking). CI: GitHub Actions matrix on Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12. Release: on git tag push, GitHub Actions publishes to PyPI via trusted publisher.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff check → mypy --strict → pytest --asyncio-mode=auto with coverage gate 90% → on tag: build wheel with python -m build → publish via PyPI trusted publisher OIDC.
devops.devops_risks: PyPI trusted publisher OIDC setup requires one-time configuration in PyPI project settings. Without it, an API token must be stored in GitHub secrets — less secure. Plan includes trusted publisher setup instructions in CONTRIBUTING.md.

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass SDK architecture for a food service IoT sensor Python library. Key decisions: ABC-based vendor backend protocol for testability and extensibility, asyncio-native TelemetryStream with reconnection handling, asyncio.Lock-protected OAuth 2.0 token refresh to prevent concurrent refresh races, and minimal httpx-only dependency footprint. The SDK is stateless — no storage layer. Plan quality is moderate: the architecture is sound but the api_design section is thinner than ideal for a library SDK (missing complete public API surface documentation), and the deployment section only covers PyPI release without addressing API stability / deprecation policy.
target_output.title: foodsensor Python SDK Architecture — v1.0
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.10+ SDK distributed via PyPI that provides a unified async client interface for food service IoT sensor vendor APIs, abstracting refrigeration temperature monitors, fryer oil quality sensors, and dishwasher temperature probes behind consistent DeviceRegistryClient, TelemetryStream, and AlertConfigClient interfaces. Vendor-specific adapters implement an ABC-defined backend protocol. OAuth 2.0 client credentials are managed in-memory with asyncio.Lock-protected token refresh. Minimal dependency footprint: httpx plus Python standard library.
target_output.architecture_overview: Python library package with src/ layout. Public API surface (DeviceRegistryClient, TelemetryStream, AlertConfigClient) delegates to vendor backend adapters that implement the VendorBackend ABC. Each vendor adapter (frigid_api, fryeriq_api, dischwash_api) handles vendor-specific API differences behind the common interface. OAuth2ClientCredentialsAuth handles token lifecycle with asyncio.Lock protecting concurrent refresh. TelemetryStream is an async context manager yielding SensorReading dataclasses via async generator; it handles vendor API disconnection with exponential backoff reconnection. No data persistence in the SDK — consuming applications own storage.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.10+ (3.12 recommended). HTTP client: httpx 0.27+ (async, TLS 1.2+, HTTP/2). Type annotations: Python typing + dataclasses. Async: asyncio (standard library). Testing: pytest, pytest-asyncio, respx (httpx mock). Code quality: ruff, mypy --strict. Build: python-build, wheel. Distribution: PyPI via trusted publisher OIDC. CI: GitHub Actions matrix (Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12). Versioning: semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH).
target_output.functional_feature_map: DeviceRegistryClient.list_devices(device_type=None): returns List[DeviceMetadata] for all registered devices; optional filter by SensorType enum. DeviceRegistryClient.get_device(device_id): returns DeviceMetadata for a specific device, raises DeviceNotFoundError if absent. TelemetryStream.subscribe(device_id): async generator yielding SensorReading dataclasses; handles reconnection transparently; consuming code uses 'async for reading in stream.subscribe(device_id)'. AlertConfigClient.set_threshold(device_id, threshold): pushes AlertThreshold to device, retries up to 3 times on non-acknowledgment, raises AlertAcknowledgmentError on exhaustion. AlertConfigClient.get_threshold(device_id): retrieves current AlertThreshold for a device.
target_output.system_components: foodsensor.core.auth (OAuth2ClientCredentialsAuth: asyncio.Lock token refresh, in-memory cache). foodsensor.core.exceptions (SensorAPIError hierarchy: AuthenticationError, DeviceNotFoundError, RateLimitError, VendorAPIUnavailableError, AlertAcknowledgmentError). foodsensor.core.base (VendorBackend ABC with abstract methods). foodsensor.devices (DeviceRegistryClient, DeviceMetadata dataclass). foodsensor.telemetry (TelemetryStream async context manager, SensorReading dataclass, StreamReconnectedEvent sentinel). foodsensor.alerts (AlertConfigClient, AlertThreshold dataclass). foodsensor.vendors.frigid_api (FrigidVendorBackend — refrigeration units). foodsensor.vendors.fryeriq_api (FryerIQVendorBackend — fryer oil quality). foodsensor.vendors.dischwash_api (DisCwashVendorBackend — dishwasher probes).
target_output.workflows: Device Registration and Telemetry Subscription: from foodsensor import DeviceRegistryClient, TelemetryStream, SensorType. client = DeviceRegistryClient(backend=FrigidVendorBackend(auth=OAuth2ClientCredentialsAuth(client_id, client_secret))). devices = await client.list_devices(device_type=SensorType.refrigeration). stream = TelemetryStream(backend=client.backend). async with stream: async for reading in stream.subscribe(devices[0].device_id): if reading.value > 5.0: trigger_alert(reading). Alert Threshold Configuration: alert_client = AlertConfigClient(backend=FryerIQVendorBackend(auth=auth)). threshold = AlertThreshold(device_id='fryer-42', metric=SensorType.fryer_oil, max_value=200.0, notification_channels=['slack://ops-channel']). await alert_client.set_threshold('fryer-42', threshold) — retries up to 3× with 2-second intervals until device acknowledges; raises AlertAcknowledgmentError if all retries exhausted.
target_output.data_model: DeviceMetadata (dataclass): device_id: str, device_type: SensorType, location_name: str, vendor_backend: str, firmware_version: str, registered_at: datetime. SensorType (Enum): refrigeration, fryer_oil, dishwasher_temp. SensorReading (dataclass): device_id: str, sensor_type: SensorType, value: float, unit: str, timestamp: datetime, quality_score: float (0.0-1.0; values below 0.8 indicate unreliable readings). AlertThreshold (dataclass): device_id: str, metric: SensorType, min_value: Optional[float], max_value: Optional[float], notification_channels: List[str], acknowledged: bool = False, acknowledged_at: Optional[datetime] = None. TokenEntry (internal dataclass): access_token: str, expires_at: datetime, client_id: str. All dataclasses are frozen=True for immutability in consuming application code.
target_output.api_design: Public module exports in foodsensor/__init__.py: DeviceRegistryClient, TelemetryStream, AlertConfigClient, OAuth2ClientCredentialsAuth, SensorType, DeviceMetadata, SensorReading, AlertThreshold, SensorAPIError (and subclasses). Vendor backends importable via foodsensor.vendors.frigid_api.FrigidVendorBackend etc. Typical consumer install: pip install foodsensor[frigid,fryeriq]. Optional extras declared in pyproject.toml for vendor-specific dependencies if any arise. Note: complete endpoint-level API documentation is not included in this plan — see vendor adapter source for mapping to underlying HTTP calls.
target_output.security_and_compliance: OAuth 2.0 tokens stored in asyncio.Lock-protected in-memory dict only; never written to disk, never logged. OAuth2ClientCredentialsAuth.__repr__ returns '<OAuth2ClientCredentialsAuth client_id={client_id}>' — secret never exposed in string representation. SDK enforces httpx default TLS verification (verify=True); any attempt to pass verify=False raises SDKSecurityWarning with explanation. All SDK log messages at level WARNING and below mask access_token values with '***'. No credential logging at DEBUG level regardless of log handler configuration. SDK documentation includes security note: do not pass credentials as CLI arguments in consuming application scripts.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: pyproject.toml build system: hatchling. GitHub Actions CI workflow: matrix strategy Python 3.10/3.11/3.12 → ruff check . → mypy --strict foodsensor/ → pytest --asyncio-mode=auto --cov=foodsensor --cov-fail-under=90. Release workflow: on push tag matching v*.*.* → python -m build → publish to PyPI via trusted publisher OIDC (no stored API token). Package name: foodsensor-sdk. Version: 0.1.0 for initial PyPI release targeting v1.0 after beta period. Changelog: CHANGELOG.md updated on each release. Deprecation policy: not specified in this plan (intentional thin section).
target_output.observability: SDK uses Python standard library logging module. Logger name: 'foodsensor'. Log events: INFO on device registry fetch, DEBUG on telemetry reading received (sensor_type, device_id, value, quality_score — never raw token), WARNING on vendor API retry attempt, ERROR on unrecoverable vendor API failure. Consuming applications configure log handlers and level. No SDK-level metrics export — consuming application is responsible for Datadog, Prometheus, or other metrics integration. TelemetryStream emits a StreamReconnectedEvent sentinel value in the async generator after reconnection so consuming applications can detect telemetry gaps.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost — pure Python library distributed via PyPI. GitHub Actions free tier sufficient for CI. PyPI free tier for package hosting. Scaling: SDK is stateless and instantiated per consuming process; no SDK-level scaling concerns. Consuming application is responsible for scaling its monitoring pipeline.
target_output.phased_implementation: v0.1.0 — MVP (6 weeks): DeviceRegistryClient, TelemetryStream, AlertConfigClient with VendorBackend ABC. Two vendor adapters: FrigidVendorBackend (refrigeration) and FryerIQVendorBackend (fryer oil). OAuth2ClientCredentialsAuth with asyncio.Lock. SensorReading and AlertThreshold dataclasses. pytest-asyncio suite with respx mocks at 90% coverage. PyPI release via trusted publisher. v0.2.0 (4 weeks post-v0.1): DisCwashVendorBackend (dishwasher probes). StreamReconnectedEvent sentinel. AlertAcknowledgmentError with retry configurable count. v1.0.0 (post-beta): API stability guarantee, deprecation policy, synchronous wrapper for non-async consumers.
target_output.development_guidelines: src/ layout with foodsensor/ package under src/. All public API classes and dataclasses fully type-annotated, passing mypy --strict. No mutable default arguments in dataclasses (use field(default_factory=...)). VendorBackend adapters must not import each other. No global state in SDK — all state in instances. respx mock fixtures in tests/conftest.py for each vendor backend. Changelog entry required for every PR modifying public API. ruff enforces import sorting and no unused imports.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Vendor API breaking changes invalidate vendor adapter implementations. Mitigation: integration tests against vendor API sandboxes in CI; vendor API version pinning via Accept header where supported. Risk: asyncio.Lock token refresh serializes all concurrent API calls while a refresh is in progress. Mitigation: token expiry checked before each request with a 30-second buffer — refresh triggered early to minimize lock contention during burst traffic. Tradeoff: async-only API accepted for v1.0 to keep the implementation clean; synchronous wrapper deferred to v1.1 based on consumer demand.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected httpx over aiohttp to minimize dependencies and leverage httpx's synchronous/async API parity for future sync wrapper. Chosen VendorBackend ABC over Protocol for explicit implementation documentation in vendor adapter code. PyPI trusted publisher OIDC selected over stored API token for improved release security. Frozen dataclasses used for SensorReading and AlertThreshold to prevent accidental mutation in consuming application code.

quality_flags: thin_section: api_design lacks complete HTTP endpoint mapping for vendor adapters
thin_section: deployment_operations missing deprecation and API stability policy
metadata.notes: Python IoT sensor SDK for food service equipment with async telemetry subscription and OAuth 2.0 credential management. Moderate quality due to thin api_design and missing deprecation policy.

project_goal.value: Migrate restaurant group's centralized menu management from a monolithic Rails API to a standalone Go microservice exposing menu item definitions, pricing rules, nutritional data, and allergen information to POS terminals, digital menu boards, mobile apps, and third-party delivery platform integrations
project_goal.rationale: Rails monolith has reached throughput and deployment agility limits as delivery platform integrations scale

target_users.value: POS terminal systems, digital menu board controllers, mobile app backends, and third-party delivery platform (DoorDash, Uber Eats, Grubhub) integration services
target_users.rationale: Machine-to-machine API consumers with distinct latency and update frequency requirements

project_class.rationale: Standalone Go microservice with no frontend; all consumers are API clients

capabilities.rationale: REST API, PostgreSQL persistence, OAuth 2.0 auth, public API for delivery platforms, usage analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-consumer API with different cache TTLs, webhook delivery to 3 delivery platforms, FDA calorie disclosure compliance

risk_level.rationale: Internal business data; no health or financial risk, but delivery platform SLAs create operational risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: Menu pricing and nutritional data are proprietary business data, not customer personal data

external_exposure.rationale: Delivery platform integrations use OAuth 2.0 client credentials; POS terminals use API keys

access_model.value: OAuth 2.0 client credentials for delivery platforms, API keys for POS terminals and menu boards, internal service-to-service JWT for mobile app backends
access_model.rationale: Different auth models for different consumer types with appropriate scope enforcement

feature_scope.value: Menu item CRUD, pricing rule management, nutritional fact storage, allergen declaration management, item availability toggling, Redis cache invalidation on item changes, webhook delivery to delivery platforms, FDA-compliant calorie disclosure in API responses
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for menu management microservice

mvp_scope.value: Menu item read API for POS and digital boards with Redis cache, item availability toggle, Go microservice replacing Rails menu endpoints
mvp_scope.rationale: Core read path for POS and boards is the highest-volume migration priority

security_baseline.value: OAuth 2.0 client credentials with scope enforcement, API key hashing for POS terminals, TLS 1.3, mTLS for delivery platform webhooks
security_baseline.rationale: partner_facing exposure with mTLS required for delivery platform webhook delivery

frontend_stack.value: None — API service only
frontend_stack.rationale: api_service project class with no frontend component

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with Gin on ECS Fargate
backend_stack.rationale: Go performance for high-volume POS read traffic; Gin for routing

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for menu data, Redis 7 for menu cache with pub/sub invalidation
data_platform.rationale: Relational data for menu item hierarchy; Redis for sub-30s cache invalidation across consumers

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1 with RDS PostgreSQL
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers consistent with existing AWS infrastructure

privacy_retention_policy.value: Menu data retained indefinitely (product catalog); API usage logs retained 90 days; webhook delivery logs retained 30 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No personal data in menu management; audit trail for delivery platform SLA compliance

future_scope.value: GraphQL API layer for mobile app consumers, A/B pricing rule testing, multi-region menu replication for international locations
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 advanced menu management and international expansion

constraints.value: Menu changes must propagate to all consumer systems within 30 seconds; FDA calorie disclosure required in all menu API responses for chains with 20+ locations; Rails API must remain live in parallel during migration
constraints.rationale: POS display latency SLA, regulatory requirement, and zero-downtime migration

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with trace sampling, custom metrics for cache hit rate and webhook delivery success rate, PagerDuty alerts on delivery platform webhook failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Need visibility into cache health and delivery platform SLA compliance

execution_preference.value: Traffic shadow and gradual cutover: route 10% of POS traffic to Go service, validate metrics, increase to 100% over 4 weeks
execution_preference.rationale: Low-risk migration approach for high-volume POS read path

compliance_context.value: FDA menu labeling law (21 CFR 101.11) requiring calorie disclosure in standard format for chains with 20+ locations; allergen declaration regulations (Top 9 allergens must be identified in menu API responses)
compliance_context.rationale: Federal regulatory requirements for menu data APIs serving consumer-facing display systems

project.goal: Go microservice replacing Rails monolith menu API with Redis cache and delivery platform webhooks
project.target_users: POS systems, menu boards, mobile backends, delivery platforms
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks, full migration in 20 weeks

security.auth: OAuth 2.0 client credentials (delivery platforms), API keys hashed with bcrypt (POS/boards), mTLS for webhook delivery

data.cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache with pub/sub for invalidation

devops.environments: dev, staging, production with traffic weighting at ALB

constraints.fda_compliance: Calorie disclosure in all menu responses

reasoner_reviews.product: The 30-second propagation SLA is the core operational contract. Redis pub/sub invalidation from the write path to all consuming cache readers is the mechanism. Digital menu boards and POS systems poll on a configurable TTL; when a menu item's cache is invalidated, the next poll gets the fresh data from PostgreSQL. Delivery platform webhooks are a push mechanism — platforms register webhook endpoints and receive availability change events within the SLA window.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go Gin is the right choice for the POS read path — low latency, high concurrency, minimal memory footprint. The cache invalidation architecture should use Redis PUBLISH on menu item write, with a subscriber goroutine per service instance that clears the relevant Redis cache keys. OAuth 2.0 client credentials for delivery platforms must enforce scope: menu:read for GET operations, menu:webhook for webhook registration.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS for webhook delivery to delivery platforms is required given partner_facing exposure — DoorDash and Uber Eats expect mutual certificate authentication on webhook endpoints. The Go webhook sender must present a client certificate signed by the restaurant group's CA, and delivery platform webhooks must verify the signature. API keys for POS terminals stored as bcrypt hashes — never in plaintext in PostgreSQL.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: FDA 21 CFR 101.11 calorie disclosure: the menu API response must include calories_per_serving as a non-null, non-zero field for all menu items. The Go handler must validate this at response serialization time and return a data quality alert flag in the response if any item is missing calorie data. Allergen Top 9 must be a structured field, not free text, to enable consumer rendering.

backend.service_design: Go Gin application with route groups: /v1/menu for item CRUD and availability, /v1/webhooks for delivery platform registration, /v1/admin for pricing and nutritional data management. Redis subscriber goroutine runs per service instance, listening to menu-invalidation pub/sub channel.
backend.api_patterns: GET /v1/menu/items?location_id=X returns full menu with pricing, nutrition, allergens, and calorie disclosure. PATCH /v1/menu/items/{id}/availability {available: bool} triggers Redis PUBLISH and webhook fan-out. POST /v1/webhooks/register for delivery platform webhook endpoint registration with OAuth 2.0 client_credentials scope.
backend.scaling_notes: ECS Fargate auto-scale on CPU 60% target; Redis ElastiCache cluster mode for cache at scale; read replica on RDS for nutritional data queries that bypass cache

security.auth_design: OAuth 2.0 client credentials: delivery platforms exchange client_id/client_secret for bearer token with scope enforcement in Gin middleware. API keys: POS terminals and menu boards use Authorization: ApiKey {key}; key stored as bcrypt hash in api_keys table. mTLS: webhook sender presents leaf certificate from restaurant group CA; delivery platform verifies.
security.secrets_management: PostgreSQL credentials, Redis URL, OAuth 2.0 client secrets stored in AWS Secrets Manager; injected via ECS task role at runtime
security.security_risks: mTLS certificate rotation must be planned — expired leaf certificates will break webhook delivery to delivery platforms without warning

data.entities: menu_items (id, location_id, name, description, category_id, base_price DECIMAL, calories_per_serving INT NOT NULL, available BOOL, created_at, updated_at), item_variants (id, menu_item_id, variant_name, price_delta DECIMAL, calories_delta INT), pricing_rules (id, menu_item_id, rule_type ENUM fixed/percentage/time_based, value DECIMAL, conditions_json), nutritional_facts (id, menu_item_id, total_fat_g, saturated_fat_g, sodium_mg, total_carbs_g, dietary_fiber_g, total_sugars_g, protein_g), allergen_declarations (id, menu_item_id, allergen ENUM wheat/milk/eggs/fish/shellfish/tree_nuts/peanuts/soybeans/sesame, present BOOL, may_contain BOOL), menu_categories (id, location_id, name, display_order, active), consumer_channels (id, name, auth_type ENUM oauth/apikey, webhook_url NULLABLE, webhook_cert_thumbprint NULLABLE)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for all menu data; Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster for menu item cache keyed as menu:item:{item_id}:v{cache_version}, with Redis pub/sub channel menu-invalidation for cache clearing

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster: Go menu service (min 2, max 8 tasks, CPU 60% scale-out), RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ, Redis ElastiCache r7g.medium cluster mode (3 shards). ALB with weighted target groups for traffic shadowing during Rails migration (10/90 → 50/50 → 100/0).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: go test ./... with race detection, Docker build, push to ECR, ECS rolling update. ALB traffic weight managed via AWS CLI in pipeline for gradual migration steps. golang-migrate runs at container startup.
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS rolling update maintains 50% minimum healthy; ALB traffic weight shift back to Rails service in <5 minutes if error rate spike detected

best_plan.title: Restaurant Menu Management API — Round 2 Architecture
best_plan.executive_summary: Go Gin microservice on ECS Fargate replacing Rails menu API with Redis cache invalidation and delivery platform webhooks.
best_plan.architecture_overview: Go Gin, PostgreSQL 16, Redis pub/sub invalidation, OAuth 2.0, mTLS webhooks, ALB traffic weighting.
